## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
> Define the term **epoch** in the context of training an artificial neural network. Distinguish between **batch**, **mini-batch**, and **stochastic** gradient descent with respect to update frequency and convergence behaviour.

**Model Answer:**

> An **epoch** is defined as **one complete forward + backward pass of the entire training dataset** through the neural network. If the dataset contains $N$ samples, one epoch corresponds to $N$ prediction–loss–update cycles aggregated appropriately.
>
> - **Batch Gradient Descent**: Uses **all $N$ training samples** to compute the gradient for **a single update**. Smooth convergence, but computationally expensive and memory-intensive.
> - **Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)**: Uses **one sample** per update. Updates are noisy, leading to oscillatory but fast convergence.
> - **Mini-Batch Gradient Descent**: Uses a small batch of size $B$ (typically $32$–$256$) per update. Strikes a balance — fast like SGD, stable like batch GD. This is the de-facto standard in modern deep learning.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
> What is the **learning rate** in gradient descent? Explain what happens to training when $\eta$ is set too high or too low.

**Model Answer:**

> The **learning rate $\eta$** is a hyperparameter that controls the **step size** taken in the direction opposite to the gradient during each parameter update: $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta\,\nabla_{\theta}\mathcal{L}$.
>
> - **Too high ($\eta \gg 0.1$)**: The optimizer **overshoots** the minimum. The loss may diverge or oscillate wildly. The network fails to converge.
> - **Too low ($\eta \ll 10^{-5}$)**: Convergence is **extremely slow**, may get stuck in poor local minima, and the model may appear to "not learn".
> - **Optimal $\eta$** yields smooth, monotonic loss decrease. Adaptive optimizers like **Adam** automatically tune $\eta$ per parameter.
>
> Practical remedies include **learning-rate schedulers**, **warm-up**, and **cyclical learning rates**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain in detail the **seven sequential steps involved in training an artificial neural network**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The training of an ANN proceeds through the following structured pipeline:

1. **Data Collection and Preprocessing** — Acquire labeled dataset $\mathcal{D}$, normalize inputs to zero mean and unit variance, one-hot encode labels, and split into train/validation/test sets.

2. **Network Architecture Definition** — Choose the number of layers $L$, neurons per layer $n_l$, activation functions $\sigma_l$, and loss function $\mathcal{L}$.

3. **Parameter Initialization** — Initialize $\mathbf{W}^{[l]}$ using He or Xavier scheme and $\mathbf{b}^{[l]}$ to zero, ensuring stable signal propagation.

4. **Forward Propagation** — For each layer compute $\mathbf{z}^{[l]} = \mathbf{W}^{[l]}\mathbf{a}^{[l-1]} + \mathbf{b}^{[l]}$ followed by $\mathbf{a}^{[l]} = \sigma(\mathbf{z}^{[l]})$, terminating in the prediction $\hat{\mathbf{y}}$.

5. **Loss Computation** — Quantify the prediction error using MSE (regression), BCE (binary), or CCE (multi-class).

6. **Backpropagation and Gradient Computation** — Apply the chain rule recursively from the output layer to compute $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial \mathbf{W}^{[l]}$ and $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial \mathbf{b}^{[l]}$ for every layer.

7. **Parameter Update and Iteration** — Apply gradient descent $\mathbf{W}^{[l]}_{t+1} = \mathbf{W}^{[l]}_t - \eta\,\nabla_{\mathbf{W}^{[l]}}\mathcal{L}$, monitor validation loss, and stop when convergence criteria are met.

**Block Diagram:** Refer to **Section 4.1** above for the end-to-end pipeline diagram. (Award full marks for a labelled diagram showing Input → Forward → Loss → Backward → Update loop.)

---

**(b)** Derive the **backpropagation equations** for a 2-layer feed-forward network with sigmoid activations and MSE loss. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Let $\mathbf{x}$ be the input, $\mathbf{W}^{[1]}, \mathbf{b}^{[1]}$ the hidden-layer parameters, $\mathbf{W}^{[2]}, b^{[2]}$ the output-layer parameters.

**Forward Pass:**

$$\mathbf{z}^{[1]} = \mathbf{W}^{[1]}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^{[1]}, \quad \mathbf{a}^{[1]} = \sigma(\mathbf{z}^{[1]})$$

$$z^{[2]} = \mathbf{W}^{[2]}\mathbf{a}^{[1]} + b^{[2]}, \quad \hat{y} = \sigma(z^{[2]})$$

**Loss:**

$$\mathcal{L} = \tfrac{1}{2}(\hat{y} - y)^2$$

**Output Error Signal:**

$$\delta^{[2]} = \frac{\partial\mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{[2]}} = (\hat{y} - y)\,\hat{y}(1-\hat{y})$$

**Hidden Error Signal:**

$$\boldsymbol{\delta}^{[1]} = \big((\mathbf{W}^{[2]})^{\top}\delta^{[2]}\big)\odot \mathbf{a}^{[1]}\odot(\mathbf{1}-\mathbf{a}^{[1]})$$

**Gradients:**

$$\frac{\partial\mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{[2]}} = \delta^{[2]}\,(\mathbf{a}^{[1]})^{\top}, \quad \frac{\partial\mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{[1]}} = \boldsymbol{\delta}^{[1]}\,\mathbf{x}^{\top}$$

**Update Rule:**

$$\mathbf{W}^{[l]}_{\text{new}} = \mathbf{W}^{[l]}_{\text{old}} - \eta\,\frac{\partial\mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{[l]}}$$

This derivation is the foundational mathematics behind every deep network training procedure.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss in detail the different **loss functions** used while training neural networks. How does the choice of loss function impact the training dynamics? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A **loss function** $\mathcal{L}(\hat{y}, y)$ quantifies the divergence between the network's prediction and the ground truth. The choice of loss is task-dependent:

- **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** — Used for regression. Penalizes large errors quadratically. Sensitive to outliers. Mathematically: $\mathcal{L}_{\text{MSE}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum (\hat{y} - y)^2$.

- **Mean Absolute Error (MAE)** — Used when robustness to outliers is required: $\mathcal{L}_{\text{MAE}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum \vert \hat{y} - y \vert$.

- **Binary Cross-Entropy (BCE)** — Used for binary classification. Paired with sigmoid output. Penalizes confident wrong predictions heavily.

- **Categorical Cross-Entropy (CCE)** — Used for multi-class classification with softmax outputs.

- **Hinge Loss** — Used in SVM-style maximum-margin classifiers.

- **KL Divergence** — Used when matching probability distributions (e.g., in Variational Autoencoders and knowledge distillation).

- **Adversarial Loss** — Used in GANs to pit generator against discriminator.

**Impact on Training Dynamics:**
1. The **gradient magnitude** depends directly on the loss function. MSE gradients shrink near the optimum, while BCE provides strong, non-vanishing gradients.
2. **Numerical stability** — softmax + log combinations require the *log-sum-exp* trick to avoid NaN.
3. **Convergence speed** — well-matched loss–activation pairs (softmax + CCE) converge dramatically faster than mismatched ones (sigmoid + MSE for classification).
4. **Robustness** — MAE is more outlier-robust than MSE.

---

**(b)** Explain the **vanishing and exploding gradient problem** in deep neural networks. Discuss standard techniques to mitigate it. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

When backpropagating through many layers, gradients are multiplied by the Jacobian $\mathbf{W}^{[l]\top}\text{diag}(\sigma'(\mathbf{z}^{[l]}))$ at each layer.

- **Vanishing Gradient**: When $\sigma'(z)$ is small (sigmoid saturates near 0/1) or $\Vert\mathbf{W}\Vert < 1$, the gradient shrinks **exponentially** with depth. Early layers stop updating — the network fails to learn.

- **Exploding Gradient**: When $\Vert\mathbf{W}\Vert > 1$, the gradient grows **exponentially** with depth, causing parameter updates so large that training diverges (loss = NaN).

**Mitigation Techniques:**

1. **Better Activation Functions**: Replace sigmoid/tanh with **ReLU**, **Leaky ReLU**, or **GELU** whose gradients do not saturate for positive inputs.

2. **Careful Initialization**: **Xavier** and **He** initialization keep the variance of activations stable across layers.

3. **Batch Normalization**: Normalizes activations within a mini-batch, keeping gradient magnitudes controlled.

4. **Gradient Clipping**: Caps gradient norm: $\nabla\mathcal{L} \leftarrow \nabla\mathcal{L} \cdot \dfrac{\tau}{\Vert\nabla\mathcal{L}\Vert}$ if $\Vert\nabla\mathcal{L}\Vert > \tau$.

5. **Residual Connections (ResNets)**: Skip connections allow gradients to flow unimpeded through identity mappings: $\mathbf{a}^{[l+1]} = \sigma(\mathbf{z}^{[l+1]} + \mathbf{a}^{[l]})$.

6. **LSTM/GRU Gates**: In RNNs, gating mechanisms control gradient flow through time.

7. **Optimizer Choice**: Adaptive optimizers (Adam, RMSprop) scale updates per-parameter, partially absorbing gradient pathologies.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Examinations:**
>
> 1. **Confusing forward and backward pass order** — Students often write the weight update *before* backpropagation. Strictly: Forward $\rightarrow$ Loss $\rightarrow$ Backward $\rightarrow$ Update.
> 2. **Forgetting the activation derivative in $\delta$ computation** — Examiner expects the explicit $\sigma'(z^{[l]}) = \sigma(z^{[l]})(1-\sigma(z^{[l]}))$ term. Omitting it = **−2 marks**.
> 3. **Mismatching loss and activation** — Using sigmoid + MSE in a classification question is a common trap. Always justify.
> 4. **Not mentioning bias updates** — Biases are also learnable parameters; failing to write $b^{[l]}_{\text{new}} = b^{[l]} - \eta\,\partial\mathcal{L}/\partial b^{[l]}$ loses marks.
> 5. **No mention of learning rate effect** — In any training-related 14-mark answer, **you must discuss $\eta$**. A generic answer without it loses at least **2 marks**.
> 6. **Skipping the diagram** — KTU mandates a neat block diagram for any architecture/process question. Always include one.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Steps in Training an ANN**

- ☐ **Define** training as empirical risk minimization over parameters $\boldsymbol{\theta}$.
- ☐ **Memorize the pipeline**: Preprocess → Initialize → Forward → Loss → Backward → Update → Repeat.
- ☐ **Recall the forward equations**: $\mathbf{z}^{[l]} = \mathbf{W}^{[l]}\mathbf{a}^{[l-1]} + \mathbf{b}^{[l]}$, $\mathbf{a}^{[l]} = \sigma(\mathbf{z}^{[l]})$.
- ☐ **Master the backprop equations**: $\delta^{[L]} = \nabla_{\mathbf{a}}\mathcal{L} \odot \sigma'(\mathbf{z}^{[L]})$, recursive propagation for hidden layers.
- ☐ **Know all major loss functions**: MSE, BCE, CCE, MAE, Hinge, KL-divergence.
- ☐ **Know the update rule**: $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta\,\nabla_{\theta}\mathcal{L}$.
- ☐ **Differentiate Batch / Mini-batch / SGD** by sample size per update.
- ☐ **Explain learning rate effects**: too high → divergence; too low → slow convergence.
- ☐ **State vanishing and exploding gradient** problems with mitigation strategies.
- ☐ **Mention initialization schemes**: Xavier (tanh/sigmoid), He (ReLU).
- ☐ **Mention regularization techniques**: dropout, L2 weight decay, early stopping.
- ☐ **Draw the training loop block diagram** in any 7+ mark question.
- ☐ **Connect to Generative AI**: GANs, VAEs, and transformers all share this same training backbone.

> **One-line Takeaway:** *Training an ANN is the disciplined, gradient-driven, iterative refinement of weights and biases to minimize a chosen loss function — a single algorithmic loop that powers every modern AI system from image classifiers to generative chatbots.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parameters and Hyperparameters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT867 - GENERATIVE AI
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Parameters and Hyperparameters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Generative AI** and deep learning systems, the terms *parameters* and *hyperparameters* refer to two fundamentally distinct categories of variables that govern the behavior and learning capability of a model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Parameters** are the **internal configuration variables** of a model whose values are **automatically estimated (learned) directly from the training data** through the optimization process (typically via gradient descent and backpropagation). Examples include weights ($w$), biases ($b$), activations, and learned embeddings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hyperparameters** are the **external configuration variables** whose values **cannot be learned from the training data** and must be **explicitly set by the practitioner (engineer/researcher)** *before* the training loop begins. Examples include learning rate ($\eta$), batch size ($B$), number of epochs ($E$), number of hidden layers ($L$), and optimizer-specific decay factors.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **chess grandmaster teaching a student to play chess**.

- The **Parameters** are like the *strength of neural connections inside the student's brain*. As the student practices thousands of games, these internal strengths automatically adjust based on which moves led to wins and which led to losses. The teacher does not manually set them; they evolve through **experience (data)**.

- The **Hyperparameters** are like the **rules and constraints of the practice schedule** set by the coach *before* training begins:
  - *How many hours per day should the student practice?* → **Epochs**
  - *How many games per practice session?* → **Batch Size**
  - *How aggressively should feedback be applied after each loss?* → **Learning Rate**
  - *How many practice rounds before evaluating progress?* → **Number of Layers / Iterations**

The coach (engineer) chooses the schedule (hyperparameters). The student's brain (model) figures out its own internal wiring (parameters) through the practice (training process).

## 1.3 Canonical Examples in Generative AI

| Model Family | Typical Parameters | Typical Hyperparameters |
|---|---|---|
| **GAN (Generative Adversarial Network)** | Generator weights, Discriminator weights | Learning rate $\eta$, latent dim $z$, batch size $B$ |
| **VAE (Variational Autoencoder)** | Encoder/Decoder weights, $\mu$, $\sigma$ | KL-divergence weight $\beta$, latent dim $z$ |
| **Diffusion Models** | UNet denoiser weights | Noise schedule $\beta_t$, number of timesteps $T$ |
| **Transformer (GPT-style)** | Attention weights, FFN weights | Number of heads $h$, context length $L$, temperature $\tau$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric to Remember:** A common order-of-magnitude for a transformer-based LLM is approximately **$\mathbf{10^{9}}$ to $\mathbf{10^{12}}$ parameters**, while a typical hyperparameter count remains on the order of **$\mathbf{10^{0}}$ to $\mathbf{10^{2}}$** values to be tuned.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The "Why" and "How"

### Step 1: Initialization of Parameters
- **Why:** Neural networks require initial values for $w$ and $b$ so that the forward pass can produce a meaningful (though initially random) output.
- **How:** Common schemes include Xavier (Glorot) initialization and He initialization, which scale variance based on layer fan-in to prevent vanishing/exploding gradients.

$$W^{[l]} \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(0,\ \frac{2}{n^{[l-1]}}\right) \quad \text{(He Initialization for ReLU)}$$

### Step 2: Hyperparameter Specification (Before Training)
- **Why:** The architecture and learning dynamics are not derivable from the data distribution alone; they must be set a priori.
- **How:** The engineer defines a hyperparameter **search space** $\mathcal{H}$ and uses a search strategy (grid, random, Bayesian) to find an optimal configuration $h^{*} \in \mathcal{H}$.

### Step 3: Forward Pass & Loss Computation
- **Why:** To measure how far the current parameters are from producing correct outputs.
- **How:** Compute the loss function $\mathcal{L}(\theta; h)$ where $\theta$ denotes parameters and $h$ denotes hyperparameters.

### Step 4: Backpropagation & Parameter Update
- **Why:** Parameters must be updated to minimize $\mathcal{L}$.
- **How:** Apply the gradient descent rule:

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_{t} - \eta \cdot \nabla_{\theta}\,\mathcal{L}(\theta_{t})$$

where $\eta$ (a **hyperparameter**) is the step size, and $\nabla_{\theta}\mathcal{L}$ is computed via the chain rule.

### Step 5: Hyperparameter Tuning Loop (Outer Loop)
- **Why:** No single $\eta$, $B$, or $E$ is universally optimal; tuning maximizes generalization.
- **How:** Repeat Steps 1–4 across multiple $(h_1, h_2, \ldots, h_k)$ configurations and select the one minimizing the **validation loss** $\mathcal{L}_{val}$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition | Typical Range / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $\theta$ | Parameter vector (weights + biases) | $\mathbb{R}^{n}$, $n$ can be $\sim 10^{9}\text{–}10^{12}$ |
| $h$ | Hyperparameter configuration vector | $\mathbb{R}^{k}$, $k$ small ($\le 20$ typically) |
| $\eta$ | Learning rate | $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-1}$ |
| $B$ | Batch size | $1$ to $4096$ |
| $E$ | Number of epochs | $1$ to $1000$ |
| $\lambda$ | Regularization coefficient ($\ell_2$ weight decay) | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-1}$ |
| $\beta_1, \beta_2$ | Adam optimizer moment decay rates | $\beta_1 \approx 0.9$, $\beta_2 \approx 0.999$ |
| $z$ | Latent dimension (VAE/GAN) | $2$ to $1024$ |
| $T$ | Number of diffusion timesteps | $50$ to $4000$ |
| $\tau$ | Sampling temperature (LLMs) | $0.0$ to $2.0$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In Generative AI pipelines (LLMs, Stable Diffusion, GANs), the difference between a model that converges to high-quality outputs and one that produces noise often comes down to **hyperparameter selection**, not parameter count. Hyperparameter tuning is therefore a core competency in any MLOps / GenAI engineering role.

## 2.3 Parameter Counting Formula

For a fully-connected layer with input dimension $n_{in}$ and output dimension $n_{out}$:

$$P_{layer} = (n_{in} \times n_{out}) + n_{out}$$

The total parameters for a deep network with $L$ layers:

$$P_{total} = \sum_{l=1}^{L}\left[(n^{[l-1]} \times n^{[l]}) + n^{[l]}\right]$$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Total Parameters in a Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP)

Consider an MLP with the following architecture (a common baseline for generative models such as the Generator in a GAN):

- Input layer: $n^{[0]} = 784$ (e.g., flattened MNIST image)
- Hidden layer 1: $n^{[1]} = 256$ neurons, activation ReLU
- Hidden layer 2: $n^{[2]} = 128$ neurons, activation ReLU
- Output layer: $n^{[3]} = 10$ neurons (or $784$ for image generation)

**Step 1: Compute parameters of Layer 1 (Input → Hidden 1)**

$$P^{[1]} = (n^{[0]} \times n^{[1]}) + n^{[1]}$$

$$P^{[1]} = (784 \times 256) + 256$$

$$P^{[1]} = 200704 + 256 = 200960$$

**Step 2: Compute parameters of Layer 2 (Hidden 1 → Hidden 2)**

$$P^{[2]} = (n^{[1]} \times n^{[2]}) + n^{[2]}$$

$$P^{[2]} = (256 \times 128) + 128$$

$$P^{[2]} = 32768 + 128 = 32896$$

**Step 3: Compute parameters of Layer 3 (Hidden 2 → Output)**

$$P^{[3]} = (n^{[2]} \times n^{[3]}) + n^{[3]}$$

$$P^{[3]} = (128 \times 10) + 10$$

$$P^{[3]} = 1280 + 10 = 1290$$

**Step 4: Sum total parameters**

$$P_{total} = P^{[1]} + P^{[2]} + P^{[3]}$$

$$P_{total} = 200960 + 32896 + 1290$$

$$P_{total} = 235146$$

**Interpretation:** This MLP has **235,146 learnable parameters**. None of these values were set manually by the engineer — they were all initialized randomly and then learned via backpropagation. The architecture decisions (784, 256, 128, 10) that *defined* these layers are **hyperparameters**.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Gradient Descent Update Rule (Why Hyperparameters Matter)

We want to minimize $\mathcal{L}(\theta)$ with respect to $\theta$. The Taylor expansion of $\mathcal{L}$ around $\theta_t$ to first order is:

$$\mathcal{L}(\theta_t + \Delta\theta) \approx \mathcal{L}(\theta_t) + \nabla_{\theta}\mathcal{L}(\theta_t)^{\top}\,\Delta\theta$$

To *decrease* the loss, we must choose $\Delta\theta$ pointing opposite to the gradient. Setting $\Delta\theta = -\eta\,\nabla_{\theta}\mathcal{L}(\theta_t)$:

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t + \Delta\theta = \theta_t - \eta\,\nabla_{\theta}\mathcal{L}(\theta_t)$$

**Analysis of $\eta$ (a hyperparameter):**

- If $\eta$ is **too large** (e.g., $\eta = 1.0$): the update overshoots minima; the loss diverges.
- If $\eta$ is **too small** (e.g., $\eta = 10^{-6}$): training is stable but excruciatingly slow.
- The "Goldilocks zone" depends on the loss landscape, which itself depends on $\theta$ (parameters) — creating a **coupled optimization problem**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Parameter vs Hyperparameter Decision Table

| Attribute | Parameters ($\theta$) | Hyperparameters ($h$) |
|---|---|---|
| Who sets them? | Learned automatically by the optimizer | Set manually by the engineer |
| When are they set? | During training (online) | Before training (offline) |
| Updated by? | Gradient descent / backpropagation | Tuning algorithms (Grid/Random/Bayesian) |
| Tuned using? | Training loss gradient $\nabla_{\theta}\mathcal{L}$ | Validation loss $\mathcal{L}_{val}$ |
| Typical count | $\sim 10^{6}$ to $10^{12}$ | $\sim 5$ to $50$ |
| Examples | $w$, $b$, $\gamma$, $\beta$ (BN params) | $\eta$, $B$, $E$, $L$, $\lambda$, $\beta_1$ |
| Sensitivity | Each individual weight has small effect | Single hyperparameter can flip success/failure |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **dual-loop interaction** between parameters (inner loop, learned) and hyperparameters (outer loop, tuned) in a typical Generative AI training pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Define Hyperparameter Search Space H] --> B[Sample Configuration h in H]
    B --> C[Initialize Parameters theta randomly]
    C --> D[Forward Pass through Generator G or LLM]
    D --> E[Compute Loss L of theta given h]
    E --> F[Backpropagation compute gradient]
    F --> G[Update Parameters theta using eta and gradient]
    G --> H{Has Epoch Reached Limit E?}
    H -- No --> D
    H -- Yes --> I[Evaluate Validation Loss L_val]
    I --> J{Is L_val Best so Far?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Save h star and theta star]
    J -- No --> L[Discard this h]
    K --> M{More Hyperparameter Trials Left?}
    L --> M
    M -- Yes --> B
    M -- No --> N[Deploy Final Model with theta star and h star]
```

## 4.2 Hierarchical Classification Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ModelConfig[Model Configuration Variables]
        subgraph Params[Parameters Learned from Data]
            P1[Weights w]
            P2[Biases b]
            P3[Layer Norm gamma beta]
        end
        subgraph Hyperparams[Hyperparameters Set by Engineer]
            H1[Learning Rate eta]
            H2[Batch Size B]
            H3[Epochs E]
            H4[Architecture L layers]
            H5[Regularization lambda]
            H6[Optimizer type and moments]
        end
    end
    Params --> TrainedModel[(Trained Model)]
    Hyperparams --> TrainedModel
```

## 4.3 Hyperparameter Tuning Strategy Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Hyperparameter Tuning Methods] --> G[Grid Search]
    Start --> R[Random Search]
    Start --> B[Bayesian Optimization]
    Start --> E[Evolutionary Search]
    G --> G1[Exhaustive over discrete grid]
    R --> R1[Sample uniformly from space]
    B --> B1[Build probabilistic surrogate model]
    E --> E1[Use genetic algorithms on population]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** For exam answers, students are expected to be able to **draw and label** the inner-loop (parameter update) and outer-loop (hyperparameter tuning) architecture clearly. The block diagram above captures all essential components.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between parameters and hyperparameters in a Generative AI model. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Parameters | Hyperparameters |
|---|---|---|
| Source | Learned from training data | Set manually before training |
| Update Mechanism | Optimized via backpropagation | Tuned via search/validation |
| Example 1 | Weights $w_{ij}$ of generator $G$ | Learning rate $\eta$ |
| Example 2 | Biases $b_k$ of discriminator $D$ | Number of epochs $E$ |

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition of parameters + 1 mark definition of hyperparameters + 1 mark examples)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**List any three hyperparameter tuning strategies and state one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Grid Search:** *Advantage* — guarantees coverage of all specified combinations; systematic and reproducible.
2. **Random Search:** *Advantage* — more efficient than grid search in high-dimensional spaces; avoids wasting trials on redundant axes.
3. **Bayesian Optimization:** *Advantage* — builds a surrogate model (e.g., Gaussian Process) to focus trials on promising regions; sample-efficient.

*(3 marks: 1 mark per strategy with its advantage)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat diagram the dual-loop optimization process involving parameter learning (inner loop) and hyperparameter tuning (outer loop) for training a Generative Adversarial Network (GAN). Clearly label at least **four hyperparameters** and **two sets of parameters**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a generator network in a GAN with the following architecture — Input latent dimension $z = 100$, Dense layer 1: $256$ neurons, Dense layer 2: $512$ neurons, Output layer: $784$ neurons (28×28 image). Calculate the **total number of trainable parameters**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Dual-loop explanation (7 marks):**

The training of a GAN involves two coupled loops:

- **Inner Loop (Parameter Learning):** Inside a single training run, the generator parameters $\theta_G$ and discriminator parameters $\theta_D$ are updated via backpropagation using the Adam/SGD optimizer with learning rate $\eta$. This loop runs every minibatch.

- **Outer Loop (Hyperparameter Tuning):** After completing all $E$ epochs for a given hyperparameter configuration $h$, the model is evaluated on a validation set. The hyperparameter $h$ is then adjusted based on a search strategy (e.g., Bayesian Optimization), and a new training run is initiated. This loop is repeated until a budget $N_{trials}$ is exhausted.

**Four hyperparameters to label:**
- Learning rate $\eta$
- Batch size $B$
- Latent dimension $z = 100$
- Number of epochs $E$

**Two sets of parameters to label:**
- Generator weights $\theta_G$
- Discriminator weights $\theta_D$

*(Full marks: 3 for diagram clarity, 2 for labeling hyperparameters, 2 for explaining inner/outer loops)*

**(b) Parameter count calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Layer 1 (Input → Dense 1)**

$$P^{[1]} = (n^{[0]} \times n^{[1]}) + n^{[1]} = (100 \times 256) + 256 = 25600 + 256 = 25856$$

**Step 2: Layer 2 (Dense 1 → Dense 2)**

$$P^{[2]} = (n^{[1]} \times n^{[2]}) + n^{[2]} = (256 \times 512) + 512 = 131072 + 512 = 131584$$

**Step 3: Layer 3 (Dense 2 → Output)**

$$P^{[3]} = (n^{[2]} \times n^{[3]}) + n^{[3]} = (512 \times 784) + 784 = 401408 + 784 = 402192$$

**Step 4: Total parameters**

$$P_{total} = 25856 + 131584 + 402192$$

$$P_{total} = 559632 \text{ parameters}$$

*(Full marks: 2 marks per step × 3 steps + 1 mark for final summation = 7 marks)*

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the role of the **learning rate ($\eta$)** as a hyperparameter. Using the gradient descent update equation, derive what happens when $\eta$ is (i) too large and (ii) too small. Mention at least **two learning rate schedules** used in Generative AI training. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between **Grid Search** and **Bayesian Optimization** for hyperparameter tuning. For a search space of 4 hyperparameters with 5 candidate values each, how many trials would Grid Search require? Comment on the scalability. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Role of Learning Rate (7 marks):**

The learning rate $\eta$ controls the **step size** of parameter updates during gradient descent:

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta\,\nabla_{\theta}\mathcal{L}(\theta_t)$$

**(i) When $\eta$ is too large:** The update term $-\eta\,\nabla_{\theta}\mathcal{L}$ becomes excessively large, causing $\theta$ to **overshoot** the minimum. The loss may oscillate or diverge to infinity. Mathematically, if $\eta > 2/L$ where $L$ is the Lipschitz constant of the gradient, the iteration becomes unstable.

**(ii) When $\eta$ is too small:** The updates are extremely tiny; convergence is guaranteed but requires an impractically large number of iterations. The model may also get stuck in **poor local minima** or saddle points.

**Two learning rate schedules in Generative AI:**

1. **Cosine Annealing:** $\eta_t = \eta_{min} + \frac{1}{2}(\eta_{max} - \eta_{min})(1 + \cos(\pi t / T))$ — used in Stable Diffusion and many LLM training runs.

2. **Warm-up + Decay (Linear or Exponential):** $\eta$ increases linearly for the first $k$ steps, then decays exponentially. Used in training of GPT-style models.

*(Marks: 2 for derivation, 2 for too-large/too-small analysis, 3 for two schedules with formulas)*

**(b) Grid Search vs Bayesian Optimization (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Grid Search | Bayesian Optimization |
|---|---|---|
| Strategy | Exhaustive enumeration | Probabilistic surrogate-guided |
| Trial count | Product of grid sizes | Adaptive; usually far fewer |
| Scalability | Poor in high dimensions | Better; suitable for expensive models |
| Optimality | Global within grid | Approximate; converges to optimum |

**Trial count for 4 hyperparameters × 5 values each:**

$$N_{trials} = 5 \times 5 \times 5 \times 5 = 5^{4} = 625 \text{ trials}$$

**Scalability comment:** If hyperparameters increase to 10, Grid Search would require $5^{10} = 9{,}765{,}625$ trials — infeasible for deep generative models that take days per trial. Bayesian Optimization (e.g., using Hyperopt, Optuna) reduces this to typically **50–200 trials**, making it the de facto choice for production GenAI pipelines.

*(Marks: 3 for differentiation, 2 for trial count, 2 for scalability comment)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing parameters with hyperparameters** is the #1 mistake — students often list the *number of layers* as a parameter. It is a hyperparameter because it is set *before* training.
> 2. **Forgetting the bias term** in parameter counting leads to a loss of 1–2 marks. Always add $n_{out}$ to each weight matrix multiplication.
> 3. **Writing gradient descent without explaining $\eta$'s role** loses marks — the equation alone is insufficient; the *hyperparameter* nature of $\eta$ must be explicitly stated.
> 4. **Mixing up tuning strategies** (e.g., calling Random Search "Bayesian") is a common error — know the distinguishing feature: surrogate model usage.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Parameters** ($w$, $b$) are **learned from data** during training via gradient descent; hyperparameters are **set by the engineer** before training.
- The **gradient descent update** is $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta\,\nabla_{\theta}\mathcal{L}(\theta_t)$, where $\eta$ is a hyperparameter and $\theta$ are parameters.
- **Total parameters** of an MLP layer $= (n_{in} \times n_{out}) + n_{out}$; sum across all $L$ layers for $P_{total}$.
- **Learning rate ($\eta$)** is the most critical hyperparameter; too large → divergence, too small → slow/no convergence.
- **Key hyperparameter tuning methods:** Grid Search (exhaustive, $5^4 = 625$ for 4 vars × 5 vals), Random Search (uniform sampling), Bayesian Optimization (surrogate-based, sample-efficient).
- **Modern GenAI-specific hyperparameters:** latent dimension $z$, noise schedule $\beta_t$, diffusion timesteps $T$, sampling temperature $\tau$, number of attention heads $h$.
- **Rule of thumb:** Parameter count scales as $O(n_{in} \cdot n_{out})$ per layer; tuning complexity scales exponentially with Grid Search but polynomially with Bayesian Optimization.
- **Always distinguish:** the **inner loop** updates parameters; the **outer loop** tunes hyperparameters — this duality is a frequently tested concept.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Backpropagation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT867 - GENERATIVE AI
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Backpropagation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Backpropagation (Backward Propagation of Errors)** is a supervised learning algorithm used to train Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs) by iteratively adjusting the network's synaptic weights and biases in the direction that minimizes a predefined loss function. Formally, it is a computational implementation of the **Chain Rule of Calculus** applied across a directed acyclic graph (the neural network), enabling efficient calculation of the gradient of the loss with respect to every weight in the network through a single forward-and-backward pass.

Mathematically, backpropagation computes:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w_{ij}^{(l)}}$$

for every weight $w_{ij}^{(l)}$ in layer $l$, where $\mathcal{L}$ is the loss function. The weights are then updated using **Gradient Descent**:

$$w_{ij}^{(l)} \leftarrow w_{ij}^{(l)} - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w_{ij}^{(l)}}$$

where $\eta$ is the **learning rate**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Backpropagation is the foundational training algorithm for nearly all deep learning and generative models, including Restricted Boltzmann Machines (RBMs), Variational Autoencoders (VAEs), and the early training phases of Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs). It is assessed frequently under CO1 (Fundamentals of Neural Computation).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **coach teaching a student archery** while blindfolded. The student shoots an arrow (forward pass), and based on where the arrow lands (prediction), you give feedback: *"You missed by 2 meters to the left."* The student then mentally traces back — *"How much did my left arm angle contribute to that error? How much did my stance?"* — and adjusts each muscle slightly (weight update). This reverse-tracing of responsibility through each contributing factor is exactly what backpropagation does.

In technical terms:
- **Forward Pass:** Input $\rightarrow$ Hidden Layers $\rightarrow$ Output Prediction $\rightarrow$ Compute Loss.
- **Backward Pass:** Loss $\rightarrow$ Trace responsibility backward through layers $\rightarrow$ Compute gradients $\rightarrow$ Update weights.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Bolded Standard Metrics)

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|------|--------|---------|
| Learning Rate | $\eta$ | Step size for weight updates; typically $\eta \in [10^{-4}, 10^{-1}]$ |
| Epoch | — | One complete pass through the entire training dataset |
| Batch Size | $m$ | Number of samples per gradient update |
| Loss Function | $\mathcal{L}$ | Measures prediction error (e.g., MSE, Cross-Entropy) |
| Activation Function | $\sigma$ | Non-linearity (e.g., ReLU, Sigmoid, Tanh) |

> [!NOTE]
> Backpropagation was popularized by **Rumelhart, Hinton & Williams (1986)** in their seminal paper *"Learning Representations by Back-propagating Errors,"* though its mathematical roots trace to **Linnainmaa (1970)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The Four Phases

The backpropagation algorithm operates in **four rigorously defined phases** per training iteration:

### Phase 1: Forward Propagation
- The input vector $\mathbf{x}^{(0)}$ is fed into the input layer.
- For each layer $l = 1, 2, \dots, L$, compute the pre-activation and activation:

$$z^{(l)} = W^{(l)} a^{(l-1)} + b^{(l)}$$

$$a^{(l)} = \sigma^{(l)}(z^{(l)})$$

- The final output $\hat{y} = a^{(L)}$ is compared with the true label $y$ to compute the loss $\mathcal{L}(\hat{y}, y)$.

### Phase 2: Output Layer Error Computation
- Compute the **error signal** (delta) at the output layer:

$$\delta^{(L)} = \nabla_a \mathcal{L} \odot \sigma'^{(L)}(z^{(L)})$$

where $\odot$ denotes the **Hadamard (element-wise) product** and $\nabla_a \mathcal{L}$ is the gradient of the loss w.r.t. the output activations.

### Phase 3: Backward Error Propagation
- For each hidden layer $l = L-1, L-2, \dots, 1$, propagate the error:

$$\delta^{(l)} = \left( (W^{(l+1)})^T \delta^{(l+1)} \right) \odot \sigma'^{(l)}(z^{(l)})$$

- This is the **direct application of the multivariable Chain Rule**.

### Phase 4: Weight & Bias Update
- Compute gradients:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(l)}} = \delta^{(l)} (a^{(l-1)})^T$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b^{(l)}} = \delta^{(l)}$$

- Update parameters using gradient descent:

$$W^{(l)} \leftarrow W^{(l)} - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(l)}}$$

$$b^{(l)} \leftarrow b^{(l)} - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b^{(l)}}$$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is **exam-ready**. Master every entry. KTU frequently asks students to *derive*, *state*, or *apply* these formulas.

| Formula | Description | Domain |
|---------|-------------|--------|
| $z^{(l)} = W^{(l)} a^{(l-1)} + b^{(l)}$ | Pre-activation at layer $l$ | Forward Pass |
| $a^{(l)} = \sigma^{(l)}(z^{(l)})$ | Activation at layer $l$ | Forward Pass |
| $\delta^{(L)} = \nabla_a \mathcal{L} \odot \sigma'^{(L)}(z^{(L)})$ | Output error term | Backward Pass |
| $\delta^{(l)} = (W^{(l+1)})^T \delta^{(l+1)} \odot \sigma'^{(l)}(z^{(l)})$ | Hidden layer error recursion | Backward Pass |
| $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(l)}} = \delta^{(l)} (a^{(l-1)})^T$ | Weight gradient | Update Phase |
| $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b^{(l)}} = \delta^{(l)}$ | Bias gradient | Update Phase |
| $W^{(l)} \leftarrow W^{(l)} - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(l)}}$ | SGD weight update | Update Phase |
| $\mathcal{L}_{\text{MSE}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Mean Squared Error loss | Loss |
| $\mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}} = -\sum_{i} y_i \log(\hat{y}_i)$ | Cross-Entropy loss | Loss |

> **Critical Reminder:** Never use the pipe character $\vert$ inside markdown tables — use `\vert` or `\mid` to denote absolute value (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) without breaking table syntax.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Computer Vision (CNNs):** Enables feature learning in convolutional layers for image classification, object detection (YOLO, ResNet).
- **Natural Language Processing (Transformers):** Although modern Transformers use a variant called **Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT)**, the underlying mechanics are identical to standard backpropagation.
- **Generative AI:** Backpropagation is the engine that trains the generator networks in VAEs, the discriminators in GANs, and the denoising networks in diffusion models.
- **Reinforcement Learning:** Actor-critic algorithms use backpropagation through policy networks.
- **Scientific Computing:** Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) use backpropagation to enforce PDE constraints.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Backpropagation from the Chain Rule

Consider a network with $L$ layers. Let the loss for a single training example be $\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}(a^{(L)}, y)$. We wish to find $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(l)}}$ for any layer $l$.

### Step 1: Express the Loss Dependency Chain

The loss depends on $a^{(L)}$, which depends on $z^{(L)}$, which depends on $W^{(L)}$. So:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(l)}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial a^{(L)}} \cdot \frac{\partial a^{(L)}}{\partial z^{(L)}} \cdot \frac{\partial z^{(L)}}{\partial a^{(L-1)}} \cdots \frac{\partial z^{(l)}}{\partial W^{(l)}}$$

### Step 2: Define the Error Term $\delta^{(L)}$

For the output layer:

$$\delta^{(L)} \equiv \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{(L)}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial a^{(L)}} \odot \frac{\partial a^{(L)}}{\partial z^{(L)}} = \nabla_a \mathcal{L} \odot \sigma'^{(L)}(z^{(L)})$$

### Step 3: Derive the Recursive Backward Formula

For a hidden layer $l < L$, the pre-activation $z^{(l)}$ affects $\mathcal{L}$ through all subsequent layers. By the chain rule:

$$\delta^{(l)} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{(l)}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{(l+1)}} \cdot \frac{\partial z^{(l+1)}}{\partial z^{(l)}}$$

Since $z^{(l+1)} = W^{(l+1)} \sigma(z^{(l)}) + b^{(l+1)}$, we have:

$$\frac{\partial z^{(l+1)}}{\partial z^{(l)}} = W^{(l+1)} \cdot \text{diag}(\sigma'^{(l)}(z^{(l)}))$$

Applying the matrix chain rule:

$$\delta^{(l)} = \left( (W^{(l+1)})^T \delta^{(l+1)} \right) \odot \sigma'^{(l)}(z^{(l)})$$

### Step 4: Derive Weight Gradient

Using $z^{(l)} = W^{(l)} a^{(l-1)} + b^{(l)}$:

$$\frac{\partial z^{(l)}}{\partial W^{(l)}} = a^{(l-1)}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(l)}} = \delta^{(l)} \cdot (a^{(l-1)})^T$$

### Step 5: Derive Bias Gradient

$$\frac{\partial z^{(l)}}{\partial b^{(l)}} = 1 \implies \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b^{(l)}} = \delta^{(l)}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (2-Layer Network)

Consider a 2-layer network with sigmoid activations, one input $x = 1$, true label $y = 0$, and:

$$W^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad b^{(1)} = 0.1, \quad W^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4 \end{bmatrix}, \quad b^{(2)} = 0.2$$

### Forward Pass Calculations

**Layer 1 Pre-activation:**

$$z^{(1)} = W^{(1)} \cdot x + b^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.3 \end{bmatrix} \cdot 1 + 0.1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 \\ 0.4 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Layer 1 Activation (Sigmoid $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$):**

$$a^{(1)} = \sigma(z^{(1)}) = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma(0.6) \\ \sigma(0.4) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6457 \\ 0.5987 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Layer 2 Pre-activation:**

$$z^{(2)} = W^{(2)} \cdot a^{(1)} + b^{(2)} = 0.4 \cdot 0.6457 + 0.2 \cdot 0.5987 + 0.2 = 0.2583 + 0.1197 + 0.2 = 0.5780$$

**Layer 2 Activation (Output):**

$$\hat{y} = a^{(2)} = \sigma(0.5780) = 0.6408$$

**Loss (MSE for single sample):**

$$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2}(y - \hat{y})^2 = \frac{1}{2}(0 - 0.6408)^2 = 0.2053$$

### Backward Pass Calculations

**Step 1 — Output Error Term:**

$$\nabla_a \mathcal{L} = (\hat{y} - y) = 0.6408$$

$$\sigma'(z^{(2)}) = \sigma(0.5780)(1 - \sigma(0.5780)) = 0.6408 \cdot 0.3592 = 0.2302$$

$$\delta^{(2)} = 0.6408 \cdot 0.2302 = 0.1475$$

**Step 2 — Hidden Layer Error Term:**

$$(W^{(2)})^T \delta^{(2)} = 0.4 \cdot 0.1475 = 0.0590$$

$$\sigma'(z^{(1)}) = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma(0.6)(1-\sigma(0.6)) \\ \sigma(0.4)(1-\sigma(0.4)) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6457 \cdot 0.3543 \\ 0.5987 \cdot 0.4013 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2288 \\ 0.2403 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\delta^{(1)} = 0.0590 \odot \begin{bmatrix} 0.2288 \\ 0.2403 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.01350 \\ 0.01418 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Weight Gradients:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(2)}} = \delta^{(2)} \cdot (a^{(1)})^T = 0.1475 \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 0.6457 & 0.5987 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.09524 & 0.08831 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(1)}} = \delta^{(1)} \cdot (a^{(0)})^T = \delta^{(1)} \cdot x = \begin{bmatrix} 0.01350 \\ 0.01418 \end{bmatrix} \cdot 1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.01350 \\ 0.01418 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Bias Gradients:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b^{(2)}} = \delta^{(2)} = 0.1475, \quad \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b^{(1)}} = \delta^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.01350 \\ 0.01418 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Parameter Update (with $\eta = 0.5$):**

$$W^{(2)}_{\text{new}} = W^{(2)} - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(2)}} = 0.4 - 0.5 \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 0.09524 & 0.08831 \end{bmatrix}$$

Note: This update is conceptual — for a 2-unit to 1-unit layer, $W^{(2)}$ is a row vector. Treating it as 1D scalar for simplicity:

$$W^{(2)}_{\text{new}} = 0.4 - 0.5 \cdot 0.1836 \approx 0.3082$$

$$W^{(1)}_{\text{new}} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.3 \end{bmatrix} - 0.5 \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 0.01350 \\ 0.01418 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4933 \\ 0.2929 \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!TIP]
> In an actual exam, you can simplify numerical evaluations to 2–3 decimal places. KTU examiners award partial marks for **correct formula setup** even if arithmetic is approximate.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Backpropagation Computational Flow (Mermaid)

The diagram below illustrates the **Block-Level Functional Architecture** of a single training iteration involving forward propagation, loss computation, backward propagation, and weight update.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Vector x] --> B[Layer 1: Linear Transform W1, b1]
    B --> C[Layer 1: Activation sigma1]
    C --> D[Layer 2: Linear Transform W2, b2]
    D --> E[Layer 2: Activation sigma2]
    E --> F[Output Prediction yHat]
    F --> G[Loss Function L yHat, y]
    G --> H[Output Error delta2]
    H --> I[Hidden Error delta1 via Chain Rule]
    I --> J[Compute Weight Gradients dL over dW]
    J --> K[Apply Gradient Descent Update]
    K --> L[Updated Weights W new]
    L -.->|Next Iteration| B
```

## 4.2 Detailed Backward Error Propagation Chain

The next diagram shows the recursive flow of error signals backward through each layer, which is the heart of the backpropagation algorithm.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Loss L] --> nodeB[delta at Layer L]
    nodeB --> nodeC[delta at Layer L-1]
    nodeC --> nodeD[delta at Layer L-2]
    nodeD --> nodeE[delta at Layer 1]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Gradient of W1]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Update W1]
    nodeB --> nodeH[Gradient of WL]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Update WL]
    style nodeA fill:#f9c74f
    style nodeG fill:#90be6d
    style nodeI fill:#90be6d
```

## 4.3 Vectorized Backprop Data Flow

This architecture-level flow maps how activations and errors interact across layers in matrix form.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    in1[Input x] --> fw1[Forward: z1 equals W1 x plus b1]
    fw1 --> fa1[Forward: a1 equals sigma z1]
    fa1 --> fw2[Forward: z2 equals W2 a1 plus b2]
    fw2 --> fa2[Forward: a2 equals sigma z2]
    fa2 --> loss[Compute Loss L]
    loss --> be2[Backward: delta2 equals grad L times sigma prime z2]
    be2 --> be1[Backward: delta1 equals WT2 delta2 times sigma prime z1]
    be1 --> gradW1[Gradient: dL over dW1 equals delta1 times a0 transpose]
    be1 --> gradW2[Gradient: dL over dW2 equals delta2 times a1 transpose]
    gradW1 --> upd[Update Weights via SGD]
    gradW2 --> upd
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]`

**Q: Define backpropagation. Why is it computationally efficient compared to naive gradient computation?**

**Model Answer:**

Backpropagation is a supervised learning algorithm that computes the gradient of the loss function with respect to each weight in a neural network by recursively applying the chain rule of calculus from the output layer back to the input layer.

It is computationally efficient because it **reuses intermediate computations**. Instead of computing each weight's gradient independently (which costs $O(W \cdot N)$ where $W$ is the number of weights and $N$ is the cost of one full forward evaluation), backpropagation performs **one forward pass** and **one backward pass**, achieving a total complexity of $O(W)$ per sample — the optimal theoretical bound.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified Style]`

**Q: State the role of the learning rate $\eta$ in backpropagation. What happens if $\eta$ is too large or too small?**

**Model Answer:**

The learning rate $\eta$ controls the **step size** taken in the direction opposite to the gradient during each weight update:

$$W \leftarrow W - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W}$$

- If $\eta$ is **too large**: The optimization may overshoot minima, causing divergence or oscillation.
- If $\eta$ is **too small**: Convergence becomes extremely slow and the model may get stuck in poor local minima.

> Typical safe ranges: $\eta \in [10^{-4}, 10^{-1}]$. Adaptive optimizers like **Adam** adjust $\eta$ per parameter automatically.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** A common mistake is forgetting to **transpose** the weight matrix correctly during backward propagation, or **confusing element-wise multiplication ($\odot$) with matrix multiplication**. Another frequent error is computing $\delta$ for the wrong layer index. KTU examiners deduct **2 marks** for each missing transpose, and **1 mark** for confusing the activation derivative $\sigma'$ with the activation $\sigma$ itself.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Predicted Style]`

**Q: Derive the backpropagation algorithm for a fully connected multi-layer neural network. Hence explain the four phases of training.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Derivation of the Backpropagation Equations

Starting from the loss $\mathcal{L}(a^{(L)}, y)$, we want to compute $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(l)}}$ and $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b^{(l)}}$ for every layer $l$.

**Define the pre-activation and activation:**

$$z^{(l)} = W^{(l)} a^{(l-1)} + b^{(l)}, \quad a^{(l)} = \sigma^{(l)}(z^{(l)})$$

**Define the error term at layer $l$:**

$$\delta^{(l)} \equiv \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{(l)}}$$

**Output layer error** (from direct differentiation):

$$\delta^{(L)} = \nabla_{a} \mathcal{L} \odot \sigma'^{(L)}(z^{(L)})$$

**Recursive backward formula** (chain rule):

$$\delta^{(l)} = \left( (W^{(l+1)})^T \delta^{(l+1)} \right) \odot \sigma'^{(l)}(z^{(l)})$$

**Gradient w.r.t. weights and biases:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(l)}} = \delta^{(l)} (a^{(l-1)})^T, \quad \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b^{(l)}} = \delta^{(l)}$$

**Update rule:**

$$W^{(l)} \leftarrow W^{(l)} - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(l)}}, \quad b^{(l)} \leftarrow b^{(l)} - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b^{(l)}}$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: The Four Phases of Training

**Phase 1 — Forward Propagation:**
Inputs flow through each layer via $z^{(l)} = W^{(l)} a^{(l-1)} + b^{(l)}$ and $a^{(l)} = \sigma^{(l)}(z^{(l)})$. The final output $\hat{y} = a^{(L)}$ is computed.

**Phase 2 — Loss Computation:**
The discrepancy between $\hat{y}$ and $y$ is measured using a loss function (MSE for regression, Cross-Entropy for classification).

**Phase 3 — Backward Propagation:**
Starting from $\delta^{(L)}$, errors are recursively propagated to all hidden layers using the chain-rule-based recursion.

**Phase 4 — Parameter Update:**
Gradients are used to update weights and biases via gradient descent, completing one training iteration. The cycle repeats for multiple epochs until convergence.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified Style]`

**Q: Consider a 2-layer neural network with sigmoid activation in both layers. Given input $x = 0.5$, true output $y = 1$, weights $W^{(1)} = [0.6, 0.4]^T$, $b^{(1)} = 0.1$, $W^{(2)} = [0.7, 0.5]$, $b^{(2)} = 0.05$, and learning rate $\eta = 0.1$, perform one complete forward and backward pass and update the weights using MSE loss.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Forward Pass and Loss Computation

**Layer 1 Pre-activation:**

$$z^{(1)} = W^{(1)} \cdot x + b^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 \\ 0.4 \end{bmatrix} \cdot 0.5 + 0.1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.30 + 0.10 \\ 0.20 + 0.10 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.40 \\ 0.30 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Layer 1 Activation:**

$$a^{(1)} = \sigma(z^{(1)}) = \begin{bmatrix} 1/(1+e^{-0.40}) \\ 1/(1+e^{-0.30}) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5987 \\ 0.5744 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Layer 2 Pre-activation:**

$$z^{(2)} = W^{(2)} \cdot a^{(1)} + b^{(2)} = 0.7 \cdot 0.5987 + 0.5 \cdot 0.5744 + 0.05 = 0.4191 + 0.2872 + 0.05 = 0.7563$$

**Layer 2 Activation (Output):**

$$\hat{y} = a^{(2)} = \sigma(0.7563) = 0.6903$$

**Loss (MSE for one sample):**

$$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2}(y - \hat{y})^2 = \frac{1}{2}(1 - 0.6903)^2 = \frac{1}{2}(0.3097)^2 = 0.0480$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Backward Pass and Weight Updates

**Output Error:**

$$\delta^{(2)} = (\hat{y} - y) \cdot \sigma'(z^{(2)}) = (0.6903 - 1) \cdot 0.6903 \cdot (1 - 0.6903)$$

$$\delta^{(2)} = (-0.3097)(0.6903)(0.3097) = -0.0662$$

**Hidden Error:**

$$\sigma'(z^{(1)}) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5987 \cdot 0.4013 \\ 0.5744 \cdot 0.4256 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2402 \\ 0.2445 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\delta^{(1)} = (W^{(2)})^T \delta^{(2)} \odot \sigma'(z^{(1)}) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7 \\ 0.5 \end{bmatrix}(-0.0662) \odot \begin{bmatrix} 0.2402 \\ 0.2445 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\delta^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.0463 \\ -0.0331 \end{bmatrix} \odot \begin{bmatrix} 0.2402 \\ 0.2445 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.01113 \\ -0.00809 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Weight Updates (with $\eta = 0.1$):**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(2)}} = \delta^{(2)} \cdot (a^{(1)})^T = -0.0662 \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 0.5987 & 0.5744 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.03963 & -0.03802 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$W^{(2)}_{\text{new}} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix} - 0.1 \cdot \begin{bmatrix} -0.03963 & -0.03802 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.70396 & 0.50380 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(1)}} = \delta^{(1)} \cdot x = \begin{bmatrix} -0.01113 \\ -0.00809 \end{bmatrix} \cdot 0.5 = \begin{bmatrix} -0.00557 \\ -0.00405 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$W^{(1)}_{\text{new}} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 \\ 0.4 \end{bmatrix} - 0.1 \cdot \begin{bmatrix} -0.00557 \\ -0.00405 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.60056 \\ 0.40041 \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> - **Sign Errors**: A *very* common mistake is writing $\delta^{(L)} = (y - \hat{y})\sigma'$ instead of $(\hat{y} - y)\sigma'$. This is determined by the *definition* of MSE. KTU markers deduct **1 full mark** for sign confusion.
> - **Matrix Dimension Mismatches**: Forgetting to transpose $(a^{(l-1)})^T$ when multiplying with $\delta^{(l)}$ will produce dimensionally invalid results — guaranteed 0 marks for that sub-part.
> - **Forgetting the derivative**: Many students write $\sigma(z^{(l)})$ instead of $\sigma'(z^{(l)})$ in the error recursion. This is a frequent 2-mark deduction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Master Before Exam Day:**

- Backpropagation = **Chain Rule + Gradient Descent** applied to neural networks.
- **Forward pass** computes activations; **backward pass** computes gradients.
- The **error term** $\delta^{(l)}$ is the gradient of loss w.r.t. pre-activation $z^{(l)}$.
- Output error: $\delta^{(L)} = \nabla_a \mathcal{L} \odot \sigma'(z^{(L)})$.
- Hidden error recursion: $\delta^{(l)} = (W^{(l+1)})^T \delta^{(l+1)} \odot \sigma'(z^{(l)})$.
- Weight gradient: $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(l)}} = \delta^{(l)} (a^{(l-1)})^T$.
- Update rule: $W^{(l)} \leftarrow W^{(l)} - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(l)}}$.
- Always remember to **cache forward-pass values** $z^{(l)}$ and $a^{(l)}$ — the backward pass needs them.
- **Sigmoid derivative:** $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$.
- **Tanh derivative:** $\tanh'(z) = 1 - \tanh^2(z)$.
- **ReLU derivative:** $\text{ReLU}'(z) = 1$ if $z > 0$, else $0$.
- Computational cost of backpropagation: $O(W)$ per sample — optimal.
- **Vanishing/Exploding Gradient Problem**: A core limitation motivating advanced architectures (LSTM, Residual Connections, BatchNorm).
- Backpropagation is the **training engine** behind all modern generative AI models (GANs, VAEs, Diffusion).
- Numerical answer writing should retain at least **3–4 decimal places** for accuracy marks.
- **Critical exam trick**: When asked to "derive," always begin by *defining* $z^{(l)}$, $a^{(l)}$, and $\delta^{(l)}$ *explicitly*. This earns 1 mark immediately even if the rest is incomplete.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Advanced Architectures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT867 - GENERATIVE AI
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Advanced Architectures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Generative AI** under the KTU 2024 Scheme, **Advanced Architectures** refer to specialized, deep neural network topologies explicitly engineered to model, learn, and synthesize high-dimensional data distributions (text, images, audio, molecules, 3D structures, etc.). Unlike classical feed-forward networks or shallow autoencoders, these architectures employ sophisticated inductive biases — self-attention, latent-variable inference, adversarial min-max optimization, or stochastic denoising — to capture complex, multi-modal generative processes.

The KTU 2024 syllabus groups the following canonical families under this umbrella:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 — Advanced Architecture Families**
> 1. **Transformer Architecture** (Self-Attention, Multi-Head Attention, Positional Encoding)
> 2. **Variational Autoencoders (VAE)**
> 3. **Generative Adversarial Networks (GAN)**
> 4. **Diffusion Models** (Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models)
> 5. **Mixture of Experts (MoE)**
> 6. **State Space Models (SSMs)** — Mamba, S4
> 7. **Energy-Based Models (EBMs)** and **Normalizing Flows** (overview level)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Specialist Workshop" Intuition

Imagine a **master artisan's workshop**. A standard machine shop (basic neural network) can make generic parts. But to craft a luxury watch, a violin, or a sculpture, you need *specialized workstations* — each designed for a specific generative craft.

| Workshop Station | Generative AI Counterpart | What It "Crafts" |
|------------------|--------------------------|------------------|
| Precision lathe (rotational symmetry) | **VAE** (latent rotational manifold) | Smooth, continuous generations |
| Sculptor vs. Critic (duel) | **GAN** (Generator vs. Discriminator) | Photorealistic, sharp images |
| Sandblasting (gradual refinement) | **Diffusion Model** (iterative denoising) | High-fidelity images, audio |
| Conference of experts (selective) | **MoE / Transformer** | Scalable language understanding |
| Filter pump (flow) | **Normalizing Flow** | Exact-likelihood sampling |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students**: Every advanced architecture introduces a *unique mathematical trick* (reparameterization, adversarial loss, denoising score matching, sparse gating). Identifying this trick is the fastest way to answer ESE questions on these topics.

## 1.3 Why "Advanced"? — The Three Driving Constraints

Advanced architectures emerged to overcome three classical limitations:

1. **Scalability Bottleneck** — RNN/LSTM cannot parallelize over sequence length $\Rightarrow$ solved by **Transformer self-attention**.
2. **Blurry Outputs** — Standard autoencoders minimize MSE $\Rightarrow$ blurry generations $\Rightarrow$ solved by **VAE (probabilistic latent space)** and **GAN (adversarial loss)**.
3. **Mode Collapse & Training Instability** — Solved by **Diffusion models** (stable likelihood-based training) and **MoE** (sparse expert routing).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Transformer — Self-Attention as the Core Inductive Bias

The **Transformer** (Vaswani et al., 2017) replaces recurrence and convolution with a mechanism called **Scaled Dot-Product Self-Attention**. Each token in a sequence attends to every other token, weighted by learned relevance.

### Operational Steps
- **Step 1 — Linear Projections**: Input embeddings $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ are projected into three matrices:
  Queries $Q = X W_Q$, Keys $K = X W_K$, Values $V = X W_V$.
- **Step 2 — Scaled Dot Product**: Compute raw attention scores $S = Q K^{\top}$.
- **Step 3 — Scale by $\sqrt{d_k}$**: Prevents softmax saturation in high dimensions.
- **Step 4 — Softmax Normalization**: Convert scores to probability distribution over keys.
- **Step 5 — Weighted Aggregation**: Multiply attention weights by $V$ to get output.

### Why It Works (The "How")
The scaling factor $\sqrt{d_k}$ counteracts the variance blow-up: if $q_i, k_i$ have unit variance, their dot product has variance $d_k$. Without scaling, softmax becomes one-hot and gradients vanish.

## 2.2 The Variational Autoencoder (VAE) — Probabilistic Latent Inference

A **VAE** (Kingma & Welling, 2014) reframes the autoencoder bottleneck as a *sampled* latent distribution, enabling generative sampling.

### Operational Steps
- **Step 1 — Encoder** $q_\phi(z \mid x)$: Maps input $x$ to parameters $(\mu, \sigma^2)$ of a Gaussian latent.
- **Step 2 — Reparameterization Trick**: Sample $z = \mu + \sigma \odot \epsilon$, where $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$. This makes sampling differentiable.
- **Step 3 — Decoder** $p_\theta(x \mid z)$: Reconstructs $\hat{x}$ from $z$.
- **Step 4 — Optimize ELBO**: Maximize a lower bound on log-likelihood.

## 2.3 The Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) — Two-Player Min-Max

A **GAN** (Goodfellow et al., 2014) frames generation as a game between:
- **Generator** $G$: maps noise $z$ to fake sample $G(z)$.
- **Discriminator** $D$: outputs probability that a sample is real.

### Operational Steps
- **Step 1 — Real Sample Pass**: $D$ is trained to output $\approx 1$ for real $x$ and $\approx 0$ for fake $G(z)$.
- **Step 2 — Generator Update**: $G$ is updated to *fool* $D$, i.e., push $D(G(z)) \to 1$.
- **Step 3 — Alternating Optimization**: Repeat until Nash equilibrium (ideally $D(G(z)) = 0.5$).

## 2.4 Diffusion Models — Iterative Denoising

**Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPM)** (Ho et al., 2020) learn to reverse a gradual noising process.

### Operational Steps
- **Step 1 — Forward (Fixed) Process**: Gradually add Gaussian noise over $T$ steps: $x_t = \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t} \, x_0 + \sqrt{1 - \bar{\alpha}_t} \, \epsilon$.
- **Step 2 — Reverse (Learned) Process**: A neural network $\epsilon_\theta(x_t, t)$ predicts the noise added at step $t$.
- **Step 3 — Training**: Minimize MSE between true noise $\epsilon$ and predicted $\hat{\epsilon}$.
- **Step 4 — Sampling**: Starting from $x_T \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$, iteratively denoise for $T$ steps.

## 2.5 Mixture of Experts (MoE) — Conditional Computation

A **MoE** layer routes each input to a subset of $N$ "expert" sub-networks via a learned gating function.

### Operational Steps
- **Step 1 — Gating**: Compute $G(x) = \text{Softmax}(W_g \cdot x)$, producing expert weights.
- **Step 2 — Top-K Selection**: Keep only top-$K$ experts (typically $K=1$ or $2$), zero out the rest.
- **Step 3 — Weighted Combination**: $y = \sum_{i \in \text{TopK}} G(x)_i \cdot E_i(x)$.
- **Step 4 — Load Balancing Loss**: Penalize uneven expert utilization.

## 2.6 State Space Models (SSMs) — The Mamba Breakthrough

**SSMs** (Gu et al., 2022; Mamba by Albert Gu & Tri Dao, 2023) model sequences via a continuous-time state-space formulation with *selective* state updates, enabling linear-time sequence modeling.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas — Memorize for ESE 2024**

| # | Architecture | Core Equation | Key Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---|--------------|---------------|--------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Transformer | $\text{Attn}(Q,K,V) = \text{Softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$ | $d_k$ = key dim; $\sqrt{d_k}$ = stabilizer | LLMs (GPT, LLaMA), Vision Transformers |
| 2 | Multi-Head Attn | $\text{MHA}(X) = \text{Concat}(H_1,\dots,H_h)W_O$ | $h$ = num heads | Parallel attention subspaces |
| 3 | VAE ELBO | $\mathcal{L} = \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z\mid x)}[\log p_\theta(x\mid z)] - D_{KL}\!\left(q_\phi(z\mid x)\,\Vert\,p(z)\right)$ | First term = reconstruction; second = regularization | Anomaly detection, molecule design |
| 4 | VAE Reparam. | $z = \mu + \sigma \odot \epsilon,\;\; \epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0,I)$ | Enables backprop through sampling | All VAE-based diffusion latents |
| 5 | GAN Objective | $\min_G \max_D V(D,G) = \mathbb{E}_{x\sim p_{data}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z\sim p_z}[\log(1-D(G(z)))]$ | Saddle-point problem | Image synthesis (StyleGAN) |
| 6 | Diffusion Forward | $q(x_t \mid x_{t-1}) = \mathcal{N}\!\left(x_t;\;\sqrt{1-\beta_t}\,x_{t-1},\;\beta_t I\right)$ | $\beta_t$ = noise schedule | Stable likelihood training |
| 7 | Diffusion Loss | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{simple}} = \mathbb{E}_{t,x_0,\epsilon}\!\left[\Vert \epsilon - \epsilon_\theta(x_t,t)\Vert^2\right]$ | Predict noise directly | DALL-E 2, Stable Diffusion |
| 8 | MoE Routing | $y = \sum_{i=1}^{N} G(x)_i \cdot E_i(x)$ | $G$ = gate; $E_i$ = $i$-th expert | Mixtral, Switch Transformer (trillion-param) |
| 9 | SSM State Update | $h'(t) = A h(t) + B x(t),\quad y(t) = C h(t)$ | $(A,B,C)$ = state matrices | Long-sequence modeling (Mamba) |
| 10 | Self-Attention Cost | $\mathcal{O}(n^2 \cdot d)$ | $n$ = seq length; $d$ = hidden dim | Quadratic bottleneck — motivates SSMs |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Healthcare**: Diffusion models for low-dose CT denoising.
- **Drug Discovery**: VAEs for novel molecular graph generation.
- **Autonomous Systems**: GANs for sim-to-real domain adaptation.
- **Edge AI / On-Device LLMs**: MoE and SSM (Mamba) for trillion-param inference with sub-linear compute.
- **Creative Industry**: Transformers + Diffusion for text-to-image (Stable Diffusion, DALL-E 3).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Why the $\sqrt{d_k}$ Scale Factor?

We derive the variance argument for the scaling factor in Scaled Dot-Product Attention.

**Given**: $q_i, k_j \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)$ i.i.d. with dimension $d_k$. The raw score is:

$$s_{ij} = q_i^{\top} k_j = \sum_{l=1}^{d_k} q_{i,l} \cdot k_{j,l}$$

**Step 1 — Compute mean**: Since $\mathbb{E}[q_{i,l}] = \mathbb{E}[k_{j,l}] = 0$,
$$\mathbb{E}[s_{ij}] = \sum_{l=1}^{d_k} \mathbb{E}[q_{i,l}] \mathbb{E}[k_{j,l}] = 0$$

**Step 2 — Compute variance**: Using independence,
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}(s_{ij}) &= \sum_{l=1}^{d_k} \text{Var}(q_{i,l} \cdot k_{j,l}) \\
&= \sum_{l=1}^{d_k} \mathbb{E}[q_{i,l}^2]\,\mathbb{E}[k_{j,l}^2] - \underbrace{(\mathbb{E}[q_{i,l}]\,\mathbb{E}[k_{j,l}])^2}_{0} \\
&= \sum_{l=1}^{d_k} (1)(1) = d_k
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion**: $s_{ij}$ has variance $d_k$. Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ normalizes the variance back to 1, keeping the softmax input in a numerically well-conditioned regime (preventing one-hot saturation). $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation II — Variational Lower Bound (ELBO) for VAE

We derive the Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO) used to train a VAE.

**Goal**: Maximize $\log p_\theta(x) = \log \int p_\theta(x, z)\, dz$.

**Step 1 — Introduce variational distribution** $q_\phi(z \mid x)$:
$$\begin{aligned}
\log p_\theta(x) &= \log \int q_\phi(z\mid x)\,\frac{p_\theta(x, z)}{q_\phi(z\mid x)}\, dz \\
&= \log \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z\mid x)}\!\left[\frac{p_\theta(x,z)}{q_\phi(z\mid x)}\right]
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Jensen's inequality** (since $\log$ is concave):
$$\log p_\theta(x) \geq \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z\mid x)}\!\left[\log \frac{p_\theta(x,z)}{q_\phi(z\mid x)}\right] \equiv \text{ELBO}$$

**Step 3 — Expand joint** $p_\theta(x, z) = p_\theta(x \mid z) p(z)$:
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{ELBO} &= \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z\mid x)}[\log p_\theta(x \mid z)] + \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z\mid x)}\!\left[\log \frac{p(z)}{q_\phi(z\mid x)}\right] \\
&= \underbrace{\mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z\mid x)}[\log p_\theta(x \mid z)]}_{\text{Reconstruction Term}} - \underbrace{D_{KL}\!\left(q_\phi(z\mid x)\,\Vert\,p(z)\right)}_{\text{Regularization Term}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation**:
- The **reconstruction term** pushes the decoder to faithfully reproduce $x$ from $z$.
- The **KL term** regularizes the latent posterior $q_\phi(z \mid x)$ toward the prior $p(z) = \mathcal{N}(0, I)$.

This is precisely the loss function minimized (with sign flipped) when training a VAE. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Derivation III — Optimal Discriminator for GAN

We show that for a fixed Generator $G$, the optimal Discriminator $D^*$ satisfies $D^*(x) = \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)}$.

**Step 1 — GAN value function** (for fixed $G$, optimize $D$):
$$V(D, G) = \int_x p_{\text{data}}(x) \log D(x)\, dx + \int_z p_z(z) \log(1 - D(G(z)))\, dz$$

**Step 2 — Change variable** $x = G(z)$ in the second integral:
$$V(D, G) = \int_x \!\left[p_{\text{data}}(x) \log D(x) + p_g(x) \log(1 - D(x))\right] dx$$

**Step 3 — Pointwise maximization**: For any $x$ where the integrand is differentiable,
$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial}{\partial D(x)}\!\left[p_{\text{data}}(x) \log D(x) + p_g(x) \log(1 - D(x))\right] &= 0 \\
\frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{D(x)} - \frac{p_g(x)}{1 - D(x)} &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Solve**:
$$\begin{aligned}
p_{\text{data}}(x)(1 - D(x)) &= p_g(x) D(x) \\
p_{\text{data}}(x) &= D(x)\!\left[p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)\right] \\
D^*(x) &= \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — At equilibrium** ($p_g = p_{\text{data}}$), $D^*(x) = \frac{1}{2}$ everywhere, confirming the **Jensen-Shannon divergence** interpretation of the GAN objective. $\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Comparative Table — Frequently Asked in ESE**

| Property | Transformer | VAE | GAN | Diffusion | MoE |
|----------|-------------|-----|-----|-----------|-----|
| **Training Stability** | High | High | Low (mode collapse) | High | Medium |
| **Output Sharpness** | High (text) | Low (blurry) | Very High | Very High | Inherits backbone |
| **Sampling Speed** | Fast (1-pass) | Fast | Fast | Slow (T steps) | Fast |
| **Likelihood** | Approximate | Lower-bounded | None | Tractable | — |
| **Latent Space** | Discrete tokens | Continuous Gaussian | None explicit | Implicit | Continuous gating |
| **Key Trick** | Self-Attention | Reparameterization | Adversarial Loss | Denoising | Sparse Gating |
| **Compute Cost** | $O(n^2 d)$ | $O(d^2)$ | $O(d^2)$ | $O(T \cdot d^2)$ | Sparse $O(d^2/k)$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Unified View of Generative Architecture Stack

This diagram maps the **functional architecture flow** showing how advanced generative architectures interact with the data lifecycle: encoding $\to$ latent representation $\to$ decoding/generation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Data X] --> B{Architecture Type}
    B -->|Token sequence| C[Transformer Encoder Stack]
    B -->|Continuous data| D[VAE Encoder]
    B -->|Image target| E[GAN Generator]
    B -->|Iterative refine| F[Diffusion U Net]
    B -->|Sparse scaling| G[MoE Gating Router]

    C --> C1[Self Attention Q K V]
    C1 --> C2[Feed Forward + Residual]
    C2 --> C3[Latent Contextual Embeddings]

    D --> D1[Reparameterization Trick]
    D1 --> D2[Sampled Latent Vector Z]
    D2 --> D3[Decoder Reconstruction]

    E --> E1[Random Noise Vector Z]
    E1 --> E2[Transposed Conv Generator]
    E2 --> E3[Fake Sample]
    E3 --> E4[Discriminator D Real or Fake]

    F --> F1[Forward Noising x0 to xT]
    F1 --> F2[Reverse Denoising Net Epsilon Theta]
    F2 --> F3[Iterative Sample xt minus 1]

    G --> G1[Top K Expert Selection]
    G1 --> G2[Expert 1]
    G1 --> G3[Expert 2]
    G1 --> G4[Expert N]
    G2 --> H[Weighted Output Y]
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H

    C3 --> I[Generated Output]
    D3 --> I
    E4 --> I
    F3 --> I
    H --> I
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Self-Attention Mechanism Internals

A detailed block-level functional architecture of **Scaled Dot-Product Self-Attention** used inside the Transformer.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[Input Embeddings X] --> QP[Linear Q Projection WQ]
    X --> KP[Linear K Projection WK]
    X --> VP[Linear V Projection WV]

    QP --> QM[Queries Matrix Q]
    KP --> KM[Keys Matrix K]
    VP --> VM[Values Matrix V]

    QM --> DOT[Q times K Transpose]
    KM --> DOT
    DOT --> SC[Scale by 1 over sqrt dk]
    SC --> MASK[Optional Mask]
    MASK --> SM[Softmax Row wise]
    SM --> AW[Attention Weights]
    AW --> MUL[Multiply by V]
    VM --> MUL
    MUL --> OUT[Attention Output]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Diffusion Forward/Reverse Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X0[Clean Sample x0] --> F1[Add Noise beta 1]
    F1 --> F2[Add Noise beta 2]
    F2 --> F3[Add Noise beta T minus 1]
    F3 --> FT[Pure Noise xT]

    FT --> R1[Denoise Net Eps theta xt t]
    R1 --> R2[Denoise Net Eps theta xt minus 1 t minus 1]
    R2 --> R3[Denoise Net Eps theta x1 1]
    R3 --> X0R[Reconstructed x0 hat]

    R1 -.predicts.-> L1[Loss MSE eps true vs eps predicted]
    R2 -.predicts.-> L1
    R3 -.predicts.-> L1
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — MoE Sparse Routing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN[Input Token x] --> GATE[Gating Network Wg dot x]
    GATE --> SOFT[Softmax Expert Probabilities]
    SOFT --> TOPK[Top K Selection K equals 2]
    TOPK --> E1[Expert 1 FFN]
    TOPK --> E2[Expert 2 FFN]
    TOPK --> E0[Expert N Masked Out]
    E1 --> COMB[Weighted Sum Gi times Ei x]
    E2 --> COMB
    COMB --> OUT[Sparse Output y]
```
