# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`
**Explain the role of the $\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling factor in Scaled Dot-Product Self-Attention. What problem does it solve?**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:
- **(1 mark)** The scaling factor $\sqrt{d_k}$ is applied to the dot-product $QK^{\top}$ before the softmax to prevent saturation of attention weights.
- **(1 mark)** It compensates for the variance of dot products: since $q_i, k_i$ have unit variance, their dot product has variance $d_k$. Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ normalizes variance to 1.
- **(1 mark)** Without scaling, when $d_k$ is large, softmax inputs become very large, pushing the output toward a one-hot vector and causing vanishing gradients during backpropagation.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is the reparameterization trick in a Variational Autoencoder? Why is it necessary?**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:
- **(1 mark)** The reparameterization trick expresses the latent sample as $z = \mu + \sigma \odot \epsilon$, where $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$, decoupling the stochasticity from the network parameters $(\mu, \sigma)$.
- **(1 mark)** It is necessary because direct sampling $z \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ is a non-differentiable operation, which breaks gradient-based training.
- **(1 mark)** By moving the randomness to an external noise variable $\epsilon$, gradients can flow through $\mu$ and $\sigma$ to update the encoder, enabling end-to-end backpropagation through the sampling layer.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper 2024]`
**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the architecture of a **Variational Autoencoder (VAE)**. Derive its training objective (ELBO) starting from the marginal log-likelihood.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Generative Adversarial Network (GAN)** training procedure. Derive the optimal discriminator $D^*(x)$ for a fixed generator and comment on the Nash equilibrium condition.

---

#### Model Solution (A-a) — VAE Architecture & ELBO Derivation

**Architecture Description (3 marks)**:
The VAE consists of:
- **Encoder (Inference Network)** $q_\phi(z \mid x)$: Takes input $x$, outputs parameters $(\mu, \sigma^2)$ of an approximate posterior Gaussian.
- **Reparameterization Layer**: Samples $z = \mu + \sigma \odot \epsilon$, $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$.
- **Decoder (Generative Network)** $p_\theta(x \mid z)$: Reconstructs $\hat{x}$ from $z$.
- **Loss Function**: Negative ELBO.

**ELBO Derivation (4 marks)**:
$$\begin{aligned}
\log p_\theta(x) &= \log \int p_\theta(x \mid z) p(z)\, dz \\
&= \log \int q_\phi(z \mid x) \frac{p_\theta(x \mid z) p(z)}{q_\phi(z \mid x)}\, dz \\
&= \log \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z \mid x)}\!\left[\frac{p_\theta(x \mid z) p(z)}{q_\phi(z \mid x)}\right] \\
&\geq \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z \mid x)}\!\left[\log p_\theta(x \mid z) + \log p(z) - \log q_\phi(z \mid x)\right] \quad \text{(Jensen's)} \\
&= \underbrace{\mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z \mid x)}[\log p_\theta(x \mid z)]}_{\text{Reconstruction}} - \underbrace{D_{KL}\!\left(q_\phi(z \mid x) \,\Vert\, p(z)\right)}_{\text{KL Regularizer}}
\end{aligned}$$

The VAE is trained by **maximizing** this ELBO (equivalently, minimizing its negative). The reconstruction term ensures faithful input recovery, while the KL term keeps the latent space close to a standard Gaussian prior — enabling smooth sampling.

---

#### Model Solution (A-b) — GAN Training & Optimal Discriminator

**Training Procedure (3 marks)**:
- **Step 1**: Sample minibatch of real data $x \sim p_{\text{data}}$ and noise $z \sim p_z$.
- **Step 2**: Update Discriminator $D$ to maximize:
$$\mathcal{L}_D = \mathbb{E}_x[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_z[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$$
- **Step 3**: Update Generator $G$ to minimize $\mathbb{E}_z[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$ (or equivalently maximize $\mathbb{E}_z[\log D(G(z))]$).
- **Step 4**: Alternate until convergence.

**Optimal Discriminator Derivation (4 marks)**:
For a fixed $G$, the value function becomes
$$V(D) = \int \left[p_{\text{data}}(x) \log D(x) + p_g(x) \log(1 - D(x))\right] dx$$
Setting $\frac{\partial V}{\partial D(x)} = 0$:
$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{D(x)} - \frac{p_g(x)}{1 - D(x)} &= 0 \\
D^*(x) &= \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Nash Equilibrium (extra credit)**: At equilibrium, $p_g = p_{\text{data}}$, giving $D^*(x) = \tfrac{1}{2}$ — the discriminator cannot distinguish real from fake, indicating perfect generation.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper 2024]`
**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Diffusion Model** (DDPM) framework. Derive the simplified training loss and explain why denoising score matching works.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Mixture of Experts (MoE)** architecture. With a neat block diagram, explain top-$K$ gating and the load-balancing loss.

---

#### Model Solution (B-a) — Diffusion Models (DDPM)

**Framework (3 marks)**:
- **Forward Process** $q$: Gradually corrupts $x_0 \to x_1 \to \dots \to x_T$ by adding Gaussian noise with a variance schedule $\beta_1, \dots, \beta_T$:
$$q(x_t \mid x_{t-1}) = \mathcal{N}\!\left(x_t;\; \sqrt{1-\beta_t}\, x_{t-1},\; \beta_t I\right)$$
- **Reverse Process** $p_\theta$: A neural network (typically a U-Net) $\epsilon_\theta(x_t, t)$ learns to predict the noise that was added, gradually denoising $x_T \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ back to $x_0$.

**Training Loss Derivation (4 marks)**:
The negative ELBO of the reverse process decomposes into a sum of per-step denoising KL divergences. Ho et al. (2020) showed that a **simplified surrogate** loss works well in practice:
$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{simple}} = \mathbb{E}_{t, x_0, \epsilon}\!\left[\Vert \epsilon - \epsilon_\theta(x_t, t)\Vert^2\right]$$
where $x_t = \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}\, x_0 + \sqrt{1 - \bar{\alpha}_t}\, \epsilon$, with $\bar{\alpha}_t = \prod_{s=1}^{t} (1 - \beta_s)$.

**Why It Works**: Minimizing $\Vert \epsilon - \epsilon_\theta \Vert^2$ is equivalent (up to a constant) to **denoising score matching** — it makes the network approximate the score $\nabla_{x_t} \log q(x_t)$, which defines the reverse diffusion drift. This connects DDPMs to **score-based generative models** (Song & Ermon, 2019).

---

#### Model Solution (B-b) — Mixture of Experts

**Architecture (3 marks)**:
A Mixture of Experts (MoE) layer replaces the dense feed-forward block in a Transformer with $N$ parallel "expert" sub-networks $\{E_1, E_2, \dots, E_N\}$ and a **gating network** $G(x)$. For each input token $x$:
$$y = \sum_{i=1}^{N} G(x)_i \cdot E_i(x)$$

**Top-$K$ Gating (3 marks)**:
- Compute gating logits $G(x) = \text{Softmax}(W_g \cdot x)$.
- **Zero out** all but the top-$K$ experts (typically $K = 1$ or $K = 2$).
- Renormalize the remaining weights to sum to 1.
- This gives **conditional computation**: total FLOPs grow sub-linearly with $N$.

**Load-Balancing Loss (1 mark)**: To prevent the router from collapsing onto a few experts, an auxiliary loss penalizes uneven routing:
$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{aux}} = \alpha \cdot N \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{N} f_i \cdot P_i$$
where $f_i$ is the fraction of tokens routed to expert $i$ and $P_i$ is the average router probability for that expert. This is used in **Mixtral**, **Switch Transformer**, and **GPT-4** (rumored).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Advanced Architecture Questions**
> 1. **Confusing the VAE KL direction**: Students often write $D_{KL}(p(z) \,\Vert\, q_\phi(z\mid x))$ — the correct direction is $D_{KL}(q_\phi(z\mid x) \,\Vert\, p(z))$. Losing 1 mark instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting the reparameterization trick**: A VAE without the reparameterization trick is *not* a VAE — it's just an encoder/decoder. Examiners will deduct 2 marks.
> 3. **Writing GAN loss without alternating updates**: Stating "minimize $V$ jointly" loses a mark; you must specify **alternating** $D$ and $G$ updates.
> 4. **Diffusion loss without the $\bar{\alpha}_t$ term**: Writing $x_t = \sqrt{1-\beta_t} x_{t-1} + \sqrt{\beta_t}\epsilon$ is correct for *one* step; the *closed-form* $x_t = \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t} x_0 + \sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_t}\epsilon$ is the form examiners expect.
> 5. **MoE without Top-$K$**: Just listing $N$ experts is *not* a Mixture of Experts — sparsity (Top-$K$ routing) is the defining feature. Worth 2 marks.
> 6. **Confusing $d_k$ and $d_{\text{model}}$**: In multi-head attention, $d_k = d_{\text{model}} / h$. Examiners love testing this.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Introduction to Advanced Architectures**

- ☐ **Transformer**: Self-Attention = $\text{Softmax}(QK^{\top}/\sqrt{d_k})V$; $\sqrt{d_k}$ stabilizes variance; **Multi-Head** = parallel $h$ attention subspaces, concat + $W_O$.
- ☐ **Positional Encoding**: Sine-cosine or learned; injected to inject order into permutation-invariant attention.
- ☐ **VAE**: Encoder → $(\mu, \sigma)$ → Reparameterization $z = \mu + \sigma\odot\epsilon$ → Decoder; Loss = Reconstruction $-$ KL.
- ☐ **GAN**: Two-player min-max game; Generator fools Discriminator; $D^* = p_{\text{data}}/(p_{\text{data}} + p_g)$.
- ☐ **Diffusion (DDPM)**: Forward = noising $q(x_t \mid x_{t-1})$; Reverse = learn $\epsilon_\theta(x_t, t)$; Loss = MSE between true and predicted noise.
- ☐ **Score Matching**: Equivalent interpretation of DDPM; $\epsilon_\theta$ approximates the score $\nabla_x \log p(x)$.
- ☐ **MoE**: Sparse conditional computation; Top-$K$ gating (K = 1 or 2); Load-balancing loss prevents expert collapse.
- ☐ **SSM (Mamba)**: Linear-time sequence modeling; selective state-space; O(n) instead of O(n²).
- ☐ **Compute cost comparison**: Transformer $O(n^2 d)$ vs. SSM $O(n d)$ — drives long-context research.
- ☐ **Engineering trade-off**: Diffusion = best quality, slow sampling; GAN = fast, sharp, unstable; VAE = stable, blurry; Transformer = dominant for language.
- ☐ **KTU hot topics**: Reparameterization derivation (3-mark favorite), GAN optimal discriminator proof, DDPM closed-form $x_t$ expression.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT867 - GENERATIVE AI
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Generative AI Models: Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of GANs

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Generative Adversarial Network (GAN):** A deep learning architecture introduced by **Ian Goodfellow and colleagues in 2014**, consisting of two neural networks — a **Generator (G)** and a **Discriminator (D)** — trained simultaneously in a **minimax adversarial game**. The Generator learns to produce synthetic data samples indistinguishable from real data, while the Discriminator learns to distinguish real samples from fake ones. Both networks improve iteratively through backpropagation until the Generator's outputs become statistically indistinguishable from the real data distribution.

Mathematically, a GAN is a pair of functions $G: \mathcal{Z} \to \mathcal{X}$ and $D: \mathcal{X} \to [0,1]$, where:
- $\mathcal{Z}$ is the **latent space** (typically a low-dimensional noise distribution, e.g., $\mathcal{N}(0, I)$).
- $\mathcal{X}$ is the **data space** (e.g., space of images).
- $D(x)$ outputs the probability that input $x$ is a real sample (drawn from the data distribution $p_{\text{data}}$) rather than a generated one.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Forger and the Detective

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Counterfeiter vs. the Art Critic**
> 
> Imagine a **counterfeiter (Generator)** who tries to forge famous paintings, and an **art critic (Discriminator)** whose job is to detect forgeries.
> 
> - Initially, the counterfeiter produces crude, obvious fakes. The critic easily spots them.
> - Each time the critic catches a fake, it provides feedback (a gradient signal) to the counterfeiter about *why* it was detected.
> - Over time, the counterfeiter refines his technique — better brushes, better pigments, better understanding of the master's style.
> - The critic, in turn, is shown real paintings alongside the improved fakes and learns to spot subtler tells.
> - This **adversarial loop** continues until the counterfeiter's paintings are so convincing that the critic can do no better than a **50/50 random guess**.

This iterative, competitive dynamic is the **core engine** of GAN training.

## 1.3 Core Components at a Glance

| Component | Symbol | Input | Output | Goal |
|-----------|--------|-------|--------|------|
| **Generator** | $G$ | Random noise $z \sim p_z(z)$ | Fake sample $G(z)$ | Fool the Discriminator |
| **Discriminator** | $D$ | Real sample $x \sim p_{\text{data}}$ or fake $G(z)$ | Probability $D(x) \in [0,1]$ | Correctly classify real vs. fake |
| **Noise Prior** | $p_z(z)$ | — | Latent vector $z$ | Source of randomness for $G$ |
| **Data Distribution** | $p_{\text{data}}(x)$ | — | Real sample $x$ | Target distribution to mimic |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick-Recall Cue:** When asked to define GANs, always mention **three keywords**: *Generator*, *Discriminator*, and *Adversarial (minimax) training*. Examiners award marks for explicit mention of all three.

## 1.4 Why "Adversarial"?

The word **adversarial** captures the fact that the two networks have **opposing objectives**:

- The Discriminator $D$ wants to **maximize** its reward — correctly distinguishing real from fake.
- The Generator $G$ wants to **minimize** the Discriminator's reward — making its fakes indistinguishable from real data.

This creates a **zero-sum game** formalized as a **minimax objective**, which we will derive rigorously in Section 2.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The GAN Value Function

The complete training objective of a GAN is given by the **value function** $V(D, G)$, first proposed in the seminal 2014 paper:

$$\min_G \max_D V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}(x)}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z(z)}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$$

### 2.1.1 Term-by-Term Interpretation

- **$\mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}(x)}[\log D(x)]$** — Expected log-probability that the Discriminator correctly identifies a **real** sample as real. $D$ wants this to be large (close to 1, so $\log D(x) \approx 0$).
- **$\mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z(z)}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$** — Expected log-probability that the Discriminator correctly identifies a **fake** sample as fake. $D$ wants $D(G(z)) \to 0$, so $\log(1 - 0) = 0$ (maximized).

The Generator, being the **minimizer**, wants $D(G(z)) \to 1$, which would make $\log(1 - D(G(z))) \to -\infty$ — minimizing the second term as desired.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Training Algorithm

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard GAN Training Procedure (per minibatch):**
> 
> 1. **Sample** a minibatch of $m$ noise samples $\{z^{(1)}, z^{(2)}, \ldots, z^{(m)}\}$ from prior $p_z(z)$.
> 2. **Sample** a minibatch of $m$ real samples $\{x^{(1)}, x^{(2)}, \ldots, x^{(m)}\}$ from the data distribution $p_{\text{data}}(x)$.
> 3. **Update the Discriminator** by **ascending** its stochastic gradient:
> $$\nabla_{\theta_d} \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ \log D(x^{(i)}) + \log(1 - D(G(z^{(i)}))) \right]$$
> 4. **Update the Generator** by **descending** its stochastic gradient (using the **non-saturating heuristic** $\log D(G(z))$ instead of $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$):
> $$\nabla_{\theta_g} \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \log D(G(z^{(i)}))$$
> 5. Repeat for a fixed number of iterations or until convergence.

## 2.3 The Optimal Discriminator

For a **fixed** Generator $G$, the optimal Discriminator $D^*(x)$ is derived by maximizing $V(D, G)$ pointwise:

$$D^*(x) = \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)}$$

where $p_g(x)$ is the **implicit distribution** induced by the Generator mapping $G(z)$ when $z \sim p_z$.

### 2.3.1 Derivation Sketch

The objective as a function of $D(x)$ (treating $D$ as a scalar function of $x$) is:

$$V(D, G) = \int_x p_{\text{data}}(x) \log D(x) \, dx + \int_z p_z(z) \log(1 - D(G(z))) \, dz$$

Change of variable in the second integral (with $x = G(z)$, $dx = g(x) \, dx$, i.e., $p_g(x) \, dx$):

$$V(D, G) = \int_x \left[ p_{\text{data}}(x) \log D(x) + p_g(x) \log(1 - D(x)) \right] dx$$

Setting $\frac{\partial V}{\partial D(x)} = 0$:

$$\frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{D(x)} - \frac{p_g(x)}{1 - D(x)} = 0 \implies D^*(x) = \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)}$$

## 2.4 Global Optimality Condition

When $G$ is optimal (i.e., $D$ is fixed at $D^*$), the value function evaluates to:

$$\min_G V(D^*, G) = -\log(4) + 2 \cdot \text{JSD}(p_{\text{data}} \parallel p_g)$$

where **JSD** denotes the **Jensen-Shannon Divergence** between the two distributions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Result:** The global minimum of the GAN training objective is achieved **if and only if** $p_g = p_{\text{data}}$, at which point $D^*(x) = \frac{1}{2}$ everywhere (the Discriminator cannot distinguish real from fake) and $V(D^*, G^*) = -\log(4) \approx -1.386$.

## 2.5 The Non-Saturating Heuristic

Early in training, $D$ can easily reject fake samples, making $D(G(z)) \approx 0$ and thus $\log(1 - D(G(z))) \approx 0$ — providing **vanishingly small gradients** to $G$. To fix this, Goodfellow's practical recommendation is to train $G$ to **maximize** $\log D(G(z))$ instead of minimizing $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$. This yields a strong gradient signal early in training without changing the fixed point.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Master these equations — they are the most-tested formulas in KTU ESE questions on GANs.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Engineering Insight |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **GAN Value Function** | $\min_G \max_D V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$ | Foundation of all GAN variants |
| 2 | **Optimal Discriminator** | $D^*(x) = \dfrac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)}$ | Bayes-optimal classifier under fixed $G$ |
| 3 | **Global Minimum Value** | $V(D^*, G^*) = -\log 4$ | Achieved at $p_g = p_{\text{data}}$ |
| 4 | **Jensen-Shannon Divergence** | $\text{JSD}(P \parallel Q) = \frac{1}{2}\text{KL}(P \parallel M) + \frac{1}{2}\text{KL}(Q \parallel M)$, $M = \frac{P+Q}{2}$ | Bounded $\in [0, \log 2]$; measures similarity between distributions |
| 5 | **Non-Saturating Loss for G** | $\max_G \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z}[\log D(G(z))]$ | Avoids vanishing gradients early in training |
| 6 | **Discriminator Accuracy (at optimum)** | $D^*(x) = 0.5 \ \forall x$ | Indicates Generator has perfectly fooled $D$ |
| 7 | **Generator Parameter Update** | $\theta_g \leftarrow \theta_g + \eta_g \nabla_{\theta_g} \frac{1}{m}\sum_i \log D(G(z^{(i)}))$ | Stochastic gradient ascent for $G$ |
| 8 | **Discriminator Parameter Update** | $\theta_d \leftarrow \theta_d + \eta_d \nabla_{\theta_d} \frac{1}{m}\sum_i \left[\log D(x^{(i)}) + \log(1 - D(G(z^{(i)})))\right]$ | Stochastic gradient ascent for $D$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Note:** In KTU answer sheets, when writing expressions like $\vert D(x) \vert$ or conditional probabilities, always use `\vert` or `\mid` for vertical bars — never the unescaped `\vert` symbol in plain Markdown tables, as it breaks the table renderer.

| Domain | GAN Application | Impact |
|--------|-----------------|--------|
| **Computer Vision** | Image super-resolution, inpainting, style transfer | Enables realistic photo editing (e.g., NVIDIA StyleGAN) |
| **Medical Imaging** | Synthetic MRI/CT scans for data augmentation | Addresses scarcity of labeled medical data |
| **Drug Discovery** | Molecular structure generation (e.g., Insilico Medicine) | Accelerates pharmaceutical R&D |
| **Data Augmentation** | Synthetic samples for training classifiers | Improves model robustness in low-data regimes |
| **3D Modeling** | Texture and mesh synthesis for games/VR | Reduces manual asset creation cost |
| **Audio/Speech** | Voice synthesis, music generation | Powers TTS systems like WaveGAN |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Optimal Discriminator $D^*(x)$

**Given:** The value function with Generator $G$ fixed:

$$V(D, G) = \int_x p_{\text{data}}(x) \log D(x) \, dx + \int_z p_z(z) \log(1 - D(G(z))) \, dz$$

**Step 1: Change of Variable in the Second Integral**

We substitute $x = G(z)$ so that $z = G^{-1}(x)$. Under this substitution, the measure transforms as $p_z(z) \, dz = p_g(x) \, dx$, where $p_g(x)$ is the probability density of the Generator's outputs.

$$V(D, G) = \int_x p_{\text{data}}(x) \log D(x) \, dx + \int_x p_g(x) \log(1 - D(x)) \, dx$$

**Step 2: Combine the Integrals**

$$V(D, G) = \int_x \left[ p_{\text{data}}(x) \log D(x) + p_g(x) \log(1 - D(x)) \right] dx$$

**Step 3: Maximize Pointwise**

Since the integral is a sum of independent contributions at each $x$, we maximize the integrand for each $x$ independently:

$$f(D) = p_{\text{data}}(x) \log D(x) + p_g(x) \log(1 - D(x))$$

**Step 4: Differentiate with Respect to $D(x)$**

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial D} = \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{D(x)} - \frac{p_g(x)}{1 - D(x)}$$

**Step 5: Set the Derivative to Zero and Solve**

$$\frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{D^*(x)} = \frac{p_g(x)}{1 - D^*(x)}$$

$$p_{\text{data}}(x) \left(1 - D^*(x)\right) = p_g(x) D^*(x)$$

$$p_{\text{data}}(x) - p_{\text{data}}(x) D^*(x) = p_g(x) D^*(x)$$

$$p_{\text{data}}(x) = D^*(x) \left[ p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x) \right]$$

$$\boxed{D^*(x) = \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)}}$$

**Step 6: Verify This Is a Maximum**

The second derivative:

$$\frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial D^2} = -\frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{D^2(x)} - \frac{p_g(x)}{(1 - D(x))^2} < 0$$

This is **always negative** (since densities are non-negative and $D(x) \in (0,1)$), confirming the critical point is indeed a **global maximum** with respect to $D$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Global Minimum Value

**Given:** Substituting $D^*(x) = \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)}$ back into $V(D^*, G)$:

$$V(D^*, G) = \int_x p_{\text{data}}(x) \log \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)} \, dx + \int_x p_g(x) \log \frac{p_g(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)} \, dx$$

**Step 1: Add and Subtract $\log 2$ in Each Term to Convert to KL Divergences**

Recall that KL divergence has the form: $\text{KL}(P \parallel Q) = \int P \log \frac{P}{Q}$. We can rewrite:

$$\log \frac{p_{\text{data}}}{p_{\text{data}} + p_g} = \log \frac{p_{\text{data}}}{2 \cdot M} + \log 2$$

where $M = \frac{p_{\text{data}} + p_g}{2}$ is the midpoint distribution.

**Step 2: Apply This to Both Terms**

$$V(D^*, G) = \int_x p_{\text{data}}(x) \log \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{2 M(x)} \, dx + \int_x p_g(x) \log \frac{p_g(x)}{2 M(x)} \, dx$$

$$= \int_x p_{\text{data}}(x) \left[ \log \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{M(x)} - \log 2 \right] dx + \int_x p_g(x) \left[ \log \frac{p_g(x)}{M(x)} - \log 2 \right] dx$$

**Step 3: Separate into KL and Constant Terms**

$$V(D^*, G) = \text{KL}(p_{\text{data}} \parallel M) - \log 2 + \text{KL}(p_g \parallel M) - \log 2$$

**Step 4: Recognize the JSD Definition**

By definition, $\text{JSD}(P \parallel Q) = \frac{1}{2}\text{KL}(P \parallel M) + \frac{1}{2}\text{KL}(Q \parallel M)$. So:

$$\text{KL}(p_{\text{data}} \parallel M) + \text{KL}(p_g \parallel M) = 2 \cdot \text{JSD}(p_{\text{data}} \parallel p_g)$$

**Step 5: Final Result**

$$\boxed{V(D^*, G) = 2 \cdot \text{JSD}(p_{\text{data}} \parallel p_g) - 2\log 2 = -\log 4 + 2 \cdot \text{JSD}(p_{\text{data}} \parallel p_g)}$$

**Step 6: Minimize Over G**

Since $-\log 4$ is a constant, minimizing $V(D^*, G)$ over $G$ is equivalent to minimizing $\text{JSD}(p_{\text{data}} \parallel p_g)$.

- The **JSD is non-negative** and equals **zero if and only if** $p_g = p_{\text{data}}$.
- Therefore, the **global minimum** is $V(D^*, G^*) = -\log 4$, achieved when $p_g = p_{\text{data}}$.

---

## 3.3 Proof That $D^*(x) = 1/2$ at Global Optimum

**Given:** $p_g(x) = p_{\text{data}}(x)$ at the global optimum.

**Step 1: Substitute into the Optimal Discriminator Formula**

$$D^*(x) = \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)} = \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{2 \, p_{\text{data}}(x)} = \frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 2: Interpretation**

The Discriminator outputs exactly $\frac{1}{2}$ for **every** $x$ — equivalent to a **random coin flip**. The Generator has become so good that no deterministic classifier can do better than chance.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analysis: KL vs. JSD

| Property | KL Divergence $\text{KL}(P \parallel Q)$ | JS Divergence $\text{JSD}(P \parallel Q)$ |
|----------|-------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------|
| **Symmetry** | Asymmetric: $\text{KL}(P \parallel Q) \neq \text{KL}(Q \parallel P)$ | Symmetric: $\text{JSD}(P \parallel Q) = \text{JSD}(Q \parallel P)$ |
| **Bounded?** | Unbounded: $\text{KL} \in [0, +\infty)$ | Bounded: $\text{JSD} \in [0, \log 2]$ |
| **Zero when?** | $P = Q$ a.e. | $P = Q$ a.e. |
| **Used in** | Vanilla VAE loss | Vanilla GAN objective |
| **Pitfall** | Can be $\infty$ if $Q(x) = 0$ where $P(x) > 0$ | Locally saturated — gradients vanish when supports don't overlap |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why JSD is preferred for GANs:** Its boundedness and symmetry make it a cleaner optimization target. However, JSD suffers from a **mode-collapsing** problem when $p_{\text{data}}$ and $p_g$ have **disjoint supports** — a key motivation for the **Wasserstein GAN (WGAN)** variant that uses **Wasserstein-1 distance** instead.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level GAN Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    noiseA[Noise Vector z]:::inputNode --> genA[Generator Network G]
    genA --> fakeSample[Fake Sample G of z]:::outputNode
    realSample[Real Sample x]:::inputNode --> concat1{Combine Real and Fake}
    fakeSample --> concat1
    concat1 --> discA[Discriminator Network D]
    discA --> probOut[Probability D of x]:::outputNode
    probOut --> lossFunc[Adversarial Loss Function]:::lossNode
    lossFunc -.Backprop to D.-> discA
    lossFunc -.Backprop to G.-> genA

    classDef inputNode fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0284C7,stroke-width:2px,color:#0C4A6E
    classDef outputNode fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#16A34A,stroke-width:2px,color:#14532D
    classDef lossNode fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#D97706,stroke-width:2px,color:#78350F
```

## 4.2 GAN Training Loop Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start Training]) --> initA[Initialize G and D with random weights]
    initA --> loopStart{For each training iteration}
    loopStart -->|Yes| sampleZ[Sample minibatch of noise z1 to zm from pz]
    sampleZ --> sampleX[Sample minibatch of real x1 to xm from pdata]
    sampleX --> updateD[Update Discriminator: ascend gradient of V of D G]
    updateD --> updateG[Update Generator: descend gradient of non-saturating loss]
    updateG --> checkConv{Convergence check: D output near 0.5}
    checkConv -->|Not converged| loopStart
    checkConv -->|Converged| endA([End Training: G mimics data distribution])

    classDef startEnd fill:#1E3A8A,stroke:#1E40AF,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef processNode fill:#F0F9FF,stroke:#0369A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#0C4A6E
    classDef decisionNode fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#78350F

    class startA,endA startEnd
    class initA,sampleZ,sampleX,updateD,updateG processNode
    class loopStart,checkConv decisionNode
```

## 4.3 Generator–Discriminator Adversarial Interaction

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Z as Noise Source pz
    participant G as Generator G
    participant D as Discriminator D
    participant L as Adversarial Loss
    participant Opt as Optimizer

    Z->>G: Sample latent z
    G->>D: Send fake sample G of z
    Note over D: Also receives real x from pdata
    D->>L: Output D of x for real and D of G of z for fake
    L->>Opt: Compute V of D and G
    Opt->>D: Update theta d to maximize V
    Opt->>G: Update theta g to minimize V
    Note over G,D: Iterative adversarial refinement
    D-->>G: Implicit feedback via gradient
    G-->>D: Increasingly realistic fakes
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Loss Computation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    realData[Real Data Batch x real]:::inputNode
    fakeData[Generated Batch x fake equals G of z]:::outputNode
    dRealOut[D of x real]:::processNode
    dFakeOut[D of x fake]:::processNode
    realLoss[Real Sample Loss: log D of x real]:::lossNode
    fakeLoss[Fake Sample Loss: log 1 minus D of x fake]:::lossNode
    totalD[D Loss equals Real Loss plus Fake Loss]:::lossNode
    gLoss[Generator Loss: log 1 minus D of G of z OR non-saturating log D of G of z]:::lossNode
    gradD[Gradient w.r.t. theta d]:::decisionNode
    gradG[Gradient w.r.t. theta g]:::decisionNode
    updateDOp[Update theta d via Adam or SGD]:::processNode
    updateGOp[Update theta g via Adam or SGD]:::processNode

    realData --> dRealOut --> realLoss --> totalD
    fakeData --> dFakeOut --> fakeLoss --> totalD
    fakeData --> gLoss
    totalD --> gradD --> updateDOp
    gLoss --> gradG --> updateGOp

    classDef inputNode fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0284C7,stroke-width:2px,color:#0C4A6E
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme | Module 2 | 3 Marks]**
> 
> **Define Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs). Explain the roles of the Generator and Discriminator with a suitable example.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Definition (1 mark):** A Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) is a deep learning framework introduced by Ian Goodfellow et al. (2014) that consists of two neural networks — a **Generator (G)** and a **Discriminator (D)** — trained simultaneously in an adversarial setting to learn the underlying distribution of a dataset.

**Generator's Role (1 mark):** The Generator $G$ takes a random noise vector $z$ sampled from a prior distribution $p_z(z)$ (e.g., Gaussian) as input and outputs a synthetic data sample $G(z)$ that mimics real data. Its objective is to **fool** the Discriminator into classifying its outputs as real.

**Discriminator's Role (1 mark):** The Discriminator $D$ takes a sample $x$ (real or generated) as input and outputs a probability $D(x) \in [0,1]$ indicating the likelihood that $x$ is real. Its objective is to **correctly classify** real vs. fake samples.

**Example:** In image generation, $G$ creates synthetic handwritten digits, and $D$ attempts to distinguish real MNIST digits from generated ones. As training progresses, $G$ produces increasingly realistic digits.

---

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme | Module 2 | 3 Marks]**
> 
> **State and explain the GAN value function $V(D, G)$. What does each term represent?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Value Function (1 mark):**

$$\min_G \max_D V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$$

**Term 1 — $\mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}}[\log D(x)]$ (1 mark):** This term represents the expected log-probability that the Discriminator correctly classifies a **real** sample as real. The Discriminator seeks to **maximize** this, driving $D(x) \to 1$ for real data.

**Term 2 — $\mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$ (1 mark):** This represents the expected log-probability that the Discriminator correctly identifies a **generated** sample $G(z)$ as fake. The Discriminator maximizes this, while the Generator minimizes it — wanting $D(G(z)) \to 1$ so that $\log(1 - 1) = -\infty$.

**Combined Meaning (Bonus for clarity):** The $\min_G \max_D$ structure captures the **adversarial nature** — $D$ maximizes, $G$ minimizes — leading to a zero-sum game whose equilibrium is $p_g = p_{\text{data}}$.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme | Module 2 | 14 Marks]**
> 
> **(a)** Derive the optimal Discriminator $D^*(x)$ for a fixed Generator $G$ in a GAN. Show all intermediate steps clearly. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** Explain the phenomenon of **mode collapse** in GANs. Discuss two strategies commonly used to mitigate it. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer for Part (a) — 7 Marks:

**Step 1: Write the Value Function with $G$ Fixed (1 mark):**

$$V(D, G) = \int_x p_{\text{data}}(x) \log D(x) \, dx + \int_z p_z(z) \log(1 - D(G(z))) \, dz$$

**Step 2: Change of Variable in the Second Integral (1 mark):**

Let $x = G(z)$ and $p_z(z) dz = p_g(x) dx$:

$$V(D, G) = \int_x \left[ p_{\text{data}}(x) \log D(x) + p_g(x) \log(1 - D(x)) \right] dx$$

**Step 3: Pointwise Maximization (1 mark):**

For each $x$, define $f(D) = p_{\text{data}}(x) \log D(x) + p_g(x) \log(1 - D(x))$ and maximize.

**Step 4: First Derivative (1 mark):**

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial D} = \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{D(x)} - \frac{p_g(x)}{1 - D(x)} = 0$$

**Step 5: Solve for $D^*(x)$ (2 marks):**

$$p_{\text{data}}(x)(1 - D^*) = p_g(x) D^*$$
$$p_{\text{data}}(x) = D^*(x)[p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)]$$
$$\boxed{D^*(x) = \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)}}$$

**Step 6: Confirm Maximum via Second Derivative Test (1 mark):**

$$\frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial D^2} = -\frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{D^2} - \frac{p_g(x)}{(1-D)^2} < 0$$

Since the second derivative is always negative, this critical point is a global maximum.

---

#### Model Answer for Part (b) — 7 Marks:

**Definition of Mode Collapse (2 marks):**

> **Mode collapse** is a failure mode in GAN training where the Generator learns to produce a **limited variety of samples** — often a single mode or a few modes of the true data distribution — even though the training data is diverse. The Generator "collapses" to producing similar-looking outputs regardless of the input noise $z$.

For example, in MNIST digit generation, a mode-collapsed Generator might produce only the digit "7" regardless of the noise input.

**Mathematical Intuition (2 marks):**

If $p_g$ concentrates on a single mode of $p_{\text{data}}$, the JSD between $p_{\text{data}}$ and $p_g$ may not be minimal. The Generator exploits the fact that fooling $D$ on a small subset of modes requires less effort than matching the full distribution. In practice, $G$ finds a "shortcut" that satisfies the Discriminator without covering the full data manifold.

**Strategy 1: Mini-batch Discrimination (1.5 marks):**

Introduced by Salimans et al. (2016), this technique allows the Discriminator to compare samples within a minibatch and detect lack of diversity. If the Generator produces near-identical samples, the Discriminator can penalize this similarity, encouraging mode coverage.

**Strategy 2: Unrolled GANs (1.5 marks):**

Unrolled GANs (Metz et al., 2016) allow the Generator to "unroll" several future steps of the Discriminator's update during its own update. This gives $G$ a better gradient signal that accounts for $D$'s response, reducing the temptation to collapse onto a few modes.

**Other Acceptable Strategies (any one, for additional credit):**
- **Wasserstein GAN (WGAN):** Uses the Wasserstein-1 distance which provides smoother gradients even when supports are disjoint.
- **Spectral Normalization:** Stabilizes $D$'s Lipschitz constant.
- **PACGAN / Multi-modal Priors:** Conditioning $G$ on class labels to encourage diverse outputs.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative to Question A)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme | Module 2 | 14 Marks]**
> 
> **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the architecture and working of a Generative Adversarial Network. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** Derive the global minimum of the GAN value function and prove that it is achieved when $p_g = p_{\text{data}}$, with $D^*(x) = \frac{1}{2}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer for Part (a) — 7 Marks:

**Architecture Overview (2 marks):**

A GAN consists of two networks trained in opposition:
- **Generator $G$:** A neural network (often with deconvolution/upsampling layers) that transforms a noise vector $z \sim p_z(z)$ into a synthetic sample $G(z) \in \mathcal{X}$.
- **Discriminator $D$:** A binary classifier (typically a CNN for images) that outputs $D(x) \in [0,1]$, the probability that $x$ is real.

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```
[Noise z] → [Generator G] → [Fake Sample G(z)]
                                       ↓
[Real Sample x] → [Discriminator D] ← [Concat Layer]
                              ↓
                  [Probability Output D(x)]
                              ↓
                [Adversarial Loss V(D,G)]
```

**Working — Adversarial Training Loop (2 marks):**

1. **Discriminator Phase:** $D$ is trained to maximize $V(D, G)$ — i.e., to output 1 for real samples and 0 for fakes. Both real and fake samples are passed through $D$ separately, and gradients are backpropagated to update $D$'s weights.
2. **Generator Phase:** $G$ is trained to minimize $V(D, G)$ — i.e., to produce fakes that $D$ classifies as real. Gradients flow back through $D$ (whose weights are frozen during this step) to update $G$.
3. **Iteration:** These two phases alternate until $D$'s output approaches 0.5, indicating $G$'s samples are indistinguishable from real data.

**Non-Saturating Heuristic (1 mark):**

In practice, $G$ is trained to **maximize** $\log D(G(z))$ instead of minimizing $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$ to avoid vanishing gradients when $D$ easily rejects fakes.

---

#### Model Answer for Part (b) — 7 Marks:

**Step 1: Substitute $D^*$ into $V$ (1 mark):**

$$V(D^*, G) = \int_x \left[ p_{\text{data}}(x) \log \frac{p_{\text{data}}}{p_{\text{data}} + p_g} + p_g(x) \log \frac{p_g}{p_{\text{data}} + p_g} \right] dx$$

**Step 2: Introduce Midpoint Distribution $M = (p_{\text{data}} + p_g)/2$ (1 mark):**

$$V(D^*, G) = \int_x \left[ p_{\text{data}} \log \frac{p_{\text{data}}}{2M} + p_g \log \frac{p_g}{2M} \right] dx$$

**Step 3: Expand the Logarithms (1 mark):**

$$= \int_x \left[ p_{\text{data}} \log \frac{p_{\text{data}}}{M} + p_g \log \frac{p_g}{M} \right] dx - 2\log 2$$

**Step 4: Recognize KL Divergence (1 mark):**

$$= \text{KL}(p_{\text{data}} \parallel M) + \text{KL}(p_g \parallel M) - 2\log 2$$

**Step 5: Convert to JSD (1 mark):**

$$V(D^*, G) = 2 \cdot \text{JSD}(p_{\text{data}} \parallel p_g) - 2\log 2$$

$$V(D^*, G) = -\log 4 + 2 \cdot \text{JSD}(p_{\text{data}} \parallel p_g)$$

**Step 6: Minimize Over G (1 mark):**

Since $\text{JSD} \geq 0$ with equality iff $p_{\text{data}} = p_g$:

$$\min_G V(D^*, G) = -\log 4 \quad \text{at} \quad p_g = p_{\text{data}}$$

**Step 7: Show $D^*(x) = 1/2$ (1 mark):**

At the optimum, $p_g = p_{\text{data}}$, so:

$$D^*(x) = \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_{\text{data}}(x)} = \frac{1}{2} \quad \forall x$$

This proves that at the Nash equilibrium, the Discriminator cannot do better than random guessing.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make in GAN Questions — Avoid These to Secure Full Marks!**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the change of variable:** In the optimal discriminator derivation, many students skip the step $p_z(z) \, dz = p_g(x) \, dx$. This is a **mandatory** transition — skipping it costs 1–2 marks.
> 
> 2. **Confusing the roles of $G$ and $D$:** $D$ **maximizes** $V(D,G)$; $G$ **minimizes** it. Writing the opposite is an instant 0.5–1 mark deduction.
> 
> 3. **Ignoring the non-saturating heuristic:** If asked about practical training, students often write only the original minimax form and lose marks for not mentioning the **non-saturating trick** ($\log D(G(z))$ instead of $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$).
> 
> 4. **Not proving the second-order condition:** When deriving $D^*$, you must verify the second derivative is negative to confirm a maximum. Examiners allocate a full mark for this.
> 
> 5. **Forgetting to state the global minimum value:** The answer "$V(D^*, G^*) = -\log 4$" is expected, not just "it converges". Specify the numerical value.
> 
> 6. **Using "GANs" without the year/context:** Always credit Ian Goodfellow (2014) when first defining GANs.
> 
> 7. **Writing `|x|` in tables:** Use `\vert x \vert` in LaTeX within Markdown tables to avoid breaking the table rendering.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Revision — Master These Bullets:**
> 
> ✅ **GANs** were introduced by **Ian Goodfellow et al. in 2014**.
> 
> ✅ A GAN has **two networks**: Generator $G$ (produces fake data) and Discriminator $D$ (classifies real vs. fake).
> 
> ✅ The training objective is a **minimax game**: $\min_G \max_D V(D, G)$.
> 
> ✅ The value function is: $V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$.
> 
> ✅ The **optimal discriminator** is $D^*(x) = \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)}$.
> 
> ✅ The **global minimum** is $V(D^*, G^*) = -\log 4 \approx -1.386$, achieved when $p_g = p_{\text{data}}$.
> 
> ✅ At the optimum, $D^*(x) = \frac{1}{2}$ everywhere (random guessing).
> 
> ✅ GANs minimize the **Jensen-Shannon Divergence (JSD)** between $p_{\text{data}}$ and $p_g$.
> 
> ✅ **JSD** is bounded in $[0, \log 2]$, symmetric, and equals 0 iff the two distributions are identical.
> 
> ✅ The **non-saturating heuristic** replaces $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$ with $\log D(G(z))$ to avoid vanishing gradients in early training.
> 
> ✅ Common failure modes: **Mode collapse** (G produces limited variety), **vanishing gradients**, **training instability**.
> 
> ✅ Mitigations for mode collapse: **Mini-batch discrimination**, **unrolled GANs**, **Wasserstein GAN (WGAN)**, **spectral normalization**, **conditional GANs**.
> 
> ✅ The training loop alternates between **updating $D$** (ascent on $V$) and **updating $G$** (descent on $V$ or ascent on non-saturating loss).
> 
> ✅ Latent vector $z$ is typically sampled from a **Gaussian** $\mathcal{N}(0, I)$ or **uniform** $U(-1, 1)$ distribution.
> 
> ✅ **Applications:** Image synthesis, super-resolution, medical imaging, drug discovery, data augmentation, style transfer.
> 
> ✅ **Nash Equilibrium:** GAN training seeks a Nash equilibrium between $G$ and $D$, but reaching it in practice is non-trivial.
> 
> ✅ **Evaluation metrics:** Inception Score (IS), Fréchet Inception Distance (FID), Kernel Inception Distance (KID).

---

> **End of KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2 — Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Variational Autoencoders (VAEs)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT867 - GENERATIVE AI
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Variational Autoencoders (VAEs)**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Variational Autoencoder (VAE)** is a directed graphical generative model that belongs to the family of **latent variable models**. It leverages **neural networks** as functional approximators to perform **variational inference**, enabling efficient inference and learning in high-dimensional probabilistic models. Introduced by **Kingma and Welling (2013)**, the VAE framework explicitly defines a joint distribution $p_{\theta}(x, z)$ between observed data $x$ and unobserved latent variables $z$, parameterized by $\theta$, and learns it via stochastic gradient descent.

Unlike a deterministic classical autoencoder, a VAE is **probabilistic** — the encoder outputs the parameters of a probability distribution (typically Gaussian) rather than a fixed latent vector. The model is trained by maximizing the **Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO)** of the log-likelihood of the data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> A VAE = **Probabilistic Encoder** $q_{\phi}(z \mid x)$ + **Probabilistic Decoder** $p_{\theta}(x \mid z)$ + **Latent Sampling** + **ELBO Maximization**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **sculptor who wants to learn to carve realistic human faces from clay** (the latent space). Instead of memorizing every single face (overfitting), you learn a *recipe* of facial features: average eye-distance, typical nose length, common skin tones, etc. These averages become your **μ (mu)** — the "expected face features." But you also learn how much these features *vary* across real faces — this becomes **σ (sigma)**. 

When you want to generate a new face, you don't pick one specific memorized face; you **sample from this distribution** ("a face slightly different from average") and then *add creative noise* (the reparameterization trick). This is why a VAE can generate **new, novel samples** — it has learned the underlying *distribution* of data, not just the data points themselves.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:** A VAE compresses data into a smooth, continuous "meaning-space" (latent space), then samples from this space to generate *new* data that resembles the training set.

## 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|------|--------|---------|
| Latent Variable | $z$ | Compressed, abstract representation of input $x$ |
| Encoder | $q_{\phi}(z \mid x)$ | Approximates the true posterior $p(z \mid x)$ |
| Decoder | $p_{\theta}(x \mid z)$ | Reconstructs input from latent code |
| Mean Vector | $\mu$ | Center of the approximate posterior distribution |
| Standard Deviation | $\sigma$ | Spread/uncertainty of the latent distribution |
| KL Divergence | $D_{KL}$ | Measures difference between two probability distributions |
| ELBO | $\mathcal{L}$ | Evidence Lower Bound — objective function maximized |
| Reparameterization | $z = \mu + \sigma \odot \epsilon$ | Trick to allow backpropagation through sampling |

## 1.4 Why VAEs Matter in Generative AI

> [!IMPORTANT]
> VAEs are foundational because they:
> 1. Provide a **principled probabilistic framework** for generative modeling.
> 2. Enable **fast inference** via amortized variational inference.
> 3. Support **smooth interpolation** in the latent space (e.g., morphing one face into another).
> 4. Form the **conceptual bridge** between classical autoencoders and modern diffusion models / LLMs (which use related reparameterization techniques).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Core Problem: Inference in Latent Variable Models

Given a dataset $X = \{x^{(1)}, x^{(2)}, \ldots, x^{(N)}\}$ and a generative model $p_{\theta}(x) = \int p_{\theta}(x \mid z)\, p(z)\, dz$, we want to estimate the parameters $\theta$ by **maximum likelihood estimation**. However, the integral over the latent space $z$ is **intractable** for high-dimensional data.

### The Decomposition (Step-by-Step Logic):

**Step 1 — Marginal Likelihood (what we want):**
$$ \log p_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) = D_{KL}\bigl(q_{\phi}(z \mid x^{(i)}) \Vert p_{\theta}(z \mid x^{(i)})\bigr) + \mathcal{L}\bigl(\theta, \phi;\, x^{(i)}\bigr) $$

- The first term $D_{KL} \geq 0$ is a non-negative divergence.
- Therefore, $\mathcal{L}(\theta, \phi;\, x^{(i)})$ is the **Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO)** on the log-likelihood.

**Step 2 — Rearranging:**
$$ \log p_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) \geq \mathcal{L}\bigl(\theta, \phi;\, x^{(i)}\bigr) = \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(z \mid x)}\!\left[\log p_{\theta}(x \mid z)\right] - D_{KL}\!\bigl(q_{\phi}(z \mid x) \Vert p(z)\bigr) $$

**Step 3 — Two Competing Objectives:**
- **Reconstruction term** $\mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}}[\log p_{\theta}(x \mid z)]$ → encourages accurate reconstruction.
- **Regularization term** $D_{KL}(q_{\phi}(z \mid x) \Vert p(z))$ → forces the latent distribution to stay close to a simple prior $p(z)$ (usually $\mathcal{N}(0, I)$).

**Step 4 — Why this matters:**
Maximizing ELBO simultaneously:
1. Makes the encoder's output distribution resemble a tractable prior (smooth latent space).
2. Makes the decoder reconstruct data accurately (high fidelity).
3. Implicitly minimizes the gap between $q_{\phi}(z \mid x)$ and the true intractable posterior $p_{\theta}(z \mid x)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** ELBO is a *lower bound* — maximizing it is equivalent to maximizing $\log p_{\theta}(x)$ (the model's ability to explain data), but in a *tractable* way.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $p_{\theta}(x) = \int p_{\theta}(x \mid z)\, p(z)\, dz$ | Marginal likelihood (intractable integral) | $x \in \mathbb{R}^{D}$ |
| 2 | $q_{\phi}(z \mid x) = \mathcal{N}\!\bigl(z;\ \mu_{\phi}(x),\, \sigma_{\phi}^{2}(x) I\bigr)$ | Encoder (variational posterior) — diagonal Gaussian | $\mu \in \mathbb{R}^{K}$, $\sigma \in \mathbb{R}^{K}_{+}$ |
| 3 | $p(z) = \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ | Standard Normal prior (isotropic) | $K$-dim, zero mean, unit variance |
| 4 | $p_{\theta}(x \mid z) = \mathcal{N}\!\bigl(x;\, \mu_{\theta}(z),\, \sigma^{2} I\bigr)$ or Bernoulli | Decoder (likelihood) | Image data: Bernoulli; continuous: Gaussian |
| 5 | $z = \mu_{\phi}(x) + \sigma_{\phi}(x) \odot \epsilon,\ \ \epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ | Reparameterization trick | $\odot$ = element-wise product |
| 6 | $\mathcal{L} = \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}}[\log p_{\theta}(x \mid z)] - D_{KL}\!\bigl(q_{\phi}(z \mid x) \Vert p(z)\bigr)$ | ELBO objective | Maximize this |
| 7 | $D_{KL}\!\bigl(\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^{2}) \Vert \mathcal{N}(0, 1)\bigr) = \tfrac{1}{2} \sum_{j=1}^{K}\!\left(\mu_{j}^{2} + \sigma_{j}^{2} - \log \sigma_{j}^{2} - 1\right)$ | Analytical KL for diagonal Gaussians | Per latent dimension $j$ |
| 8 | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{VAE}} = -\text{ReconLoss} + D_{KL}$ (in minimization form) | Total VAE loss | ReconLoss: BCE or MSE |
| 9 | $p_{\theta}(x, z) = p_{\theta}(x \mid z)\, p(z)$ | Joint distribution (factorization) | Generative process |
| 10 | $\nabla_{\phi}\, \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}}[\,f(z)\,] = \mathbb{E}_{\epsilon}\!\left[\nabla_{\phi} f(\mu_{\phi} + \sigma_{\phi} \epsilon)\right]$ | Score-function / reparameterization gradient | Enables SGD |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Note:** Equation 7 (analytical KL) is the *closed-form* KL used in practice — it requires **no sampling** and gives an exact regularizer. Memorize this formula for numerical problems.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

VAEs power several modern systems:
- **Medical Imaging**: Denoising MRI/CT scans, anomaly detection in X-rays.
- **Drug Discovery**: Generating novel molecular structures.
- **Text Generation**: Early language VAEs (though GANs/transformers dominate now).
- **Anomaly Detection**: KL divergence flags out-of-distribution samples.
- **Data Augmentation**: Generating synthetic samples when real data is scarce.
- **Latent-space Interpolation**: Smooth transitions in audio, video, and 3D meshes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Marginal Likelihood to ELBO

**Goal:** Show how the ELBO emerges naturally as a lower bound.

**Step 1.** Write the marginal log-likelihood of a single data point $x^{(i)}$ by introducing an arbitrary distribution $q(z \mid x^{(i)})$:

$$
\log p_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) = \log \int p_{\theta}(x^{(i)}, z)\, dz
$$

**Step 2.** Multiply and divide by $q_{\phi}(z \mid x^{(i)})$:

$$
\log p_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) = \log \int q_{\phi}(z \mid x^{(i)})\, \frac{p_{\theta}(x^{(i)}, z)}{q_{\phi}(z \mid x^{(i)})}\, dz
$$

**Step 3.** Apply the **Jensen's Inequality** (since $\log$ is concave):

$$
\log \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}}\!\left[\frac{p_{\theta}(x^{(i)}, z)}{q_{\phi}(z \mid x^{(i)})}\right] \geq \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}}\!\left[\log \frac{p_{\theta}(x^{(i)}, z)}{q_{\phi}(z \mid x^{(i)})}\right]
$$

**Step 4.** Therefore the ELBO is:

$$
\mathcal{L}\bigl(\theta, \phi;\, x^{(i)}\bigr) = \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(z \mid x^{(i)})}\!\left[\log p_{\theta}(x^{(i)} \mid z) + \log p(z) - \log q_{\phi}(z \mid x^{(i)})\right]
$$

**Step 5.** Separate into two recognizable terms:

$$
\mathcal{L}\bigl(\theta, \phi;\, x^{(i)}\bigr) = \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}}[\log p_{\theta}(x^{(i)} \mid z)] - D_{KL}\!\bigl(q_{\phi}(z \mid x^{(i)}) \Vert p(z)\bigr)
$$

**Interpretation:**
- First term → reconstruction likelihood.
- Second term → prior-matching regularizer.

## 3.2 Derivation: KL Divergence for Diagonal Gaussians

Assume $q_{\phi}(z \mid x) = \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^{2} I)$ and $p(z) = \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ (both diagonal).

**Step 1.** General KL formula for two Gaussians $\mathcal{N}(\mu_{1}, \Sigma_{1})$ and $\mathcal{N}(\mu_{2}, \Sigma_{2})$:

$$
D_{KL} = \tfrac{1}{2}\!\left[\text{tr}(\Sigma_{2}^{-1}\Sigma_{1}) + (\mu_{2} - \mu_{1})^{T}\Sigma_{2}^{-1}(\mu_{1} - \mu_{2}) - d + \log\frac{\vert \Sigma_{2} \vert}{\vert \Sigma_{1} \vert}\right]
$$

**Step 2.** Substitute $\mu_{1} = \mu$, $\Sigma_{1} = \text{diag}(\sigma_{1}^{2}, \ldots, \sigma_{K}^{2})$, $\mu_{2} = 0$, $\Sigma_{2} = I$:

$$
D_{KL} = \tfrac{1}{2}\!\left[\sum_{j=1}^{K}\sigma_{j}^{2} + \sum_{j=1}^{K}\mu_{j}^{2} - K - \sum_{j=1}^{K}\log \sigma_{j}^{2}\right]
$$

**Step 3.** Rearrange in standard textbook form:

$$
D_{KL}\!\bigl(\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^{2}) \Vert \mathcal{N}(0, 1)\bigr) = -\tfrac{1}{2}\sum_{j=1}^{K}\!\left(1 + \log \sigma_{j}^{2} - \mu_{j}^{2} - \sigma_{j}^{2}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Equivalently, the formulation used in code: $\tfrac{1}{2} \sum_{j=1}^{K}(\mu_{j}^{2} + \sigma_{j}^{2} - \log \sigma_{j}^{2} - 1)$. Both forms are identical; the minus sign cancels due to log identities.

## 3.3 Derivation: The Reparameterization Trick

**Problem:** Sampling $z \sim q_{\phi}(z \mid x) = \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^{2} I)$ is a **stochastic operation** — gradients cannot flow through the random node.

**Step 1.** Express the sample as a deterministic function of parameters and external noise:

$$
z = g_{\phi}(\epsilon, x) = \mu_{\phi}(x) + \sigma_{\phi}(x) \odot \epsilon,\quad \epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)
$$

**Step 2.** The expectation now becomes a deterministic function of $\phi$:

$$
\mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(z \mid x)}[\,f(z)\,] = \mathbb{E}_{\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0,I)}[\,f(\mu_{\phi}(x) + \sigma_{\phi}(x) \odot \epsilon)\,]
$$

**Step 3.** Monte Carlo estimate with one sample $L = 1$:

$$
\nabla_{\phi}\, \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}}[\,f(z)\,] \approx \nabla_{\phi}\, f(\mu_{\phi}(x) + \sigma_{\phi}(x) \odot \epsilon)
$$

**Why this works:**
- The randomness is "outsourced" to the noise variable $\epsilon$ (which has no learnable parameters).
- The chain rule of backpropagation now works smoothly through $\mu_{\phi}(x)$ and $\sigma_{\phi}(x)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The reparameterization trick is the single most important idea that makes VAEs trainable with backpropagation. Always draw a small computation graph in the answer.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: KL Divergence

**Problem:** Compute $D_{KL}\!\bigl(\mathcal{N}(0.5,\, 0.8^{2}) \Vert \mathcal{N}(0, 1)\bigr)$.

**Step 1.** Identify parameters: $\mu = 0.5$, $\sigma = 0.8$ (so $\sigma^{2} = 0.64$).

**Step 2.** Apply the closed-form formula:

$$
D_{KL} = \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl(\mu^{2} + \sigma^{2} - \log\sigma^{2} - 1\bigr)
$$

**Step 3.** Substitute values:

$$
D_{KL} = \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl(0.5^{2} + 0.64 - \log(0.64) - 1\bigr)
$$

**Step 4.** Evaluate each term:

- $\mu^{2} = 0.25$
- $\sigma^{2} = 0.64$
- $\log(0.64) \approx -0.4463$
- $-1$

**Step 5.** Sum and halve:

$$
D_{KL} = \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl(0.25 + 0.64 + 0.4463 - 1\bigr) = \tfrac{1}{2}(0.3363) \approx 0.1681
$$

**Answer:** $D_{KL} \approx 0.168$ nats.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: VAE Variants and Extensions

| Variant | Modification | Application |
|---------|--------------|-------------|
| **Vanilla VAE** | Standard Gaussian latent space, ELBO objective | Image generation baseline |
| **β-VAE** | Weighted KL term: $\mathcal{L} = \text{Recon} - \beta D_{KL}$ | Disentangled representation learning |
| **Conditional VAE (CVAE)** | Condition encoder/decoder on label $y$ | Class-conditional generation |
| **VQ-VAE** | Discrete latent codes via vector quantization | High-fidelity image/audio |
| **IWAE** | Importance-weighted ELBO with multiple samples | Tighter log-likelihood bound |
| **Hierarchical VAE** | Multiple latent layers (Ladder VAE) | Complex structured data |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 VAE Architecture — End-to-End Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["INPUT DATA X"] --> B["ENCODER NETWORK q_phi z given x"]
    B --> C["LATENT MEAN mu_phi x"]
    B --> D["LATENT LOG VARIANCE log sigma squared phi x"]
    C --> E["SAMPLING LAYER reparameterization trick"]
    D --> E
    F["NOISE epsilon from N 0 I"] --> E
    E --> G["LATENT CODE z"]
    G --> H["DECODER NETWORK p_theta x given z"]
    H --> I["RECONSTRUCTION x hat"]
    I --> J["RECONSTRUCTION LOSS MSE or BCE"]
    C --> K["KL DIVERGENCE TERM analytic Gaussian"]
    D --> K
    J --> L["TOTAL VAE LOSS = Reconstruction + KL"]
    K --> L
    L --> M["BACKPROPAGATION update theta and phi"]

    style A fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1976d2,color:#000
    style F fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#f57c00,color:#000
    style G fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#7b1fa2,color:#000
    style L fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#388e3c,color:#000
    style M fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#c2185b,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Training Loop — Conceptual Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TRAINING_PHASE["TRAINING PHASE"]
        A1["Sample minibatch X from dataset"] --> A2["Encode: get mu, log_var"]
        A2 --> A3["Sample epsilon from N 0 I"]
        A3 --> A4["Compute z = mu + sigma * epsilon"]
        A4 --> A5["Decode: get x_hat from z"]
        A5 --> A6["Compute ELBO loss"]
        A6 --> A7["Backprop and update weights"]
        A7 --> A1
    end

    subgraph GENERATION_PHASE["GENERATION PHASE"]
        B1["Sample z from prior N 0 I"] --> B2["Decode z using trained decoder"]
        B2 --> B3["Output new sample x_hat"]
    end

    style TRAINING_PHASE fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,color:#000
    style GENERATION_PHASE fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#ff6f00,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Mathematical Dependency Graph (ELBO Components)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["ELBO L"] --> B["Reconstruction Term E q phi log p theta x given z"]
    A --> C["Regularization Term negative D KL q phi z given x || p z"]
    B --> B1["Decoder network output p theta"]
    B --> B2["Sampled latent z from reparameterization"]
    C --> C1["Encoder mean mu phi"]
    C --> C2["Encoder variance sigma squared phi"]
    C --> C3["Prior p z = N 0 I"]
    B2 --> B1
    C1 --> D["Total Loss for backprop"]
    C2 --> D
    B1 --> D
    D --> E["SGD update on parameters theta and phi"]

    style A fill:#e8eaf6,stroke:#283593,color:#000
    style D fill:#ffebee,stroke:#c62828,color:#000
    style E fill:#e0f2f1,stroke:#00695c,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Reparameterization Trick — Computation Graph

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X["Input x"] --> ENC["Encoder NN"]
    ENC --> MU["mu x"]
    ENC --> SIG["sigma x"]
    EPS["epsilon ~ N 0, I"] --> MULT["z = mu + sigma * epsilon"]
    SIG --> MULT
    MU --> MULT
    MULT --> DEC["Decoder NN"]
    DEC --> OUT["x_hat reconstruction"]

    style EPS fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    style MULT fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000
    style OUT fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
```

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 Marks each — Short Answer)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 2]`
**Define Variational Autoencoders (VAEs). How do they differ from classical autoencoders?**

**Model Answer:**

A **Variational Autoencoder (VAE)** is a generative model that learns a *probabilistic* mapping between a high-dimensional data space and a lower-dimensional latent space, trained by maximizing the **Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO)** using variational inference.

**Differences from classical autoencoders:**

| Aspect | Classical Autoencoder | Variational Autoencoder |
|--------|----------------------|-------------------------|
| Latent space | Deterministic point | Probability distribution |
| Output of encoder | A vector $z$ | Parameters $\mu, \sigma$ of a distribution |
| Sampling | Not possible | $z$ sampled from learned distribution |
| Training objective | MSE reconstruction loss | ELBO (Reconstruction + KL Divergence) |
| Generation | Not directly generative | Generates new samples by sampling prior |

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 2]`
**What is the reparameterization trick in VAEs? Why is it necessary?**

**Model Answer:**

The **reparameterization trick** expresses the latent sample $z$ as a deterministic function of the encoder's output and an external noise variable $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$:

$$
z = \mu_{\phi}(x) + \sigma_{\phi}(x) \odot \epsilon
$$

**Why it is necessary:**
1. Direct sampling $z \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_{\phi}(x), \sigma_{\phi}^{2}(x))$ is a **non-differentiable stochastic operation**, which blocks backpropagation.
2. By outsourcing randomness to $\epsilon$ (which has no learnable parameters), the computation graph becomes fully differentiable.
3. This allows the model to be trained end-to-end using standard **stochastic gradient descent** and the reparameterization gradient estimator.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

---

### 📝 Question A (14 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 | Module 2 | 14 Marks]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO) for a Variational Autoencoder starting from the marginal log-likelihood. Clearly identify the two terms and their roles.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a 2-dimensional latent space, the encoder outputs $\mu = [0.4,\ -0.3]$ and $\sigma = [0.9,\ 0.7]$. Compute the KL divergence $D_{KL}(q_{\phi}(z \mid x) \Vert p(z))$ where $p(z) = \mathcal{N}(0, I)$. Also explain why minimizing this term creates a "smooth" latent space.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) ELBO Derivation [7 Marks]**

We start with the marginal log-likelihood of data point $x^{(i)}$:

$$
\log p_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) = \log \int p_{\theta}(x^{(i)}, z)\, dz \quad \text{...(1)}
$$

Introduce an arbitrary variational distribution $q_{\phi}(z \mid x^{(i)})$ and use the identity $1 = \tfrac{q_{\phi}(z \mid x^{(i)})}{q_{\phi}(z \mid x^{(i)})}$:

$$
\log p_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) = \log \int q_{\phi}(z \mid x^{(i)}) \cdot \frac{p_{\theta}(x^{(i)}, z)}{q_{\phi}(z \mid x^{(i)})}\, dz \quad \text{...(2)}
$$

This integral is an expectation:

$$
\log p_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) = \log\, \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}}\!\left[\frac{p_{\theta}(x^{(i)}, z)}{q_{\phi}(z \mid x^{(i)})}\right] \quad \text{...(3)}
$$

Apply **Jensen's inequality** ($\log$ is concave):

$$
\log p_{\theta}(x^{(i)}) \geq \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}}\!\left[\log \frac{p_{\theta}(x^{(i)}, z)}{q_{\phi}(z \mid x^{(i)})}\right] \quad \text{...(4)}
$$

The right-hand side is the **ELBO** $\mathcal{L}$:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\theta, \phi; x^{(i)}) = \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(z \mid x^{(i)})}\!\left[\log p_{\theta}(x^{(i)} \mid z) + \log p(z) - \log q_{\phi}(z \mid x^{(i)})\right] \quad \text{...(5)}
$$

Expanding using the factorization $p_{\theta}(x,z) = p_{\theta}(x \mid z)p(z)$:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\theta, \phi; x^{(i)}) = \underbrace{\mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}}[\log p_{\theta}(x^{(i)} \mid z)]}_{\text{Reconstruction term}} - \underbrace{D_{KL}(q_{\phi}(z \mid x^{(i)}) \Vert p(z))}_{\text{Regularization term}} \quad \text{...(6)}
$$

**Roles:**
- **Reconstruction term:** Forces the decoder to accurately reconstruct $x^{(i)}$ from $z$.
- **KL term:** Forces the encoder's output to remain close to the prior $p(z) = \mathcal{N}(0,I)$, ensuring a smooth, continuous latent space.

---

**(b) Numerical KL Computation [7 Marks]**

**Step 1.** Apply the analytical formula for diagonal Gaussian KL:

$$
D_{KL} = \tfrac{1}{2}\sum_{j=1}^{K}\!\left(\mu_{j}^{2} + \sigma_{j}^{2} - \log \sigma_{j}^{2} - 1\right)
$$

**Step 2.** Compute per-dimension terms:

- **Dimension 1:** $\mu_{1} = 0.4$, $\sigma_{1} = 0.9$, $\sigma_{1}^{2} = 0.81$
$$
\tfrac{1}{2}(0.4^{2} + 0.81 - \log(0.81) - 1) = \tfrac{1}{2}(0.16 + 0.81 + 0.2107 - 1) = \tfrac{1}{2}(0.1807) = 0.0904
$$

- **Dimension 2:** $\mu_{2} = -0.3$, $\sigma_{2} = 0.7$, $\sigma_{2}^{2} = 0.49$
$$
\tfrac{1}{2}((-0.3)^{2} + 0.49 - \log(0.49) - 1) = \tfrac{1}{2}(0.09 + 0.49 + 0.7133 - 1) = \tfrac{1}{2}(0.2933) = 0.1467
$$

**Step 3.** Sum both dimensions:

$$
D_{KL} = 0.0904 + 0.1467 \approx 0.2371 \text{ nats}
$$

**Smooth latent space explanation [3 Marks]:**

Minimizing the KL term forces every encoder output $q_{\phi}(z \mid x)$ to overlap with the standard normal prior $\mathcal{N}(0, I)$. As a result:
1. The latent codes for different inputs are forced to lie on a *continuous* manifold around the origin.
2. There are no "gaps" or "holes" in the latent space.
3. Therefore, sampling any $z \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ produces a *meaningful* decoded output — enabling smooth interpolation between data points.

---

### 📝 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 2 | 14 Marks]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the architecture of a Variational Autoencoder in detail. Describe the role of the encoder, the sampling layer, and the decoder. Use a labelled block diagram.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the reparameterization trick mathematically and discuss its significance in training VAEs. What would happen if we did not use this trick?

**Model Solution:**

**(a) VAE Architecture [7 Marks]**

A VAE consists of three main components connected sequentially:

**1. Encoder Network $q_{\phi}(z \mid x)$:**
- Input: Data $x \in \mathbb{R}^{D}$ (e.g., a flattened image).
- Architecture: Typically a Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) or Convolutional Neural Network (CNN).
- Output: Two vectors $\mu_{\phi}(x) \in \mathbb{R}^{K}$ and $\log \sigma_{\phi}^{2}(x) \in \mathbb{R}^{K}$ (using log-variance for numerical stability).

**2. Sampling Layer (Reparameterization Layer):**
- Receives $\mu$, $\sigma$ from the encoder and noise $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$.
- Computes latent code $z = \mu + \sigma \odot \epsilon$.
- This layer is the *bridge* between the deterministic encoder and the differentiable decoder.

**3. Decoder Network $p_{\theta}(x \mid z)$:**
- Input: Latent code $z \in \mathbb{R}^{K}$.
- Architecture: MLP/CNN (mirror of encoder).
- Output: Reconstruction $x$ (Bernoulli probabilities for binary images, or Gaussian mean for continuous data).

```
        ┌─────────────────┐
   x →  │  Encoder q_phi  │ → mu, log_var
        └─────────────────┘
                │
                ▼
        ┌─────────────────┐
        │  Sample z using │  ← epsilon ~ N(0, I)
        │  Reparameterize │
        └─────────────────┘
                │
                ▼ z
        ┌─────────────────┐
        │ Decoder p_theta │ → x_hat
        └─────────────────┘
```

**Loss Computation:**
$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{VAE}} = -\mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}}[\log p_{\theta}(x \mid z)] + D_{KL}(q_{\phi}(z \mid x) \Vert p(z))
$$

(Minimization form; equivalent to maximizing ELBO.)

---

**(b) Reparameterization Trick Derivation & Significance [7 Marks]**

**Derivation:**

We wish to compute the gradient $\nabla_{\phi}\, \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(z \mid x)}[f(z)]$.

**Without reparameterization (naïve Monte Carlo + score function):**

$$
\nabla_{\phi}\, \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}}[f(z)] = \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}}[f(z)\, \nabla_{\phi} \log q_{\phi}(z \mid x)]
$$

This estimator has **very high variance** — making training unstable and slow.

**With reparameterization:** Write $z = g_{\phi}(\epsilon, x) = \mu_{\phi}(x) + \sigma_{\phi}(x) \odot \epsilon$ where $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$.

The expectation is rewritten as:

$$
\mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(z \mid x)}[f(z)] = \mathbb{E}_{\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)}[f(\mu_{\phi}(x) + \sigma_{\phi}(x) \odot \epsilon)]
$$

Now the gradient flows *only* through the deterministic path $\mu_{\phi}(x)$ and $\sigma_{\phi}(x)$:

$$
\nabla_{\phi}\, \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}}[f(z)] = \mathbb{E}_{\epsilon}\!\left[\nabla_{\phi} f(\mu_{\phi}(x) + \sigma_{\phi}(x) \odot \epsilon)\right]
$$

Monte Carlo estimate with $L$ samples:

$$
\nabla_{\phi}\, \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}}[f(z)] \approx \tfrac{1}{L}\sum_{l=1}^{L} \nabla_{\phi} f(\mu_{\phi}(x) + \sigma_{\phi}(x) \odot \epsilon^{(l)})
$$

**Significance:**
1. **Low variance gradients** — training converges much faster than score-function estimators.
2. **Fully differentiable pipeline** — standard SGD/backprop works.
3. **End-to-end training** — encoder and decoder parameters $\phi, \theta$ update jointly.

**Without the trick:**
- Gradient cannot propagate through the stochastic sampling node.
- Training becomes infeasible with standard backpropagation.
- Alternative score-function estimators have prohibitive variance for high-dimensional latent spaces.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
>
> 1. **Sign confusion:** Many students write the ELBO loss in *minimization* form as $\mathcal{L} = -\text{Recon} + D_{KL}$ but forget to negate the KL term correctly. The KL is *subtracted* in maximization and *added* (positive) in minimization. Getting this sign wrong is the **#1 cause of full-mark loss**.
>
> 2. **KL formula units:** $D_{KL}$ for natural-log is in *nats*; for $\log_{2}$ it is in *bits*. KTU expects *nats* unless stated otherwise.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the reconstruction term:** Some students only explain the KL divergence. Both terms are essential — explain **both** for full credit.
>
> 4. **Reparameterization in words only:** You must write the equation $z = \mu + \sigma \odot \epsilon$ explicitly. A verbal-only explanation will fetch only 2 of 7 marks in the derivation sub-part.
>
> 5. **Mixing VAE with GAN:** Do not confuse the adversarial training of GANs with the variational inference framework of VAEs. They are fundamentally different.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — VAEs**

- ✅ VAE = **Probabilistic** autoencoder trained via **variational inference**.
- ✅ Encoder outputs distribution parameters $(\mu, \sigma)$; decoder reconstructs $x$ from $z$.
- ✅ Latent prior: $p(z) = \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ (standard normal).
- ✅ Objective: **Maximize ELBO** = Reconstruction Likelihood − KL Divergence.
- ✅ Reparameterization: $z = \mu + \sigma \odot \epsilon$ — essential for backprop.
- ✅ KL for diagonal Gaussians has **closed form** (no sampling needed).
- ✅ Reconstruction loss: **BCE** (binary data) or **MSE** (continuous data).
- ✅ VAEs produce **blurry** samples compared to GANs but offer **smooth latent interpolation**.
- ✅ Variants to remember: **β-VAE** (weighted KL), **CVAE** (conditional), **VQ-VAE** (discrete).
- ✅ ELBO is a **lower bound** on $\log p_{\theta}(x)$ — tighter is better.
- ✅ ELBO $= \log p_{\theta}(x) - D_{KL}(q_{\phi} \Vert p_{\theta})$ — gap = posterior approximation error.
- ✅ Sampling during generation: $z \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I) \to$ decode $\to$ new sample.
- ✅ Amortized inference: a *single* encoder network serves *all* data points.
- ✅ KTU favourite numerical problem: compute KL for given $(\mu, \sigma)$ — memorize the formula!

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
