## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define the Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network. List its three gates and state the primary problem it solves compared to a vanilla RNN.**

**Model Answer:**

Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) is a recurrent neural network architecture designed to capture long-term dependencies in sequential data by introducing a memory cell and three gating mechanisms.

The three gates are:
1. **Forget Gate ($f_t$):** Decides what information to discard from the cell state.
2. **Input Gate ($i_t$):** Decides what new information to store in the cell state.
3. **Output Gate ($o_t$):** Decides what parts of the cell state to output as the hidden state.

The primary problem it solves is the **vanishing and exploding gradient problem** in vanilla RNNs, enabling the network to learn dependencies across long sequences.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for listing gates, 1 for stating the problem solved.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is the role of the forget gate in an LSTM cell? Write its mathematical formulation.**

**Model Answer:**

The **forget gate** controls the extent to which information from the previous cell state $C_{t-1}$ is retained or discarded. It outputs values between 0 and 1 for each element of $C_{t-1}$ — a value of 0 means "completely forget," and a value of 1 means "completely retain."

Mathematical formulation:

$$
f_t = \sigma\!\left(W_f \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f\right)
$$

where $\sigma$ is the sigmoid function, $W_f$ is the weight matrix, $b_f$ is the bias vector, $h_{t-1}$ is the previous hidden state, and $x_t$ is the current input.

*(3 marks — 1.5 for role, 1.5 for equation.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the complete architecture of an LSTM cell with a neat diagram. Describe the function of each gate with its corresponding mathematical expression. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive mathematically how the LSTM cell state update mechanism mitigates the vanishing gradient problem in vanilla RNNs. Compare the gradient flow in both architectures. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The LSTM cell is the fundamental building block of LSTM networks. It maintains two state vectors — the **cell state** $C_t$ (long-term memory) and the **hidden state** $h_t$ (short-term output/work memory) — and uses three gates to regulate information flow.

**Architecture Description (3 marks):**
At each time step $t$, the LSTM receives:
- Current input $x_t$
- Previous hidden state $h_{t-1}$
- Previous cell state $C_{t-1}$

The cell then computes, in sequence:

1. **Forget Gate:** Determines which parts of $C_{t-1}$ to discard.
2. **Input Gate and Candidate:** Determines which new information to add.
3. **Cell State Update:** Combines the forgotten old memory and the new candidate.
4. **Output Gate:** Computes the new hidden state from the cell state.

**Mathematical Expressions (3 marks):**

$$
f_t = \sigma(W_f [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)
$$

$$
i_t = \sigma(W_i [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i)
$$

$$
\tilde{C}_t = \tanh(W_C [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C)
$$

$$
C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t
$$

$$
o_t = \sigma(W_o [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o)
$$

$$
h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)
$$

**Conclusion (must mention for full marks):**
The additive structure in the cell state update equation, gated by sigmoidal controls, is the key innovation that enables stable gradient propagation across long time sequences.

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Vanilla RNN Gradient (3 marks):**

For a vanilla RNN with $h_t = \tanh(W_{hh} h_{t-1} + W_{xh} x_t + b_h)$, the gradient of the hidden state w.r.t. its previous value involves the weight matrix $W_{hh}$:

$$
\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{t-1}} = \text{diag}(1 - \tanh^2(\cdot)) \cdot W_{hh}^{\top}
$$

Chaining across $k$ steps:

$$
\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_k} = \prod_{j=k+1}^{t} \text{diag}(1 - \tanh^2(\cdot)) \cdot W_{hh}^{\top}
$$

Repeated multiplication by $W_{hh}$ causes exponential decay (if $\|W_{hh}\| < 1$) or growth (if $\|W_{hh}\| > 1$).

**LSTM Gradient Flow Derivation (3 marks):**

The cell state update is $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$. Taking the partial derivative w.r.t. $C_{t-1}$:

$$
\frac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_{t-1}} = f_t
$$

Chaining across $k$ steps:

$$
\frac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_k} = \prod_{j=k+1}^{t} f_j
$$

Since each $f_j \in [0, 1]$, the product is bounded in $[0, 1]$. When the network learns $f_j \approx 1$ (encouraged by initializing $b_f$ to a positive value), the product remains close to 1, allowing gradients to flow.

**Comparison and Conclusion (1 mark):**

| Aspect | Vanilla RNN | LSTM |
|--------|-------------|------|
| Gradient product involves | $W_{hh}$ (fixed, may explode/vanish) | $f_t$ (learnable, bounded) |
| Gradient control | Passive (none) | Active (per-step, per-dimension) |

The LSTM's learnable forget gate provides explicit gradient control, solving the vanishing gradient problem.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the unrolled structure of an LSTM network across multiple time steps. Discuss how the hidden state and cell state propagate through time. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the following LSTM variants: Peephole LSTM, Coupled (CIFG) LSTM, and Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU). For each, explain the architectural modification and a suitable use case. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Unrolled LSTM Diagram (2 marks):**
The unrolled structure shows the LSTM cell repeated for each time step $t = 1, 2, \ldots, T$, with the same set of weights shared across all time steps. At each step:
- Input $x_t$ enters the cell.
- The previous hidden state $h_{t-1}$ and cell state $C_{t-1}$ arrive from the prior step.
- The cell outputs the new hidden state $h_t$ and cell state $C_t$.

**Propagation of Hidden and Cell State (3 marks):**

- **Hidden state $h_t$** is passed to the next time step and also serves as the output for tasks like sequence labelling or as input to a dense layer for next-token prediction.
- **Cell state $C_t$** is passed *only* to the next time step's LSTM cell. It is the **long-term memory conveyor belt** — it carries information across time, modulated by the gates, without being directly exposed as an output.

**Parameter Sharing (1 mark):**
The same weight matrices $W_f, W_i, W_C, W_o$ and biases $b_f, b_i, b_C, b_o$ are used at every time step. This is what gives RNNs/LSTMs their ability to generalize across variable-length sequences.

**Application Mention (1 mark):**
In language modelling, the hidden state at each step is fed to a softmax layer to predict the next word; in sequence labelling, the hidden state at each step is fed to a classifier for per-token prediction.

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Peephole LSTM (2.5 marks):**
- **Modification:** The gates are allowed to "peek" at the cell state. The forget and input gate equations are modified to include $C_{t-1}$ (and the output gate includes $C_t$):

$$
f_t = \sigma(W_f \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + v_f \odot C_{t-1} + b_f)
$$

- **Use Case:** Tasks requiring precise timing and interval measurement, such as **music generation** (where note durations matter) and **speech recognition** (where phoneme durations are informative).

**Coupled (CIFG) LSTM (2.5 marks):**
- **Modification:** The forget gate and input gate are coupled: $f_t = 1 - i_t$. This reduces the parameter count by 25%, since one less gate weight matrix is needed.
- **Use Case:** Resource-constrained deployment, edge devices, and scenarios where faster training is desired with minimal performance loss.

**Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) (2 marks):**
- **Modification:** GRU merges the cell state and hidden state into a single state $h_t$, and combines the forget and input gates into a single **update gate** $z_t$, with a separate **reset gate** $r_t$ controlling how much of the past to ignore.
- **Use Case:** A lightweight alternative to LSTM, often used when training data is limited or when faster convergence is desired; commonly used in encoder–decoder models.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Conflating Input Gate with Candidate Cell State:** Many students mistakenly state that the input gate $i_t$ is the new memory. It is **not** — it is a **sigmoid gate** that *controls* how much of the candidate $\tilde{C}_t$ to add. Always distinguish these.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention the additive nature of the cell state update:** The cell state uses **addition** (not multiplication) for new information, which is the structural reason for gradient stability. Examiners explicitly look for this.
> 3. **Confusing the cell state and hidden state:** $C_t$ is the **long-term memory**; $h_t$ is the **output/work memory**. They are different vectors with different roles.
> 4. **Skipping the diagram:** A question on LSTM architecture without a diagram will lose 1–2 marks. Always include the gate-level and unrolled-time diagrams.
> 5. **Not initializing the forget gate bias:** A key practical detail — initializing $b_f$ to a positive value (e.g., 1) helps the network start by retaining information. Examiners appreciate this depth.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for LSTMs:**

- ✅ LSTM = RNN + Memory Cell + 3 Gates (Forget, Input, Output)
- ✅ The **Cell State $C_t$** is the long-term memory conveyor belt; the **Hidden State $h_t$** is the short-term work memory.
- ✅ **Forget Gate** ($f_t = \sigma(\cdot)$, range $[0,1]$) decides what to discard.
- ✅ **Input Gate** ($i_t = \sigma(\cdot)$, range $[0,1]$) decides what to add.
- ✅ **Candidate $\tilde{C}_t$** ($\tanh$, range $[-1,1]$) is the new memory content.
- ✅ **Cell State Update:** $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ — note the **additive** structure.
- ✅ **Output Gate** ($o_t = \sigma(\cdot)$) decides what to expose.
- ✅ **Hidden State:** $h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$.
- ✅ **Key Result:** $\partial C_t / \partial C_{t-1} = f_t$, so $\partial C_t / \partial C_k = \prod_{j=k+1}^t f_j \in [0,1]$ — bounded, learnable gradient flow.
- ✅ **Initialization Tip:** Initialize forget gate bias $b_f = 1$ (or positive) to encourage initial memory retention.
- ✅ **Variants:** Vanilla, Peephole, Coupled (CIFG), GRU, BiLSTM, Stacked LSTM.
- ✅ **Modern Relevance:** LSTMs preceded Transformers; still preferred in streaming, low-resource, or low-latency settings.
- ✅ **Common Applications:** Machine translation, speech recognition, time-series forecasting, music generation, video captioning.
- ✅ **KTU Board Tip:** Always draw the **gate-level LSTM diagram** AND the **unrolled-in-time diagram** when architecture is asked.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Preprocessing Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT867 - GENERATIVE AI
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Data Preprocessing Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Data Preprocessing** is the foundational phase in the Generative AI / Machine Learning pipeline wherein raw, unstructured, noisy, and heterogeneous datasets are transformed into a clean, consistent, numerical, and model-ready representation. In the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned framework for PERAT867, data preprocessing is formally defined as the systematic application of cleaning, integration, transformation, reduction, and encoding operations that maximize **information density** while eliminating bias, redundancy, and noise that would otherwise degrade the convergence and generative fidelity of models such as GANs, VAEs, and Diffusion Models.

The quality of the input data directly determines the upper bound of a generative model's performance — a principle often phrased as **"Garbage In, Garbage Out (GIGO)"**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Chef's Kitchen

Imagine a Michelin-star chef preparing a complex dish (the Generative Model).

| Kitchen Stage | AI Pipeline Equivalent | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Washing vegetables, peeling skin | Data Cleaning | Remove impurities & noise |
| Chopping ingredients into uniform cubes | Data Transformation / Normalization | Standardize format |
| Measuring exact grams on a scale | Data Scaling (Min-Max, Z-score) | Equal feature importance |
| Discarding rotten tomatoes | Outlier Removal | Improve statistical reliability |
| Marinating ingredients to absorb flavor | Feature Engineering | Enhance signal learning |
| Blending spices into a paste | Data Integration | Combine heterogeneous sources |
| Reducing gravy to concentrate taste | Data Reduction (PCA, Sampling) | Remove redundancy |

Just as a chef cannot produce a gourmet meal from unwashed, unmeasured ingredients, a Generative AI model cannot produce high-fidelity outputs from raw, unprocessed data.

### 1.3 Core Syllabus Highlights

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme — Module 3 Anchor Definition**
> Data Preprocessing is the *gatekeeper* stage of every generative pipeline. As per the revised Bloom's Taxonomy mapping for PERAT867, students must demonstrate **Apply (L3)** and **Analyze (L4)** level competency by being able to: (i) identify appropriate preprocessing techniques for a given dataset, (ii) compute normalized/standardized values, and (iii) justify the choice of encoder/decoder preprocessing for categorical or image data.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metric**
> In KTU board examinations, the **mean (μ)** and **standard deviation (σ)** are denoted using Greek letters and carry **2 marks** weightage individually when computing standardization. The **variance (σ²)** is the squared value of σ and is required when computing the Z-score denominator.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Data Preprocessing Techniques

Data preprocessing is structurally divided into **five principal pillars**, each addressing a distinct class of data irregularity:

1. **Data Cleaning** — Handles missing values, noise, outliers, and inconsistencies.
2. **Data Integration** — Merges data from multiple heterogeneous sources (databases, APIs, flat files).
3. **Data Transformation** — Normalizes, aggregates, smooths, and discretizes features.
4. **Data Reduction** — Reduces volume via dimensionality reduction (PCA, LDA) or sampling.
5. **Data Discretization / Encoding** — Converts categorical variables into numerical form.

### 2.2 Operational Breakdown — The "Why" and "How"

#### A. Handling Missing Values
- **Why:** Real-world datasets often contain null entries due to sensor failure, human error, or transmission loss.
- **How:** Three primary strategies exist:
  1. **Deletion (Listwise / Pairwise):** Remove rows or columns with missing entries.
  2. **Mean / Median / Mode Imputation:** Replace missing entries with central tendency measures.
  3. **Predictive Imputation (KNN, Regression):** Estimate missing values using other features.

#### B. Noise & Outlier Treatment
- **Binning:** Smoothing via equal-width or equal-frequency partitioning.
- **Regression:** Fit a regression surface to smooth noise.
- **Clustering:** Detect and remove outliers as points lying outside cluster boundaries.

#### C. Data Transformation
- **Normalization (Min-Max Scaling):** Compresses data into the interval [0, 1] or [−1, 1].
- **Standardization (Z-Score):** Centers data around mean 0 with unit variance.
- **Decimal Scaling:** Divides by powers of 10 to bring magnitude into range.

#### D. Data Reduction
- **Dimensionality Reduction (PCA):** Projects high-dimensional data onto orthogonal principal components.
- **Numerosity Reduction:** Sampling, clustering, or histogram aggregation.
- **Data Cube Aggregation:** Roll-up operations across hierarchies.

#### E. Data Encoding for Generative Models
- **Label Encoding:** Maps categories to integers (0, 1, 2, ...).
- **One-Hot Encoding:** Creates binary indicator columns per category.
- **Embedding Layers:** Learns dense vector representations for high-cardinality categorical data (used in NLP Generative Models).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Technique | Mathematical Formula | Output Range | Key Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Min-Max Normalization** | $X_{\text{norm}} = \dfrac{X - X_{\min}}{X_{\max} - X_{\min}}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Neural network input scaling |
| **Min-Max to [-1, 1] Range** | $X' = 2 \cdot \dfrac{X - X_{\min}}{X_{\max} - X_{\min}} - 1$ | $[-1, 1]$ | Tanh activation input prep |
| **Z-Score Standardization** | $Z = \dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$ | $(-\infty, \infty)$, mean 0 | Gaussian-distributed features |
| **Decimal Scaling** | $X' = \dfrac{X}{10^{j}}$ | $[-1, 1]$ approx | Magnitude reduction |
| **Mean Imputation** | $X_{\text{fill}} = \mu = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i$ | Continuous | Replace nulls |
| **Median Imputation** | $X_{\text{fill}} = \text{Median}(X)$ | Robust to outliers | Skewed distributions |
| **Variance** | $\sigma^{2} = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (X_i - \mu)^{2}$ | $\geq 0$ | Z-score denominator step |
| **Population Standard Deviation** | $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (X_i - \mu)^{2}}$ | $\geq 0$ | Z-score denominator step |
| **Sample Standard Deviation** | $s = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n-1} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (X_i - \bar{X})^{2}}$ | $\geq 0$ | Sample-based standardization |
| **Cosine Similarity (Reduction Metric)** | $\cos(\theta) = \dfrac{A \cdot B}{\vert A \vert \cdot \vert B \vert}$ | $[-1, 1]$ | Similarity after PCA projection |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** Always verify whether the question specifies **population** ($\sigma$, divide by $n$) or **sample** ($s$, divide by $n-1$) standard deviation. KTU examiners frequently award zero marks when students use $n$ instead of $n-1$ (or vice versa) without stating the assumption.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Medical Imaging** (GAN-based MRI synthesis) | Pixel intensity normalization to $[-1, 1]$ for Stable Diffusion VAE encoder input |
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | LiDAR point cloud outlier removal for safety-critical generative simulations |
| **NLP / LLMs** | Token embedding normalization for Stable Diffusion text encoder |
| **Finance** | Time-series imputation for stock-price generative forecasting models |
| **Climate Science** | Satellite imagery standardization for downscaling super-resolution GANs |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Min-Max Normalization

The Min-Max Normalization rescales a feature $X$ to a fixed range $[X_{\text{new\_min}}, X_{\text{new\_max}}]$, typically $[0, 1]$ for sigmoid-output generative heads or $[-1, 1]$ for tanh-based decoders in VAEs.

#### Step 1: Establish the Linear Mapping Assumption

We assume the transformation is **linear**, preserving the relative distances between data points:

$$
X_{\text{norm}} = aX + b
$$

where $a$ and $b$ are scalars determined by the boundary conditions.

#### Step 2: Apply Boundary Conditions

- When $X = X_{\max}$, then $X_{\text{norm}} = X_{\text{new\_max}}$.
- When $X = X_{\min}$, then $X_{\text{norm}} = X_{\text{new\_min}}$.

#### Step 3: Solve for $a$ and $b$ via Substitution

Substituting the two boundary conditions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_{\text{new\_max}} &= a \cdot X_{\max} + b \\
X_{\text{new\_min}} &= a \cdot X_{\min} + b
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4: Subtract the Two Equations to Eliminate $b$

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_{\text{new\_max}} - X_{\text{new\_min}} &= a \cdot (X_{\max} - X_{\min}) \\
a &= \dfrac{X_{\text{new\_max}} - X_{\text{new\_min}}}{X_{\max} - X_{\min}}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 5: Substitute Back to Solve for $b$

$$
\begin{aligned}
b &= X_{\text{new\_min}} - a \cdot X_{\min} \\
b &= X_{\text{new\_min}} - \left( \dfrac{X_{\text{new\_max}} - X_{\text{new\_min}}}{X_{\max} - X_{\min}} \right) \cdot X_{\min}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 6: Combine into Final Compact Form

For the standard case where $X_{\text{new\_min}} = 0$ and $X_{\text{new\_max}} = 1$:

$$
X_{\text{norm}} = \dfrac{X - X_{\min}}{X_{\max} - X_{\min}}
$$

**Interpretation:** The numerator $(X - X_{\min})$ shifts the minimum to zero, and the denominator $(X_{\max} - X_{\min})$ scales the range to unity. This is the canonical preprocessing step used in **Min-Max scaling for Generative Adversarial Networks** before feeding data into the discriminator network.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Z-Score Standardization

Z-score standardization (also called **standard scaling**) is preferred when the dataset is assumed to follow a Gaussian distribution, as is the standard initialization assumption in many generative priors.

#### Step 1: Define the Objective

We seek a transformation $Z = f(X)$ such that the resulting distribution has:

- Mean = 0
- Standard Deviation = 1

#### Step 2: Apply Mean Centering

To center the data around zero, subtract the population mean $\mu$:

$$
Y = X - \mu
$$

The new mean becomes:

$$
E[Y] = E[X - \mu] = E[X] - \mu = \mu - \mu = 0
$$

#### Step 3: Apply Variance Scaling

The variance of $Y$ is:

$$
\text{Var}(Y) = E[(Y - E[Y])^2] = E[(X - \mu)^2] = \sigma^2
$$

To force unit variance, divide $Y$ by $\sigma$:

$$
Z = \dfrac{Y}{\sigma} = \dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma}
$$

#### Step 4: Verify the Resulting Mean and Variance

**Mean of Z:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[Z] &= E\left[\dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma}\right] \\
&= \dfrac{1}{\sigma} \cdot E[X - \mu] \\
&= \dfrac{1}{\sigma} \cdot (E[X] - \mu) \\
&= \dfrac{\mu - \mu}{\sigma} \\
&= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Variance of Z:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}(Z) &= \text{Var}\left(\dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma}\right) \\
&= \dfrac{1}{\sigma^2} \cdot \text{Var}(X - \mu) \\
&= \dfrac{1}{\sigma^2} \cdot \sigma^2 \\
&= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Z-score standardization is the **default preprocessing** for **Diffusion Models** and **Latent Diffusion (Stable Diffusion)**. The VAE encoder of Stable Diffusion expects pixel values scaled to $[-1, 1]$ — a combination of Min-Max followed by Z-score is applied.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Technique

| Scenario | Recommended Technique | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Bounded range known (e.g., pixel values 0–255) | Min-Max Normalization | Preserves exact bounds for image generators |
| Unknown range, Gaussian-distributed | Z-Score Standardization | Robust to outliers, zero-centered |
| High cardinality categorical feature | Embedding Layer | Avoids dimensionality explosion of one-hot |
| Low cardinality nominal feature (≤10) | One-Hot Encoding | Simple, no ordinal assumption |
| Ordinal categorical feature | Label Encoding | Preserves order semantics |
| High-dimensional tabular data | PCA + Standardization | Reduces redundancy, ensures equal variance |
| Time-series with missing gaps | Forward/Backward Fill Imputation | Respects temporal continuity |
| Skewed numeric feature | Log / Box-Cox Transformation | Reduces skewness for better learning |

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** Given the feature vector $X = [2, 4, 6, 8, 10]$, compute:
1. Min-Max Normalization (range $[0, 1]$)
2. Z-Score Standardization (assume population statistics)

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Min and Max:**

$$
X_{\min} = 2, \quad X_{\max} = 10
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Min-Max Formula:**

$$
X_{\text{norm}} = \dfrac{X - 2}{10 - 2} = \dfrac{X - 2}{8}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
2 &\rightarrow 0 \\
4 &\rightarrow 0.25 \\
6 &\rightarrow 0.50 \\
8 &\rightarrow 0.75 \\
10 &\rightarrow 1.00
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Mean:**

$$
\mu = \dfrac{2 + 4 + 6 + 8 + 10}{5} = \dfrac{30}{5} = 6
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Population Standard Deviation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma^2 &= \dfrac{1}{5}\left[(2-6)^2 + (4-6)^2 + (6-6)^2 + (8-6)^2 + (10-6)^2\right] \\
&= \dfrac{1}{5}\left[16 + 4 + 0 + 4 + 16\right] \\
&= \dfrac{40}{5} = 8
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{8} = 2\sqrt{2} \approx 2.828
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Z-Score Formula:**

$$
Z = \dfrac{X - 6}{2\sqrt{2}}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
2 &\rightarrow \dfrac{-4}{2\sqrt{2}} = -\sqrt{2} \approx -1.414 \\
4 &\rightarrow \dfrac{-2}{2\sqrt{2}} = -\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx -0.707 \\
6 &\rightarrow 0 \\
8 &\rightarrow +\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx +0.707 \\
10 &\rightarrow +\sqrt{2} \approx +1.414
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Notice that the Z-scored vector is **centered at zero** and symmetric around 0, while the Min-Max vector is bounded in $[0, 1]$. This mathematical property is what determines the choice between the two techniques for a given activation function in the generative model.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Data Preprocessing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    nodeI -- No --> nodeK[Numerical Scaling Min Max Z Score]

```

### 4.3 Decision Tree for Scaling Method Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start Feature Vector X] --> nodeB{Known Bounded Range?}
    nodeB -- Yes --> nodeC[Use Min Max Scaling to 0 1 or Minus 1 1]
    nodeB -- No --> nodeD{Approx Gaussian Distribution?}
    nodeD -- Yes --> nodeE[Use Z Score Standardization]
    nodeD -- No --> nodeF{High Skewness Detected?}
    nodeF -- Yes --> nodeG[Apply Log or Box Cox then Standardize]
    nodeF -- No --> nodeH[Apply Robust Scaling using Median and IQR]

```

### 4.4 Detailed Preprocessing Sub-Module Architecture

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

> Define **Data Preprocessing**. List any **four** major techniques used in the preprocessing stage of a Generative AI pipeline.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Data Preprocessing is the process of transforming raw, noisy, incomplete, and inconsistent data into a clean, normalized, and model-ready format suitable for training machine learning and generative AI models.

**Four Major Techniques (2 Marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Data Cleaning** — Handles missing values, noise, and outliers.
2. **Data Integration** — Merges data from multiple heterogeneous sources.
3. **Data Transformation** — Normalization, standardization, aggregation.
4. **Data Reduction** — Dimensionality reduction (PCA) and sampling.

*(Alternative valid answers: Discretization, Binarization, Encoding.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**

> Differentiate between **Min-Max Normalization** and **Z-Score Standardization**. State one advantage of each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Min-Max Normalization | Z-Score Standardization |
|---|---|---|
| Formula | $X_{\text{norm}} = \dfrac{X - X_{\min}}{X_{\max} - X_{\min}}$ | $Z = \dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$ |
| Output Range | Fixed (e.g., $[0, 1]$) | Unbounded, mean 0, SD 1 |
| Sensitivity to Outliers | **High** (1 Mark) | **Lower** (1 Mark) |
| Best Use Case | Bounded data (e.g., images) | Gaussian-distributed data |

**Advantage of Min-Max (0.5):** Preserves exact data bounds — useful for pixel intensity normalization in image generators.
**Advantage of Z-Score (0.5):** Robust to outliers and zero-centers the data — ideal for diffusion model latents.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q.A.** *(a)* Explain the **five major stages of data preprocessing** with one example for each stage. *(7 Marks)*

*(b)* Compute the **Min-Max normalized** and **Z-score standardized** values for the dataset $X = [12, 15, 18, 21, 24, 27]$. Assume population statistics. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Five Major Stages (7 Marks, 1.4 each):**

1. **Data Cleaning:** Identifying and correcting errors. *Example:* Replacing missing age values in a customer dataset with the median age.
2. **Data Integration:** Combining data from multiple sources. *Example:* Merging SQL customer records with NoSQL clickstream logs.
3. **Data Transformation:** Changing data distribution or scale. *Example:* Rescaling image pixel values from $[0, 255]$ to $[-1, 1]$.
4. **Data Reduction:** Reducing volume while preserving information. *Example:* Applying PCA to reduce 100 features into 10 principal components.
5. **Data Discretization / Encoding:** Converting categorical to numerical. *Example:* One-hot encoding the "Color" feature (Red, Green, Blue) into 3 binary columns.

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $X = [12, 15, 18, 21, 24, 27]$, $n = 6$.

**Step 1 — Min-Max Normalization (3.5 Marks):**

$$
X_{\min} = 12, \quad X_{\max} = 27
$$

$$
X_{\text{norm}} = \dfrac{X - 12}{27 - 12} = \dfrac{X - 12}{15}
$$

| Original $X$ | Normalized $X_{\text{norm}}$ |
|---|---|
| 12 | $0/15 = 0.000$ |
| 15 | $3/15 = 0.200$ |
| 18 | $6/15 = 0.400$ |
| 21 | $9/15 = 0.600$ |
| 24 | $12/15 = 0.800$ |
| 27 | $15/15 = 1.000$ |

**Step 2 — Compute Mean for Z-Score (1 Mark):**

$$
\mu = \dfrac{12 + 15 + 18 + 21 + 24 + 27}{6} = \dfrac{117}{6} = 19.5
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Standard Deviation (1 Mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma^2 &= \dfrac{1}{6}\left[(12-19.5)^2 + (15-19.5)^2 + (18-19.5)^2 + (21-19.5)^2 + (24-19.5)^2 + (27-19.5)^2\right] \\
&= \dfrac{1}{6}\left[56.25 + 20.25 + 2.25 + 2.25 + 20.25 + 56.25\right] \\
&= \dfrac{157.5}{6} = 26.25
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{26.25} \approx 5.124
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Z-Score Formula (1.5 Marks):**

| Original $X$ | $X - \mu$ | $Z = (X - \mu)/\sigma$ |
|---|---|---|
| 12 | $-7.5$ | $\approx -1.464$ |
| 15 | $-4.5$ | $\approx -0.878$ |
| 18 | $-1.5$ | $\approx -0.293$ |
| 21 | $+1.5$ | $\approx +0.293$ |
| 24 | $+4.5$ | $\approx +0.878$ |
| 27 | $+7.5$ | $\approx +1.464$ |

---

#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q.B.** *(a)* With a neat block diagram, explain the **role of data preprocessing in a Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) pipeline**. Why is pixel normalization to $[-1, 1]$ critical for the discriminator? *(7 Marks)*

*(b)* Given a dataset $X = [4, 8, 12, 16, 20]$ with one missing value at position 3, demonstrate **mean imputation** followed by **decimal scaling normalization**. Justify why decimal scaling is preferred when the data spans multiple orders of magnitude. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Role in GAN Pipeline (7 Marks):**

A Generative Adversarial Network comprises two networks — the **Generator ($G$)** and the **Discriminator ($D$)** — trained adversarially. The data preprocessing stage is responsible for:

1. **Image Resizing & Cropping** (1 Mark): Standardizing all training images to a fixed resolution (e.g., $64 \times 64$ or $256 \times 256$) to ensure uniform tensor dimensions.

2. **Pixel Normalization to $[-1, 1]$** (2 Marks): The original pixel values in $[0, 255]$ are scaled to $[-1, 1]$ using:
$$
X_{\text{norm}} = 2 \cdot \dfrac{X}{255} - 1
$$

3. **Justification for Discriminator** (2 Marks): The discriminator's output layer typically uses a **Tanh** activation (in the generator) or expects inputs centered at zero. Normalized inputs lead to:
   - **Faster gradient convergence** (avoids saturation of sigmoid/tanh).
   - **Stable adversarial training** (mitigates mode collapse).
   - **Symmetric feature distribution** around zero.

4. **Data Augmentation Pipeline** (1 Mark): Random flips, rotations, and color jitter expand the effective training set.

5. **Batch Normalization Compatibility** (1 Mark): Pre-normalized inputs interact smoothly with batch normalization layers inside the discriminator's convolutional blocks.

**Part (b) — Mean Imputation + Decimal Scaling (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $X = [4, 8, \_, 16, 20]$ with missing at index 3.

**Step 1 — Mean Imputation (2 Marks):**

$$
\mu = \dfrac{4 + 8 + 16 + 20}{4} = \dfrac{48}{4} = 12
$$

The imputed dataset becomes:
$$
X_{\text{new}} = [4, 8, 12, 16, 20]
$$

**Step 2 — Decimal Scaling (3 Marks):**

Decimal scaling transforms $X$ by dividing by $10^j$, where $j$ is the smallest integer such that $\max \vert X' \vert < 1$.

For our dataset, $\max \vert X \vert = 20$.

- $j = 1$: $\max = 2.0$ (not less than 1) ❌
- $j = 2$: $\max = 0.2$ (less than 1) ✅

Thus, $X' = \dfrac{X}{10^2} = \dfrac{X}{100}$:

| Original $X$ | Decimal Scaled $X'$ |
|---|---|
| 4 | $0.04$ |
| 8 | $0.08$ |
| 12 | $0.12$ |
| 16 | $0.16$ |
| 20 | $0.20$ |

**Step 3 — Justification (2 Marks):**

Decimal scaling is preferred when:
- Data spans **multiple orders of magnitude** (e.g., $[1, 10000]$).
- The goal is to preserve the **original distribution shape** while reducing magnitude.
- Unlike Min-Max, it does **not require knowledge of the minimum** — only the maximum absolute value.
- It is computationally cheaper than iterative Z-score for streaming data.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Data Preprocessing Questions**
> 1. **Forgetting the $n-1$ Bessel Correction (1–2 marks lost):** When the question states "sample" data, students must divide by $n-1$ in the standard deviation. Using $n$ silently loses full credit.
> 2. **Confusing Population vs Sample Notation:** Mixing $\sigma$ and $s$ without clear definition is penalized.
> 3. **Skipping the Imputation Step:** When "missing values" are present, students often compute the mean on the original incomplete array, forgetting that the missing value itself needs to be filled first.
> 4. **Reporting Z-scores without intermediate steps:** KTU examiners require explicit $X - \mu$ and $\sigma$ computation shown separately. A single final answer without intermediate algebra loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Wrong denominator in Min-Max:** Using $(X - X_{\max})$ instead of $(X - X_{\min})$ in the numerator — a frequent inversion error.
> 6. **Not specifying the range target:** For $[-1, 1]$ Min-Max, students forget the factor of 2 and the offset of $-1$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Data Preprocessing** is the gatekeeper stage — the **upper bound** of any generative model's performance is dictated by input data quality (**GIGO principle**).
- The **five pillars** are: Cleaning, Integration, Transformation, Reduction, and Encoding.
- **Min-Max Normalization** produces output in $[0, 1]$ using $X_{\text{norm}} = (X - X_{\min})/(X_{\max} - X_{\min})$ — sensitive to outliers.
- **Z-Score Standardization** produces output with mean 0, variance 1 using $Z = (X - \mu)/\sigma$ — robust to outliers.
- **Population SD** uses denominator $n$; **Sample SD** uses denominator $n-1$ — choose based on the question.
- **GAN / Stable Diffusion VAE** pipelines require pixel normalization to $[-1, 1]$ for stable Tanh-based generator outputs.
- **PCA** reduces dimensionality by projecting onto orthogonal eigenvectors of the covariance matrix.
- **One-Hot Encoding** is suitable for low-cardinality nominal features; **Embedding Layers** are used for high-cardinality categorical features in NLP generative models.
- **Mean Imputation** is simple but reduces variance; **KNN / Regression Imputation** is statistically superior but computationally expensive.
- **Decimal Scaling** $X' = X / 10^j$ is preferred when only the magnitude order needs reduction without distribution shape change.
- **Log / Box-Cox Transformation** is recommended before scaling for highly skewed numerical features.
- Always **state assumptions** (population vs sample, distribution type, range target) explicitly in KTU answers to earn full marks.
- **Information density maximization** and **noise minimization** are the twin objectives of preprocessing — every operation should be justifiable in these terms.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications in Various Fields: Art and Creativity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT867 - GENERATIVE AI > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Applications in Various Fields: Art and Creativity # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Generative AI in Art and Creativity** refers to the class of deep learning systems that learn the underlying probability distribution of creative artifacts (images, music, text, 3D models, video) and synthesize novel, statistically coherent samples that mimic, extend, or reimagine human artistic expression. Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020)**, this domain is studied as a convergence of three pillars: 1. **Representation Learning** — encoding high-dimensional creative data (pixels, audio waveforms, semantic tokens) into compact latent spaces. 2. **Probabilistic Generative Modeling** — learning the joint distribution $p(x, y)$ or conditional distribution $p(x \vert c)$ where $x$ is the artifact and $c$ is the conditioning signal (text prompt, style reference, melody). 3. **Cross-Modal Translation** — bridging modalities (text-to-image, audio-to-video, sketch-to-photograph) through aligned embedding spaces. The principal model families driving this field are: - **Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)** — StyleGAN, BigGAN, CycleGAN - **Diffusion Models** — DDPM, Stable Diffusion, DALL·E 2, Imagen - **Autoregressive Transformers** — GPT, MusicGen, Phenaki - **Variational Autoencoders (VAEs)** — foundational latent generators - **Multimodal Foundation Models** — CLIP, Flamingo, Gen-2 ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Infinite Art Studio" > [!NOTE] > **Analogy:** Imagine hiring an apprentice artist who has memorized every painting in the Louvre, every chord progression in jazz history, and every script ever written — but instead of merely copying, this apprentice has internalized the *grammar of beauty*. When you whisper a description ("a cyberpunk cat in a Monet garden at sunset"), the apprentice doesn't retrieve an image; it *composes* a brand-new one by combining learned concepts of "cyberpunk," "cat," "Monet's brushstrokes," and "sunset lighting." This is precisely how modern generative systems operate: they do not store art, they learn a **compressed language of aesthetics** and decode it on demand. ## 1.3 Scope of the Field | Creative Domain | Representative Tool | Modal Output | |---|---|---| | Visual Art & Illustration | Midjourney, DALL·E 3, Stable Diffusion | 2D Images | | Music & Audio | MusicGen, Jukebox, Suno AI | Audio waveforms | | Literature & Poetry | GPT-4, Claude, Llama | Natural language text | | Video & Animation | Runway Gen-3, Sora, Pika Labs | Temporal video frames | | 3D & Game Design | DreamFusion, Point-E, Meshy | Meshes, point clouds | | Fashion & Textile Design | CALA, Resleeve, AI Pattern generators | Garment mockups | | Architecture | ArkDesign AI, Spacemaker | Floor plans, renderings | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The syllabus frames creativity as a measurable engineering problem — one where loss functions, latent vectors, and sampling strategies replace intuition, brushes, and ink. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Generative Modeling Pipeline (Structured Logic) A modern generative creative system executes the following pipeline: - **Step 1 — Data Ingestion:** A large corpus of paired (or unpaired) creative artifacts is collected and normalized. For images, this means resizing to $256 \times 256$ or $512 \times 512$ and scaling pixel values to $[-1, 1]$. - **Step 2 — Encoding (Latent Compression):** A neural encoder $E_\phi$ maps the high-dimensional artifact $x \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C}$ to a lower-dimensional latent vector $z \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ where typically $d \ll H \cdot W \cdot C$. - **Step 3 — Distribution Learning:** The model learns the prior $p_\theta(z)$ over the latent space, conditioned on a prompt embedding $c$ obtained from a text encoder (e.g., CLIP's text transformer). - **Step 4 — Sampling:** A new latent $z'$ is drawn from $p_\theta(z \vert c)$ via iterative denoising (diffusion) or ancestral sampling. - **Step 5 — Decoding:** A decoder $D_\psi$ reconstructs the artifact $\hat{x} = D_\psi(z')$ back into the original modality. - **Step 6 — Refinement (optional):** Classifier-Free Guidance, upsampling super-resolution networks, or human-in-the-loop RLHF fine-tuning polish the output. > [!NOTE] > **The 'Why':** This pipeline decouples *what to generate* (semantic content, governed by the prompt) from *how it looks* (style, governed by the decoder), enabling compositional creativity. ## 2.2 The Three Pillars — How Each Works ### 2.2.1 GANs (Generative Adversarial Networks) Two networks compete in a **minimax game**: - The **Generator** $G$ produces fake samples from noise $z \sim p_z$. - The **Discriminator** $D$ tries to distinguish real from fake. ### 2.2.2 Diffusion Models Inspired by non-equilibrium thermodynamics, they learn to **reverse a gradual noising process**. A clean image is corrupted over $T$ steps with Gaussian noise; the model learns to predict and remove that noise. ### 2.2.3 Autoregressive Transformers They tokenize the artifact (e.g., image patches or musical notes) and generate them one-by-one, predicting $p(x_t \vert x_{ [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorize the following table — these are the equations most frequently tested in KTU University Exams for this module.** | Concept | Equation | Description | |---|---|---| | GAN Objective | $\min_G \max_D V(D,G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{data}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$ | Minimax game between G and D | | Forward Diffusion (DDPM) | $q(x_t \vert x_{t-1}) = \mathcal{N}(x_t; \sqrt{1-\beta_t}\, x_{t-1}, \beta_t I)$ | Adds Gaussian noise at step t | | Closed-Form Forward | $x_t = \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}\, x_0 + \sqrt{1 - \bar{\alpha}_t}\, \epsilon, \quad \epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0,I)$ | Direct sampling at any timestep | | Reverse Diffusion | $p_\theta(x_{t-1} \vert x_t) = \mathcal{N}(x_{t-1}; \mu_\theta(x_t, t), \Sigma_\theta(x_t, t))$ | Learned denoising step | | Simplified Loss | $L_{simple} = \mathbb{E}_{t, x_0, \epsilon}\left[\Vert \epsilon - \epsilon_\theta(\sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t} x_0 + \sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_t}\epsilon, t) \Vert^2\right]$ | Noise prediction MSE | | VAE ELBO | $\mathcal{L}_{ELBO} = \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z \vert x)}[\log p_\theta(x \vert z)] - D_{KL}(q_\phi(z \vert x) \Vert p(z))$ | Reconstruction minus KL | | Classifier-Free Guidance | $\tilde{\epsilon}_\theta(x_t, c) = (1 + w)\,\epsilon_\theta(x_t, c) - w\,\epsilon_\theta(x_t, \emptyset)$ | Amplifies prompt adherence | | CLIP Similarity | $S_{CLIP}(I, T) = \cos(E_I(I), E_T(T)) = \frac{E_I(I) \cdot E_T(T)}{\Vert E_I(I) \Vert \,\Vert E_T(T) \Vert}$ | Image-text alignment | | Scaled Dot-Product Attention | $\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V$ | Core transformer block | | FID Score | $FID = \Vert \mu_r - \mu_g \Vert^2 + \text{Tr}\!\left(\Sigma_r + \Sigma_g - 2(\Sigma_r \Sigma_g)^{1/2}\right)$ | Distribution distance metric | | Inception Score | $IS = \exp\!\left(\mathbb{E}_x\, D_{KL}\!\left(p(y \vert x) \Vert p(y)\right)\right)$ | Quality + diversity metric | > **Notation note:** In all rows above, $\Vert \cdot \Vert$ denotes the $L^2$ (Euclidean) norm; the vertical pipe symbol has been avoided to preserve table integrity. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > Beyond art, the same generative pipelines are deployed in **drug discovery** (molecule generation), **CAD design** (generative engineering), **data augmentation** for training classifiers, **synthetic medical imaging**, and **procedural game world creation**. Understanding the art-domain pipeline is your gateway to all of these. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: The DDPM Training Objective The Diffusion Probabilistic Model (DDPM) is the mathematical engine behind Stable Diffusion, DALL·E 2, and Imagen. We derive its loss function step-by-step. **Starting Point — The Forward Process:** We define a Markov chain that gradually adds Gaussian noise to a clean image $x_0$ over $T$ timesteps: $$q(x_t \vert x_{t-1}) = \mathcal{N}\!\left(x_t;\, \sqrt{1-\beta_t}\, x_{t-1},\, \beta_t I\right)$$ where $\beta_t \in (0, 1)$ is a variance schedule (either linear or cosine). **Step 1 — Reparameterization Trick:** Apply the reparameterization $x_t = \sqrt{1-\beta_t}\, x_{t-1} + \sqrt{\beta_t}\, \epsilon_{t-1}$ with $\epsilon_{t-1} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$. **Step 2 — Define Cumulative Product:** Let $\alpha_t = 1 - \beta_t$ and $\bar{\alpha}_t = \prod_{s=1}^{t} \alpha_s$. **Step 3 — Marginalize the Chain:** By repeated substitution, we obtain the closed-form marginal: $$q(x_t \vert x_0) = \mathcal{N}\!\left(x_t;\, \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}\, x_0,\, (1-\bar{\alpha}_t) I\right)$$ This is the key insight: we can jump directly to *any* timestep $t$ from $x_0$ without simulating intermediate steps. **Step 4 — Express $x_t$ Explicitly:** $$x_t = \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}\, x_0 + \sqrt{1 - \bar{\alpha}_t}\, \epsilon, \quad \epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$$ As $t \to T$, $\bar{\alpha}_T \to 0$, so $x_T$ becomes pure Gaussian noise. **Step 5 — Set Up the Reverse Process:** We wish to learn $p_\theta(x_{t-1} \vert x_t)$ to denoise step by step: $$p_\theta(x_{t-1} \vert x_t) = \mathcal{N}\!\left(x_{t-1};\, \mu_\theta(x_t, t),\, \Sigma_\theta(x_t, t)\right)$$ **Step 6 — Predict the Noise Instead (Ho et al. 2020 simplification):** Ho et al. showed that instead of predicting $\mu_\theta$ directly, predicting the noise $\epsilon$ yields better empirical results. The network $\epsilon_\theta(x_t, t)$ outputs an estimate of the noise added. **Step 7 — Write the Simplified Loss:** $$L_{simple} = \mathbb{E}_{t, x_0, \epsilon}\!\left[\left\Vert \epsilon - \epsilon_\theta\!\left(\sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}\, x_0 + \sqrt{1 - \bar{\alpha}_t}\, \epsilon,\, t\right) \right\Vert^2\right]$$ **Step 8 — Interpretation:** At each training step, the algorithm: - Samples a clean image $x_0$. - Samples a random timestep $t \in \{1, \ldots, T\}$. - Samples noise $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$. - Computes the noisy $x_t$. - Asks the network to recover $\epsilon$. - Updates weights via gradient descent on the MSE. > [!NOTE] > **Why this works for creativity:** During inference, the model is *given* pure noise and a text conditioning $c$, then iteratively denoises for $T$ steps — slowly transforming random static into a coherent, prompt-aligned image. This is the mathematical heart of every modern text-to-image system. ## 3.2 Derivation: Classifier-Free Guidance Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) is the trick that makes prompts "stick" — it amplifies the influence of the conditioning signal. **Step 1 — Define Conditional and Unconditional Predictions:** The network predicts noise twice: once conditioned on the prompt $c$, and once on a null token $\emptyset$: $$\epsilon_\theta(x_t, c) \quad \text{and} \quad \epsilon_\theta(x_t, \emptyset)$$ **Step 2 — Combine Linearly:** $$\tilde{\epsilon}_\theta(x_t, c) = \epsilon_\theta(x_t, \emptyset) + w \cdot \left(\epsilon_\theta(x_t, c) - \epsilon_\theta(x_t, \emptyset)\right)$$ **Step 3 — Simplify:** $$\tilde{\epsilon}_\theta(x_t, c) = (1 + w)\,\epsilon_\theta(x_t, c) - w\,\epsilon_\theta(x_t, \emptyset)$$ **Step 4 — Interpretation of $w$:** - $w = 0$: ignores the prompt (pure unconditional generation). - $w = 1$: standard conditional generation. - $w > 1$ (typically $w = 7.5$): **over-conditioning** — the output is forced to match the prompt more aggressively, often at the cost of diversity and naturalness. > [!IMPORTANT] > **High $w$** = prompt-faithful but sterile imagery. **Low $w$** = artistic but unfaithful. This is the central tradeoff explored in KTU valuation answers. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Model Families for Art | Property | GANs | Diffusion Models | Autoregressive Transformers | VAEs | |---|---|---|---|---| | Sample Quality (FID) | Excellent (low FID) | State-of-the-art | High | Blurry | | Diversity | Mode collapse risk | High | High | High | | Training Stability | Unstable (adversarial) | Stable | Stable | Stable | | Inference Speed | Fast (1 forward pass) | Slow ($T$ steps) | Slow (sequential) | Fast | | Controllability | Moderate (Style mixing) | Excellent (CFG) | Limited | Limited | | Famous Art Models | StyleGAN3, BigGAN | Stable Diffusion, Imagen | DALL·E (original), Parti | NVAE, VQ-VAE-2 | | Best Use Case | Face generation, style transfer | Photorealistic synthesis | Text, music, code | Latent compression backbone | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 High-Level Generative AI Creative Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Creative Corpus
Paintings, Audio, Text] --> B[Preprocessing
Resize, Tokenize, Normalize] B --> C[Neural Encoder E_φ
CNN, ViT, or Audio CNN] C --> D[Latent Space Z
Compressed Representation] D --> E[Generative Model
Diffusion / GAN / Transformer] F[User Prompt c] --> G[Text Encoder
CLIP, T5, BERT] G --> E E --> H[Sampler
DDPM, DDIM, CFG] H --> I[Decoder D_ψ
Latent to Pixel] I --> J[Output Artifact
Image, Music, Text] J --> K{Quality Check} K -->|Pass| L[Deliver to User] K -->|Fail| M[Refine / Re-prompt] M --> F ``` ## 4.2 Diffusion Model Denoising Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Forward_Training["Forward Process - Training"] X0[Clean Image x_0] --> A1[Sample t and ε] A1 --> A2[Compute x_t = sqrt(α_bar_t)·x_0 + sqrt(1-α_bar_t)·ε] A2 --> A3[U-Net ε_θ predicts noise] A3 --> A4[Loss = MSE between ε and prediction] end subgraph Reverse_Inference["Reverse Process - Inference"] XT[Pure Noise x_T] --> B1[Inject Text Conditioning c] B1 --> B2[Apply CFG with weight w] B2 --> B3[U-Net predicts ε] B3 --> B4[Compute x_t-1 = denoise step] B4 --> B5{T reaches 0?} B5 -->|No| B2 B5 -->|Yes| B6[Final Clean Image x_0] end ``` ## 4.3 Cross-Modal Generative Application Map ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Input_Modalities["Input Modalities"] I1[Text Prompt] I2[Sketch Image] I3[Audio Clip] I4[Style Reference] end subgraph Core_Engines["Generative Engines"] C1[CLIP Text Encoder] C2[ControlNet] C3[AudioLDM] C4[IP-Adapter] end subgraph Output_Modalities["Output Modalities"] O1[Photorealistic Image] O2[3D Mesh / Point Cloud] O3[Music Track] O4[Animated Video] end I1 --> C1 --> O1 I2 --> C2 --> O1 I3 --> C3 --> O3 I4 --> C4 --> O1 C1 --> O2 C1 --> O4 ``` ## 4.4 Human-AI Creative Collaboration Loop ```mermaid flowchart TB H[Human Artist] -->|1. Concept and Prompt| P[Prompt Engineering] P -->|2. Submit| M[Generative Model] M -->|3. Generate Drafts| D[Multiple Outputs] D -->|4. Review| H H -->|5. Refine Prompt or Inpaint| M H -->|6. Final Selection| F[Curated Artwork] F -->|7. Post-Process| PP[Upscaling, Color Grade] PP -->|8. Publish| PUB[Portfolio / Gallery] ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Insight:** These diagrams are high-yield for theory questions asking *"Explain the architecture of a text-to-image generation system"* or *"Describe the human-AI co-creation loop."* # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define Generative AI in the context of art and creativity. List any two model families used for art generation.** **Model Answer:** Generative AI in art and creativity refers to deep learning systems that learn the probability distribution of creative artifacts (images, music, text) and synthesize novel samples that mimic or extend human artistic expression. Two model families used for art generation: 1. **Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)** — e.g., StyleGAN, used for high-fidelity face and art synthesis. 2. **Diffusion Models** — e.g., Stable Diffusion, DALL·E 2, used for text-to-image generation with iterative denoising. ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **What is Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) and what role does the guidance weight $w$ play?** **Model Answer:** Classifier-Free Guidance is a sampling technique that combines the conditional noise prediction $\epsilon_\theta(x_t, c)$ and the unconditional prediction $\epsilon_\theta(x_t, \emptyset)$ to amplify prompt adherence: $$\tilde{\epsilon}_\theta(x_t, c) = (1 + w)\,\epsilon_\theta(x_t, c) - w\,\epsilon_\theta(x_t, \emptyset)$$ The guidance weight $w$ controls the trade-off: - **Higher $w$** → stronger prompt adherence, lower diversity, often more saturated/synthetic looking. - **Lower $w$** → more natural, diverse outputs, but weaker prompt following. - $w = 0$ → unconditional generation ignoring the prompt. --- ## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Answer either Question A or Question B in full. Each carries 14 marks split as (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.** ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]` **(a)** Explain the architecture and working of **Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPMs)** for image generation. Derive the simplified training loss. (7 Marks) **(b)** Discuss the application of generative AI in **music composition and audio synthesis**. Name two specific models and explain their approach. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **(a) DDPM Architecture and Loss Derivation (7 Marks):** DDPMs consist of two processes: 1. **Forward (Diffusion) Process** — A fixed Markov chain that gradually adds Gaussian noise to a clean image $x_0$ over $T$ timesteps according to a variance schedule $\beta_t$: $$q(x_t \vert x_{t-1}) = \mathcal{N}(x_t; \sqrt{1-\beta_t}\, x_{t-1}, \beta_t I)$$ Using the reparameterization trick and the cumulative product $\bar{\alpha}_t = \prod_{s=1}^{t}(1-\beta_s)$, we can express any noisy sample directly: $$x_t = \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}\, x_0 + \sqrt{1 - \bar{\alpha}_t}\, \epsilon, \quad \epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$$ 2. **Reverse (Denoising) Process** — A *learned* neural network (typically a U-Net) $\epsilon_\theta(x_t, t)$ predicts the noise that was added, enabling iterative denoising from pure noise $x_T$ back to a clean image $x_0$. **Derivation of the Simplified Loss:** The full variational bound involves KL divergences at every timestep: $$L_{VB} = \mathbb{E}_q\!\left[D_{KL}(q(x_T \vert x_0) \Vert p(x_T)) + \sum_{t>1} D_{KL}(q(x_{t-1} \vert x_t, x_0) \Vert p_\theta(x_{t-1} \vert x_t)) - \log p_\theta(x_0 \vert x_1)\right]$$ Ho et al. (2020) showed empirically that reparameterizing the network to predict the noise $\epsilon$ rather than the mean $\mu$ simplifies the per-timestep loss to an MSE: $$L_{simple} = \mathbb{E}_{t, x_0, \epsilon}\!\left[\left\Vert \epsilon - \epsilon_\theta\!\left(\sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}\, x_0 + \sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_t}\, \epsilon,\, t\right) \right\Vert^2\right]$$ **Training algorithm:** 1. Sample a clean image $x_0$ from the dataset. 2. Sample a random timestep $t \sim \text{Uniform}(1, T)$. 3. Sample noise $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$. 4. Compute the noisy sample $x_t$ using the closed-form equation. 5. Predict the noise $\hat{\epsilon} = \epsilon_\theta(x_t, t)$. 6. Compute MSE loss and backpropagate. **Inference (Sampling):** Start from $x_T \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ and iteratively apply the learned reverse process for $T$ steps, gradually producing a clean image. **(b) Generative AI in Music and Audio (7 Marks):** Generative AI has transformed music composition from a purely human activity into a collaborative human-AI process. **1. MusicGen (Meta, 2023):** - Uses an **autoregressive transformer** over discrete audio tokens produced by EnCodec. - Accepts text descriptions (e.g., "90s hip-hop beat with piano") and optionally melodic conditioning. - Generates 30-second audio clips at 32 kHz. - Approach: tokenizes audio into a discrete vocabulary, then uses a language-model-style decoder to predict the next token sequence, finally decoding back to waveform. **2. Jukebox (OpenAI, 2020):** - A hierarchical VQ-VAE with autoregressive priors operating at three temporal scales. - Generates raw audio (not MIDI), including vocals. - Approach: compresses audio into discrete codes at multiple resolutions; separate autoregressive transformers model the coarse, middle, and fine codes. **Additional applications include:** - **Stem separation and remastering** (e.g., Demucs). - **Voice synthesis and cloning** (e.g., VALL-E, Tortoise-TTS). - **Sound effect generation** (e.g., AudioLDM for text-to-audio). - **Algorithmic composition assistance** in DAWs like Ableton, FL Studio. **Impact on creativity:** Musicians now treat AI as an "infinite jam partner" — generating ideas, harmonies, and textures that can be selectively curated, edited, and layered into final productions. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]` **(a)** Compare **GANs, Diffusion Models, and Autoregressive Transformers** as generative model families for art. Discuss the strengths and weaknesses of each. (7 Marks) **(b)** Explain the **human-AI co-creation workflow** in modern generative art. What are the ethical and copyright concerns? (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **(a) Comparison of Generative Model Families (7 Marks):** | Aspect | GANs | Diffusion Models | Autoregressive Transformers | |---|---|---|---| | **Core Idea** | Two networks compete (G vs D) | Iteratively denoise random noise | Predict next token given previous ones | | **Training** | Adversarial, unstable | Stable, MSE-based | Stable, cross-entropy loss | | **Sample Quality** | Sharp, high-FID | State-of-the-art photorealism | High, but tokenization artifacts possible | | **Diversity** | Mode collapse risk | Excellent | Excellent | | **Speed** | 1 forward pass (fast) | $T$ iterations (slow) | Sequential token generation (slow) | | **Controllability** | Style mixing, latent walks | CFG, ControlNet, LoRA | Prompt engineering, in-context examples | | **Failure Modes** | Mode collapse, artifacts | Slow inference, VRAM hungry | Exposure bias, repetition | | **Examples** | StyleGAN3, BigGAN | Stable Diffusion, Imagen | Parti, DALL·E (v1), MusicGen | **Strengths and Weaknesses Summary:** **GANs — Strengths:** Fast inference, sharp outputs, well-suited for face and style domains. **Weaknesses:** Training instability, mode collapse, difficult to scale to diverse datasets, no native text conditioning (in original form). **Diffusion Models — Strengths:** SOTA quality, stable training, strong text conditioning via CFG, compositional via ControlNet. **Weaknesses:** Slow iterative sampling (mitigated by DDIM, DPM-Solver), high VRAM usage for high resolutions. **Autoregressive Transformers — Strengths:** Unified language-modeling paradigm, strong compositional generalization, easy to scale. **Weaknesses:** Slow sequential generation, exposure bias during training, and (in image domain) lossy tokenization. **(b) Human-AI Co-Creation Workflow and Ethics (7 Marks):** **The Co-Creation Workflow:** 1. **Ideation:** The artist formulates a concept, mood, or reference. 2. **Prompt Engineering:** The concept is translated into a precise text prompt (often with negative prompts, style tokens, and seed values). 3. **Generation:** The model produces multiple candidate outputs. 4. **Curation:** The artist selects the most promising outputs. 5. **Refinement:** Inpainting, outpainting, img2img, or ControlNet-guided editing refines specific regions. 6. **Post-Processing:** Upscaling (Real-ESRGAN), color grading, compositing, and integration with traditional tools (Photoshop, Procreate, Blender). 7. **Attribution and Documentation:** The artist documents the AI's role for transparency. **Ethical and Copyright Concerns:** 1. **Training Data Provenance:** Models are trained on web-scraped images, often without artists' consent. The "Have I Been Trained" project revealed that millions of artists' works were used without permission. 2. **Style Mimicry:** Models can replicate the distinctive style of living artists (e.g., imitating a specific living painter's brushwork), potentially devaluing their work. 3. **Copyright of Outputs:** Legal status of AI-generated art is unresolved globally. The U.S. Copyright Office has ruled that purely AI-generated works lack human authorship and are not copyrightable. The EU AI Act (2024) requires transparency about AI-generated content. 4. **Labor Displacement:** Concept artists, illustrators, and stock photographers face economic pressure as AI tools automate portions of their workflows. 5. **Deepfakes and Misinformation:** Generative video and image tools can fabricate realistic but false content, posing risks to journalism, politics, and personal reputation. 6. **Bias:** Training data reflects cultural, racial, and gender biases, which models can perpetuate and amplify. **Mitigations emerging in the industry:** - Opt-out platforms like **Glaze** and **Kudurru** that help artists protect their style. - **Content provenance standards** (C2PA, watermarking with tools like SynthID). - **Licensed training datasets** (e.g., Shutterstock's partnership with OpenAI, Adobe's licensed Firefly model). - **Royalty distribution frameworks** under active legislative discussion. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students frequently lose marks by: > - **Confusing GANs and VAEs** — VAEs use a probabilistic encoder-decoder and are trained with ELBO; GANs are adversarial and have no encoder (in the basic form). > - **Omitting the derivation** in diffusion model questions — KTU examiners *require* the closed-form $x_t$ equation and the simplified loss. > - **Treating CFG as optional** — it is a *mandatory* concept for any text-to-image discussion and is asked almost every exam cycle. > - **Ignoring ethics** — questions on "applications in art" increasingly include an ethical/copyright sub-part worth 2–3 marks. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4: Art and Creativity** - **Generative AI in art** = learning the distribution of creative artifacts and synthesizing novel, prompt-conditioned samples. - **Four principal model families:** GANs, Diffusion Models, Autoregressive Transformers, VAEs. Diffusion dominates the current art landscape. - **DDPM forward process:** $q(x_t \vert x_{t-1}) = \mathcal{N}(x_t; \sqrt{1-\beta_t}\, x_{t-1}, \beta_t I)$ — fixed, no learnable parameters. - **DDPM closed-form marginal:** $x_t = \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}\, x_0 + \sqrt{1 - \bar{\alpha}_t}\, \epsilon$ — must be memorized verbatim. - **DDPM simplified loss:** $L_{simple} = \mathbb{E}[\Vert \epsilon - \epsilon_\theta(x_t, t) \Vert^2]$ — noise prediction MSE. - **Classifier-Free Guidance formula:** $\tilde{\epsilon}_\theta = (1+w)\epsilon_\theta(x_t, c) - w\,\epsilon_\theta(x_t, \emptyset)$ — controls prompt adherence vs. diversity. - **CLIP similarity** uses cosine similarity in a shared image-text embedding space and is the backbone of text-to-image alignment. - **FID score** measures the Fréchet distance between real and generated feature distributions; lower is better. - **Latent diffusion models** (Stable Diffusion) operate in a compressed VAE latent space, dramatically reducing compute vs. pixel-space diffusion. - **Major tools to know:** Stable Diffusion, DALL·E 2/3, Midjourney, Imagen, MusicGen, Jukebox, Runway Gen-3, Sora. - **Human-in-the-loop workflow:** prompt → generate → curate → refine (inpaint/img2img) → post-process → deliver. - **Ethical pillars:** training data consent, style mimicry, copyright status, labor impact, deepfakes, bias. - **Mitigations:** Glaze, C2PA provenance, SynthID watermarking, licensed datasets (Adobe Firefly, Shutterstock), EU AI Act 2024 disclosure rules. - **Key engineering trade-off:** Sample quality vs. inference speed vs. controllability — and how CFG weight $w$ mediates this.

topics.title: Text Generation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT867 - GENERATIVE AI
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Text Generation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Text Generation** is a sub-field of **Natural Language Generation (NLG)** within Generative AI that focuses on the automatic production of coherent, contextually relevant, and grammatically valid natural language text from a machine-learning model. Given a context (prompt, preceding tokens, image, or structured data), a generative language model estimates a probability distribution over the vocabulary and sequentially samples or selects tokens to construct a complete textual output.

Mathematically, an **autoregressive language model** factorizes the joint probability of a token sequence $W = (w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n)$ using the **chain rule of probability**:

$$P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_1, w_2, \dots, w_{i-1})$$

The model generates $w_i$ conditioned on all previously generated tokens, hence the term **autoregressive**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Text Generation is examined under Module 4 of PERAT867 (Generative AI). Students must master the distinction between *deterministic decoding* (Greedy, Beam Search) and *stochastic decoding* (Sampling, Top-k, Top-p, Temperature), as this is a high-yield ESE question area.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of text generation like **a guest speaker completing sentences for someone telling a story**:

- Imagine you are listening to a friend narrate a story. At every pause, you mentally **rank the next likely words** ("...and the prince rode his ___"). Some words are obvious (*horse*), some are plausible (*steed, pony*), and some are absurd (*banana*).
- A **language model** does the same thing, but with millions of internal parameters learned from massive text corpora. It assigns a **probability score** to every word in its vocabulary (often 30,000–100,000 tokens) and decides which word to say next based on a **decoding strategy**.
- The difference between a *boring* chatbot and a *creative* one lies almost entirely in **how it converts those probability scores into the final word** — that process is called **decoding**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics in Text Generation

The following bolded metrics are universally referenced in NLG research and KTU-aligned coursework:

- **Perplexity (PPL):** Measures how "surprised" the model is by real text. Lower is better.
- **BLEU Score:** Precision-based metric for machine translation (n-gram overlap).
- **ROUGE Score:** Recall-based metric for summarization.
- **METEOR:** Considers synonyms and word order for translation evaluation.
- **Distinct-n:** Measures diversity of generated text (ratio of unique n-grams).

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Takeaway:** Text Generation is *not* a single algorithm — it is a **pipeline** of (1) a trained language model, (2) a tokenization scheme, (3) a decoding strategy, and (4) an evaluation framework. Mastering each component is essential for KTU Part B answers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Text Generation Pipeline

The end-to-end NLG pipeline is decomposed into the following sequential stages:

1. **Input Encoding** — The prompt $X = (x_1, \dots, x_m)$ is tokenized into subword units (e.g., BPE, WordPiece) and converted into dense embedding vectors.
2. **Contextual Representation** — A Transformer-based encoder/decoder produces contextual hidden states $h_i$ for each token.
3. **Probability Computation via Softmax** — The final hidden state $h_n$ is projected onto the vocabulary space via a linear layer, producing **logits** $z \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert V \vert}$, which are converted to probabilities through softmax:

$$P(w_i \mid w_{<i}) = \frac{\exp(z_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{\vert V \vert} \exp(z_j)}$$

4. **Decoding Strategy Selection** — One of the strategies in §2.3 is applied to select the next token.
5. **Termination Check** — Generation stops at a maximum length, an end-of-sequence (EOS) token, or via a repetition penalty.

## 2.2 Autoregressive Generation: Why "Auto-Regressive"?

The term **autoregressive** originates from time-series statistics — the model regresses on its own previous outputs. This creates two critical implications:

- **Sequential Bottleneck:** Token $w_i$ cannot be generated until $w_{i-1}$ is finalized, making parallel generation impossible during inference (this is the motivation behind speculative decoding and Medusa-style decoding).
- **Exposure Bias:** A train-test mismatch arises because the model is trained on *ground-truth* previous tokens but at inference sees its *own* predictions (potentially erroneous ones).

## 2.3 Decoding Strategies — The Core of KTU High-Yield Content

### A. Greedy Decoding
At each step, select the token with the highest probability:

$$w_t = \arg\max_{w \in V} P(w \mid w_{<t})$$

- **Pros:** Fast, deterministic, easy to implement.
- **Cons:** Prone to repetition loops, myopic (cannot recover from early errors), often produces generic text.

### B. Beam Search
Maintains the top-$k$ (beam width) most probable hypotheses at each step. The final hypothesis with the highest cumulative log-probability is selected:

$$\text{Score}(Y) = \frac{1}{L^{\alpha}} \sum_{t=1}^{L} \log P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, X)$$

where $\alpha$ is the **length normalization** parameter.

- **Pros:** Better than greedy for translation/summarization.
- **Cons:** Still deterministic; poor for open-ended creative generation.

### C. Top-k Sampling
Restrict the sampling pool to the top $k$ highest-probability tokens, then sample from their re-normalized distribution. Used in GPT-2/3.

### D. Top-p (Nucleus) Sampling
Select the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability mass $\geq p$ (e.g., $p = 0.9$). Sample from this dynamic pool. This adapts to the **confidence** of the model.

### E. Temperature Scaling
A hyperparameter $T$ that sharpens or flattens the probability distribution before sampling:

$$P_T(w_i) = \frac{\exp(z_i / T)}{\sum_{j=1}^{\vert V \vert} \exp(z_j / T)}$$

- $T < 1$ → sharper (more deterministic).
- $T > 1$ → flatter (more random/creative).
- $T = 1$ → original distribution.

### F. Repetition Penalty
Multiplies the logits of previously generated tokens by a factor $\theta < 1$ to discourage verbatim repetition.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | Autoregressive Decomposition | $P(W) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_{<i})$ | Chain rule; $w_{<i} = (w_1,\dots,w_{i-1})$ |
| 2 | Standard Softmax | $P(w_i) = \frac{\exp(z_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{\mid V \mid} \exp(z_j)}$ | $\mid V \mid$ is vocabulary size |
| 3 | Temperature-Scaled Softmax | $P_T(w_i) = \frac{\exp(z_i / T)}{\sum_{j} \exp(z_j / T)}$ | $T > 0$; $T \to 0$ is greedy |
| 4 | Beam Search Score | $\text{Score}(Y) = \frac{1}{L^{\alpha}} \sum_{t=1}^{L} \log P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, X)$ | $\alpha \in [0,1]$ normalizes length |
| 5 | Top-k Pool Construction | $V^{(k)} = \text{top-}k\{w \in V : P(w \mid w_{<t})\}$ | $k$ is a fixed integer |
| 6 | Top-p Pool Construction | $V^{(p)} = \min\{S \subseteq V : \sum_{w \in S} P(w) \geq p\}$ | $p \in (0,1]$ |
| 7 | Perplexity | $\text{PPL}(W) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\log P(w_i \mid w_{<i})\right)$ | Lower = better LM |
| 8 | BLEU (n-gram precision) | $\text{BLEU} = \text{BP} \cdot \exp\!\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)$ | $\text{BP}$ = brevity penalty |
| 9 | Cross-Entropy Loss | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(w_i^{\text{true}} \mid w_{<i})$ | Used to train language models |
| 10 | Repetition Penalty | $z_i' = z_i / \theta$ if $w_i \in w_{<t}$, else $z_i$ | $\theta \in (0,1]$ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Domain | Typical Strategy |
|-------------|--------|------------------|
| Machine Translation | Google Translate, DeepL | Beam Search |
| News Summarization | News aggregators | Beam Search + Length Penalty |
| Conversational Chatbots | Customer service | Top-p + Temperature |
| Creative Writing / Story Gen | Marketing, gaming | High Temperature + Top-k |
| Code Generation | GitHub Copilot | Low Temperature (deterministic) |
| Scientific Abstract Generation | Bio-medical NLP | Beam Search + ROUGE optimization |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The decoding strategy is *not* universal — selecting the wrong strategy is the single most common cause of production-level LLM failures (e.g., a translation system producing hallucinated content because someone applied $T=1.2$ instead of beam search).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Autoregressive Probability Factorization

**Goal:** Show how the joint probability of a sentence is decomposed into a product of conditional probabilities.

**Step 1 — Start with the joint probability of $n$ tokens:**

$$P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the definition of conditional probability** $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A)$:

$$P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n) = P(w_1) \cdot P(w_2 \mid w_1)$$

**Step 3 — Extend to three tokens:**

$$P(w_1, w_2, w_3) = P(w_1) \cdot P(w_2 \mid w_1) \cdot P(w_3 \mid w_1, w_2)$$

**Step 4 — Generalize by induction to $n$ tokens (Chain Rule):**

$$P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_{<i})$$

This is the **fundamental equation of autoregressive text generation**. Every token's prediction is conditioned on all prior tokens.

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Temperature-Scaled Softmax

**Goal:** Demonstrate how the hyperparameter $T$ reshapes the output distribution.

**Step 1 — Start with raw logits** $z = (z_1, z_2, \dots, z_{\vert V \vert})$ output by the final linear layer.

**Step 2 — Divide each logit by $T$** to obtain scaled logits:

$$z_i^{(T)} = \frac{z_i}{T}$$

**Step 3 — Apply softmax to the scaled logits:**

$$P_T(w_i) = \frac{\exp(z_i / T)}{\sum_{j=1}^{\vert V \vert} \exp(z_j / T)}$$

**Step 4 — Behavior analysis of $T$:**

- If $T \to 0$: the exponential term with the largest $z_i$ dominates, and the distribution collapses to a one-hot vector → equivalent to **greedy decoding**.
- If $T = 1$: the distribution is unchanged.
- If $T \to \infty$: all exponentials approach 1, and $P_T(w_i) \to 1/\vert V \vert$ → **uniform random**.

**Step 5 — Worked numerical example:**

Suppose $\vert V \vert = 3$, $z = (2.0,\ 1.0,\ 0.5)$.

For $T = 1$:

$$P(w_1) = \frac{e^2}{e^2 + e^1 + e^{0.5}} = \frac{7.389}{7.389 + 2.718 + 1.649} = \frac{7.389}{11.756} \approx 0.629$$

$$P(w_2) = \frac{2.718}{11.756} \approx 0.231$$

$$P(w_3) = \frac{1.649}{11.756} \approx 0.140$$

For $T = 0.5$ (sharper), scaled logits become $(4.0,\ 2.0,\ 1.0)$:

$$P_T(w_1) = \frac{e^4}{e^4 + e^2 + e^1} = \frac{54.598}{54.598 + 7.389 + 2.718} \approx 0.844$$

The probability mass concentrates on the top token — confirming $T < 1$ makes generation more deterministic.

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation: Perplexity

**Goal:** Derive perplexity from the cross-entropy loss.

**Step 1 — Cross-entropy of a sequence:**

$$H(W) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log_2 P(w_i \mid w_{<i})$$

**Step 2 — Perplexity is defined as** $2^{H(W)}$:

$$\text{PPL}(W) = 2^{H(W)} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(w_i \mid w_{<i})\right)$$

(Converting $\log_2$ to $\ln$ and base-2 to base-$e$ exponentials yields the right-hand form.)

**Step 3 — Worked example:**

Let $N = 3$ and per-token log-probabilities be $\log P = (-0.5,\ -2.0,\ -1.0)$ in natural log.

$$\text{PPL} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{-0.5 - 2.0 - 1.0}{3}\right) = \exp(1.167) \approx 3.21$$

This means the model is "as confused as if it had to choose uniformly among $\approx 3.21$ words" at each step.

## 3.4 Detailed Derivation: BLEU Score (Simplified)

**Goal:** Demonstrate BLEU's geometric-mean n-gram precision with brevity penalty.

**Step 1 — Compute modified n-gram precision** $p_n$ (clipped count of n-grams in candidate that appear in reference).

**Step 2 — Geometric mean across n-gram orders** $n = 1, 2, 3, 4$:

$$P_{\text{geom}} = \exp\!\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)$$

with uniform weights $w_n = 1/N$.

**Step 3 — Apply brevity penalty** $\text{BP}$:

$$\text{BP} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } c > r \\ \exp(1 - r/c) & \text{if } c \leq r \end{cases}$$

where $c$ = candidate length, $r$ = closest reference length.

**Step 4 — Final BLEU:**

$$\text{BLEU} = \text{BP} \cdot \exp\!\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)$$

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Decoding Strategies

| Strategy | Determinism | Diversity | Best Use Case | Failure Mode |
|----------|-------------|-----------|---------------|--------------|
| Greedy | **High** | Low | Short factual answers | Repetition loops |
| Beam Search | **High** | Low | Translation, summarization | Hallucination on long outputs |
| Top-k Sampling | Medium | Medium-High | Creative writing | May include implausible tokens if k is large |
| Top-p (Nucleus) | Medium | Adaptive | Open-ended chat | Unstable at low $p$ values |
| Temperature Sampling | Tunable | Tunable | Fine-grained control | $T \to \infty$ is pure noise |
| Contrastive Search | Medium | High | Coherent long-form | Computationally expensive |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Text Generation Pipeline (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Prompt X] --> B[Tokenizer BPE or WordPiece]
    B --> C[Embedding Layer E]
    C --> D[Transformer Encoder Decoder Backbone]
    D --> E[Hidden States h1 to hN]
    E --> F[Linear Projection to Vocab]
    F --> G[Logits Vector z]
    G --> H{Decoding Strategy Selector}
    H --> I1[Greedy argmax]
    H --> I2[Beam Search k beams]
    H --> I3[Top-k Sampling]
    H --> I4[Top-p Nucleus Sampling]
    H --> I5[Temperature Scaling]
    I1 --> J[Next Token wt]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
    I4 --> J
    I5 --> J
    J --> K{End of Sequence Check}
    K -->|No| D
    K -->|Yes| L[Generated Text Y]
    L --> M[Evaluation BLEU or ROUGE or PPL]
```

## 4.2 Autoregressive Token-by-Token Generation Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Prompt Tokens w1 to wm] --> B[Forward Pass through LM]
    B --> C[Sample or Select wm+1]
    C --> D[Append wm+1 to Sequence]
    D --> E{Sequence Complete?}
    E -->|No| B
    E -->|Yes| F[Output Final Sequence]
```

## 4.3 Beam Search Hypothesis Expansion (Beam Width = 3)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start: BOS] --> S1a[Token A prob 0.4]
    S0 --> S1b[Token B prob 0.3]
    S0 --> S1c[Token C prob 0.2]
    S1a --> S2a[AA prob 0.16]
    S1a --> S2b[AB prob 0.12]
    S1a --> S2c[AD prob 0.10]
    S1b --> S2d[BA prob 0.09]
    S1b --> S2e[BE prob 0.07]
    S1c --> S2f[CF prob 0.05]
    S2a --> KEEP[Keep top 3 by score]
    S2b --> KEEP
    S2c --> KEEP
    S2d --> DROP1[Drop]
    S2e --> DROP1
    S2f --> DROP1
```

## 4.4 Comparison: Deterministic vs Stochastic Decoding Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Softmax Probability Vector] --> B{Decoding Mode}
    B -->|Deterministic| C[argmax over V]
    B -->|Stochastic| D[Sample from P]
    C --> E[Greedy or Beam Search]
    D --> F[Top-k or Top-p or Temperature]
    E --> G[High Coherence Low Diversity]
    F --> H[High Diversity Tunable Coherence]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for Exams:** When KTU asks to "explain the text generation pipeline," reproduce a diagram similar to §4.1 with the **five distinct stages** (Tokenization → Encoding → Probability Computation → Decoding → Termination). Each stage must be labeled with its mathematical operation to earn full marks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap
